diseases.symptoms_early: Sudden appearance of petechiae (pinpoint red-purple spots) and purpura (bruising) in a healthy child, often without any history of trauma.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Multiple bruises across the extremities and trunk, bleeding from the gums, and recurrent epistaxis (nosebleeds) that are difficult to stop.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Internal bleeding, marked by hematuria (blood in urine), melena or hematochezia (blood in stool), and intracranial hemorrhage (rare, occurring in <1% of cases, marked by severe headache, vomiting, or altered consciousness).
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Complete blood count showing isolated thrombocytopenia (platelet count <100,000/µL, often <20,000/µL) with normal white and red blood cell counts. Bone marrow biopsy is performed if atypical features are present.
diseases.treatment_plan: Most cases are acute and self-limiting; a "watch and wait" approach is appropriate if bleeding is minimal. For active bleeding or extremely low platelets, administer intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) or systemic corticosteroids.
diseases.home_care: Avoid contact sports and rough play to prevent trauma. Do not administer aspirin, ibuprofen, or other anti-platelet medications. Monitor for any signs of head injury.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Intravenous Immunoglobulin (IVIG - generic active ingredient to block splenic clearing of platelets); Methylprednisolone or Prednisone (generic systemic steroid active ingredients to suppress antibody production).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Seek immediate emergency evaluation if the child developments a severe headache, persistent vomiting, blood in the urine or stool, or an uncontrollable nosebleed.
diseases.prevention: No preventative measures exist, as the condition represents a transient post-viral autoimmune response.
diseases.vaccination_info: Routine childhood immunizations should be maintained; avoid live vaccines during high-dose steroid therapy.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent. Up to 80% of children recover spontaneously within 6 months without long-term complications or a recurrence.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Spontaneous resolution typically occurs within 2 to 8 weeks; chronic ITP persists beyond 12 months in a minority of cases.
diseases.complications: Severe bleeding (menorrhagia in adolescent girls, gastrointestinal hemorrhage), and intracranial hemorrhage.

diseases.name: Hemophilia A
diseases.affected_age_group: Infants and Toddlers (Often detected during circumcision, or when the child begins crawling/walking)
diseases.causes: A mutation or deletion in the F8 gene on the X chromosome, leading to a deficiency of functional Coagulation Factor VIII.
diseases.pathophysiology: Factor VIII is a cofactor in the intrinsic pathway of the coagulation cascade. A deficiency of Factor VIII impairs the activation of Factor X, preventing the conversion of prothrombin to thrombin. This results in an inability to form a stable fibrin clot, leading to prolonged bleeding.
diseases.symptoms_early: Prolonged bleeding following circumcision, heel sticks, or minor cuts. Easy bruising on the trunk and limbs when the child begins to crawl.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Hemarthrosis (bleeding into joints, commonly knees, elbows, and ankles), causing sudden joint pain, swelling, warmth, and reluctance to move the limb.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Intramuscular hemorrhages causing compartment syndrome, hematuria, gastrointestinal bleeding, and intracranial hemorrhage following minor head trauma.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Prolonged Activated Partial Thromboplastin Time (aPTT) with a normal Prothrombin Time (PT) and platelet count. Diagnosis is confirmed by a specific Factor VIII activity assay.
diseases.treatment_plan: Prevent and treat bleeding episodes by replacing the deficient Factor VIII. Administer prophylactic infusions of recombinant Factor VIII. Desmopressin (DDAVP) can be used for mild cases.
diseases.home_care: Ensure a safe home environment to minimize trauma (padded furniture corners, protective helmet when learning to walk). Avoid aspirin and NSAIDs. Administer factor replacement at home under clinical guidance.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Recombinant Coagulation Factor VIII (active replacement ingredient); Desmopressin acetate (DDAVP - generic active ingredient to stimulate release of stored Factor VIII in mild cases).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Any head injury, neck injury, or signs of joint bleeding require immediate medical evaluation and factor replacement therapy.
diseases.prevention: Avoid trauma. Genetic counseling for families with a known history of hemophilia; prenatal diagnosis is available.
diseases.vaccination_info: Administer vaccines subcutaneously rather than intramuscularly, and apply firm pressure for at least 5 minutes to prevent hematoma.
diseases.prognosis: Good with modern prophylactic factor replacement; children can expect a normal lifespan and active lifestyle, though joint damage remains a risk.
diseases.recovery_timeline: This is a lifelong genetic coagulation disorder; acute bleeding episodes resolve within days of factor replacement.
diseases.complications: Chronic hemophilic arthropathy (joint destruction from repeated bleeds), development of inhibitory antibodies against infused Factor VIII, and blood-borne infections.

diseases.name: Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus (Pediatric)
diseases.affected_age_group: Peak presentation windows occur between 4 to 7 years and 10 to 14 years.
diseases.causes: Autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta cells triggered by environmental exposures (such as viral infections) in children with a genetic susceptibility linked to HLA-DR3/4 complex markers.
diseases.pathophysiology: A T-cell mediated autoimmune attack leads to the progressive destruction of insulin-producing beta cells in the islets of Langerhans. Clinical symptoms appear once roughly 80–90% of beta cells are destroyed. The resulting absolute insulin deficiency prevents glucose from entering skeletal muscle and adipose tissues, causing hyperglycemia and shifting metabolism toward fat breakdown and ketone production.
diseases.symptoms_early: Polyuria (frequent urination, including a return of bedwetting), polydipsia (increased, unquenchable thirst), and polyphagia (increased appetite).
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Unexplained rapid weight loss despite an increased appetite, persistent fatigue, muscle weakness, blurred vision, and recurrent skin or monilial diaper infections.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA): presentation includes deep, rapid breathing (Kussmaul respirations), a fruity breath odor from acetone, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, altered mental status, and progressive obtundation.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Random plasma glucose $ge 200	ext{ mg/dL}$ with classic symptoms, a fasting plasma glucose $ge 126	ext{ mg/dL}$, or an elevated Hemoglobin A1C ($ge 6.5%$). Elevated serum or urine ketones confirm ketoacidosis.
diseases.treatment_plan: Lifelong, intensive insulin replacement therapy using basal-bolus regimens or an insulin pump. Continuous monitoring of blood glucose levels and careful management of carbohydrate intake are required.
diseases.home_care: Regular checking of blood glucose levels throughout the day. Ensure access to rapid-acting carbohydrates for treating low blood sugar (hypoglycemia) and test for urine ketones during illness.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Insulin Lispro or Aspart (rapid-acting generic analogues for mealtime coverage); Insulin Glargine or Detemir (long-acting generic basal insulin formulations).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Any child showing signs of the classic "3 Ps" (polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss) requires immediate evaluation. Go to the emergency room if vomiting, abdominal pain, or heavy breathing develops.
diseases.prevention: No effective preventative measures exist to stop the autoimmune process once triggered.
diseases.vaccination_info: Annual influenza vaccination and up-to-date routine childhood immunizations are critical, as infections can complicate glycemic control and increase the risk of DKA.
diseases.prognosis: Good with consistent, intensive glycemic management. Long-term outcomes depend heavily on maintaining blood glucose levels near target ranges to reduce vascular complications.
diseases.recovery_timeline: This is a lifelong chronic metabolic condition requiring continuous daily management; there is no permanent cure.
diseases.complications: Diabetic ketoacidosis, severe hypoglycemia, and long-term microvascular and macrovascular complications including retinopathy, nephropathy, and neuropathy.

diseases.name: Congenital Hypothyroidism
diseases.affected_age_group: Neonates (Screened at birth; symptoms develop in the first few weeks if untreated)
diseases.causes: Thyroid dysgenesis (absence or underdevelopment of the thyroid gland) in 85% of cases; thyroid dyshormonogenesis (inborn errors of thyroid hormone synthesis) in 15%.
diseases.pathophysiology: The absence or failure of the thyroid gland to produce adequate thyroxine (T4). Thyroxine is essential for early brain development and skeletal growth. Deficiencies lead to irreversible intellectual disability and growth failure.
diseases.symptoms_early: Term infants are often asymptomatic due to maternal thyroid hormone crossing the placenta. A prolonged physiological jaundice is the earliest indicator.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Poor feeding, lethargy (sleeping too much), constipation, hypothermia, cold extremities, and a hoarse or weak cry.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Classic cretinism: coarse facial features, large protruding tongue (macroglossia), umbilical hernia, dry skin, wide cranial sutures, and severe developmental delay.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Newborn screening showing an elevated Thyroid Stimulating Hormone (TSH) and low free thyroxine (T4) from a heel stick blood spot. Confirmed with serum thyroid labs.
diseases.treatment_plan: Initiate daily oral replacement of Levothyroxine immediately. Dosing must begin within the first 2 weeks of life to prevent developmental delay. Monitor TSH and free T4 levels closely to adjust dosage as the child grows.
diseases.home_care: Administer the tablet crushed in a small amount of breast milk or water at the same time every day. Never mix it with soy formula or iron/calcium supplements, which impair absorption.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Levothyroxine sodium (generic active thyroid hormone replacement ingredient).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Consult a pediatrician if newborn screening results are delayed, or if a young infant develops persistent jaundice, constipation, or sleeps excessively.
diseases.prevention: Ensure adequate maternal iodine intake during pregnancy; no direct prevention exists for dysgenesis.
diseases.vaccination_info: No specific immunizations are associated with this endocrine disorder; follow standard schedules.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent if treatment is started within the first 2 weeks of life. Untreated infants develop severe, irreversible intellectual disabilities.
diseases.recovery_timeline: This is a lifelong genetic or developmental endocrine disorder requiring continuous daily hormone replacement.
diseases.complications: Intellectual disability, short stature, delayed bone age, cardiac enlargement, and motor coordination problems.

diseases.name: Phenylketonuria (PKU)
diseases.classification: Inborn Error of Amino Acid Metabolism
diseases.affected_age_group: Neonates (Screened at birth; symptoms develop over the first year if untreated)
diseases.causes: An autosomal recessive mutation in the PAH gene on chromosome 12, leading to a deficiency of the hepatic enzyme phenylalanine hydroxylase.
diseases.pathophysiology: The enzyme deficiency prevents the conversion of the essential amino acid phenylalanine to tyrosine. Phenylalanine accumulates in the blood and tissues, crossing the blood-brain barrier. High brain concentrations are neurotoxic, impairing myelination and neurotransmitter synthesis.
diseases.symptoms_early: Asymptomatic at birth. Within the first few months, infants may exhibit vomiting, irritability, and a characteristic "musty" or "mousy" odor to the urine and skin.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Eczema-like skin rashes, fair skin and blue eyes (due to impaired melanin synthesis from tyrosine deficiency), and microcephaly.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Untreated children show severe intellectual disability, autistic-like behaviors, hyperactivity, tremors, and seizures.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Newborn screening demonstrating elevated blood phenylalanine levels ($ge 2	ext{ mg/dL}$). Diagnosis is confirmed by measuring blood phenylalanine-to-tyrosine ratios.
diseases.treatment_plan: Initiate a lifetime dietary restriction of phenylalanine. This requires avoiding high-protein foods and using a specialized phenylalanine-free amino acid medical formula. Maintain blood levels within a target therapeutic range.
diseases.home_care: Maintain a strict low-phenylalanine diet. Weigh foods carefully, monitor intake using dietary logs, and ensure regular blood spot testing at home.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Sapropterin dihydrochloride (generic active cofactor used to enhance remaining enzyme activity in responsive patients); Phenylalanine-free amino acid formula.
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Immediate consultation is required if newborn screening indicates elevated phenylalanine levels, or if an infant develops a musty odor or developmental delay.
diseases.prevention: Pre-conception genetic screening for carrier parents. Strict dietary control for mothers with PKU during pregnancy (maternal PKU) to prevent fetal brain damage.
diseases.vaccination_info: No specific immunizations are associated with this metabolic disorder.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent if a strict diet is initiated in the first weeks of life. Late initiation leads to irreversible intellectual impairment.
diseases.recovery_timeline: This is a lifelong genetic metabolic disorder requiring continuous daily dietary management.
diseases.complications: Severe intellectual disability, microcephaly, developmental delay, and neurological problems (seizures, tremors).
diseases.overview: Inborn Error of Amino Acid Metabolism

diseases.name: Classic Galactosemia
diseases.affected_age_group: Neonates (Symptoms present within days of initiating milk feeding)
diseases.causes: An autosomal recessive mutation in the GALT gene on chromosome 9, leading to a deficiency of the enzyme galactose-1-phosphate uridylyltransferase.
diseases.pathophysiology: The enzyme deficiency prevents the conversion of galactose (derived from lactose in milk) to glucose. Galactose and its toxic metabolite, galactose-1-phosphate, accumulate in tissues, causing cell damage in the liver, kidneys, brain, and ocular lens.
diseases.symptoms_early: Feeding difficulties, vomiting, diarrhea, and progressive lethargy within days of consuming breast milk or cow's milk formula.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Neonatal jaundice, hepatomegaly (enlarged liver), bleeding tendencies (due to liver damage), and poor weight gain.
diseases.symptoms_severe: E. coli sepsis (due to impaired leukocyte function from galactose accumulation), cataracts, renal tubular dysfunction (Fanconi syndrome), liver failure, and death if milk feeding continues.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Newborn screening showing elevated total blood galactose and reduced GALT enzyme activity. Confirmed with genetic testing.
diseases.treatment_plan: Immediately and permanently exclude all lactose and galactose from the diet. Switch to soy-based formula. Maintain a strict lactose-free diet throughout life.
diseases.home_care: Strictly avoid breast milk, cow's milk formulas, and dairy products. Check ingredient lists on processed foods and medications for hidden lactose.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Calcium carbonate and Cholecalciferol (Vitamin D3) supplements to support bone health due to the restriction of dairy products.
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Any newborn presenting with vomiting, jaundice, lethargy, or failure to feed after starting milk requires urgent evaluation.
diseases.prevention: Prenatal genetic testing for carrier parents; immediately stop breast/milk feeding in newborns suspected of metabolic disorders.
diseases.vaccination_info: No specific immunizations are associated with this carbohydrate metabolic disorder.
diseases.prognosis: Good if milk is stopped immediately. Despite dietary compliance, children remain at risk for long-term complications like learning disabilities, speech delays, and ovarian failure in girls.
diseases.recovery_timeline: This is a lifelong genetic metabolic disorder requiring continuous daily dietary exclusion.
diseases.complications: Neonatal E. coli sepsis, cirrhosis, liver failure, cataracts, developmental delay, speech disorders, and premature ovarian insufficiency.

diseases.name: Minimal Change Disease (Pediatric Nephrotic Syndrome)
diseases.affected_age_group: 2 to 7 Years (Accounts for over 85% of childhood nephrotic cases)
diseases.causes: Idiopathic etiology; believed to involve T-cell dysregulation that leads to the production of a circulating permeability factor, which damages the glomerular capillary wall.
diseases.pathophysiology: The unclassified circulating cytokine causes the effacement (flattening) of podocyte foot processes in the glomerulus. This disrupts the negative charge barrier of the glomerular basement membrane, allowing large amounts of serum albumin to leak into the urine. The resulting heavy proteinuria leads to hypoalbuminemia, which drops plasma oncotic pressure and causes fluid to shift into the interstitial spaces, driving widespread edema.
diseases.symptoms_early: Periorbital edema (puffiness around the eyes), often noticed first in the morning and mistaken for allergic reactions.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Pitting dependent edema spreading to the ankles, feet, and labia or scrotum, along with a noticeable reduction in urine output and frothy or foamy urine.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Generalized fluid overload (anasarca), significant ascites causing abdominal pain and respiratory discomfort, respiratory distress from pleural effusions, and signs of intravascular volume depletion.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Urinalysis showing heavy proteinuria (3+ or 4+), a 24-hour urine protein collection exceeding 40 mg/m²/hour, serum labs revealing hypoalbuminemia (<2.5 g/dL), and hyperlipidemia.
diseases.treatment_plan: Induce clinical remission using a standardized course of systemic corticosteroids. Manage fluid balance carefully with dietary sodium restrictions and targeted diuretic therapy if fluid overload compromises breathing.
diseases.home_care: Monitor daily weights using the same scale first thing in the morning. Use urine dipsticks at home to track protein levels and monitor for early signs of a relapse.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Prednisone (systemic corticosteroid active ingredient used to restore podocyte integrity); Furosemide (loop diuretic active ingredient used to manage severe edema under clinical supervision).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Seek prompt evaluation if a child develops swelling around the eyes or extremities, has a significant decrease in urine output, or shows signs of an active infection while taking steroids.
diseases.prevention: No preventative methods exist, as the underlying immunological trigger remains unclassified.
diseases.vaccination_info: Avoid live viral vaccines (such as MMR and Varicella) while the child is on high-dose immunosuppressive corticosteroid therapy.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent response rate; over 90% of children achieve complete remission with steroid therapy, though a significant portion may experience subsequent relapses during childhood.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Proteinuria typically resolves within 7 to 14 days of starting steroids; the full initial course of steroid therapy spans 8 to 12 weeks.
diseases.complications: Severe bacterial infections (such as spontaneous bacterial peritonitis from Streptococcus pneumoniae due to immunoglobulin loss), thromboembolism, and hypervolemic shock.

diseases.name: Acute Post-Streptococcal Glomerulonephritis (APSGN)
diseases.affected_age_group: 5 to 12 Years (Rare under 3 years)
diseases.causes: An immune-mediated reaction following pharyngeal or skin infection (impetigo) with nephritogenic strains of Group A Beta-Hemolytic Streptococcus.
diseases.pathophysiology: Streptococcal antigens bind to glomerular structures, prompting the formation of antigen-antibody immune complexes. These complexes deposit in the subepithelial space of the glomerular basement membrane, activating the complement cascade. This recruits neutrophils, causing glomerular inflammation, a drop in glomerular filtration rate (GFR), and fluid retention.
diseases.symptoms_early: A history of a sore throat 1 to 2 weeks prior, or skin sores 3 to 6 weeks prior, followed by the appearance of mild periorbital edema.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Gross hematuria: urine appears tea-colored, smoky, or cola-colored. Mild to moderate oliguria (reduced urine output) and mild hypertension.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Severe volume overload leading to acute hypertensive encephalopathy (marked by headache, seizures, or confusion) and pulmonary edema.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Urinalysis showing dysmorphic red blood cells, RBC casts, and mild-to-moderate proteinuria. Serum complement level C3 is characteristically low. Elevated anti-streptolysin O (ASO) titer.
diseases.treatment_plan: Supportive management focusing on fluid restriction, sodium-restricted diet, and management of hypertension. Eradicate any remaining streptococcal infection with antibiotics.
diseases.home_care: Monitor and restrict daily fluid intake as advised. Weigh the child daily to monitor fluid retention. Avoid adding sodium to meals.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Furosemide (generic loop diuretic active ingredient to manage volume overload and hypertension); Nifedipine or Hydralazine (generic active anti-hypertensive agents for severe blood pressure spikes); Penicillin V (to eradicate remaining GAS bacteria).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Seek prompt care if a child develops tea-colored urine, facial puffiness, a significant decrease in urine output, or a severe headache weeks after a sore throat or skin infection.
diseases.prevention: Prompt and complete antibiotic treatment of all Group A Streptococcal infections.
diseases.vaccination_info: No vaccine is available targeting Group A Streptococcus.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent. Over 95% of children achieve complete recovery with normal renal function restored. Long-term renal damage is extremely rare in pediatric patients.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Oliguria and edema resolve within 1 to 2 weeks; hematuria may take several weeks, and microscopic hematuria can persist for up to a year.
diseases.complications: Acute renal failure, hypertensive crisis, hypertensive encephalopathy, and congestive heart failure.

diseases.name: Urinary Tract Infection (UTI)
diseases.classification: Bacterial Colonization of the Urinary Tract
diseases.affected_age_group: Infants and toddlers (Uncircumcised boys under 1 year, and girls under 4 years)
diseases.causes: Escherichia coli (causes >80% of cases); Klebsiella, Proteus mirabilis, and Enterococcus are secondary bacterial causes.
diseases.pathophysiology: Uropathogenic fecal bacteria ascend from the perineum into the urethra and bladder (cystitis). In some cases, the bacteria continue ascending into the ureters and renal parenchyma (pyelonephritis), triggering localized inflammation, tissue damage, and potential renal scarring.
diseases.symptoms_early: In infants: vague, non-specific symptoms including unexplained fever, irritability, poor feeding, vomiting, and failure to thrive.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: In toilet-trained children: dysuria (pain or burning during urination), frequency, urgency, suprapubic pain, and new-onset daytime or nighttime wetting.
diseases.symptoms_severe: High fever, chills, flank or back pain, vomiting, dehydration, and lethargy, indicating acute pyelonephritis and potential urosepsis.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Urinalysis showing leukocyte esterase, nitrites, and pyuria. Diagnosis is confirmed by a urine culture obtained via catheterization in non-toilet-trained children, or mid-stream clean catch in older children ($ge 50,000	ext{ CFU/mL}$ for catheter, $ge 100,000	ext{ CFU/mL}$ for clean catch).
diseases.treatment_plan: Initiate appropriate oral or intravenous antibiotics targeting enteric Gram-negative bacilli. Perform a renal ultrasound for all infants with their first febrile UTI to screen for structural abnormalities.
diseases.home_care: Encourage frequent fluid intake to help flush out the urinary tract. Ensure proper wiping techniques in girls (front to back). Do not administer bubble baths.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Cephalexin or Cefdinir (generic oral cephalosporin active ingredients used for uncomplicated UTIs); Amoxicillin-Clavulanate (second-line generic antibiotic option).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Seek prompt care for any unexplained fever in an infant, or if an older child complains of pain when urinating or develops back pain and vomiting.
diseases.prevention: Avoid bubble baths and harsh soaps. Encourage regular urination and treat constipation, which is a major driver of urinary stasis.
diseases.vaccination_info: No specific immunizations exist targeting uropathogenic Escherichia coli.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent with early antibiotic therapy. Delayed treatment of pyelonephritis increases the risk of permanent renal scarring and future hypertension.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Fever and dysuria typically improve within 24 to 48 hours of starting antibiotics; the total treatment course ranges from 7 to 10 days.
diseases.complications: Renal abscess, renal scarring, hypertension, chronic kidney disease, and urosepsis.
diseases.overview: Bacterial Colonization of the Urinary Tract

diseases.name: Vesicoureteral Reflux (VUR)
diseases.affected_age_group: Infants and Toddlers (Typically diagnosed following a febrile UTI; more common in girls)
diseases.causes: Primary VUR is congenital, caused by an abnormally short submucosal segment of the ureter as it enters the bladder wall. Secondary VUR is caused by high intravesical pressures (e.g., neurogenic bladder).
diseases.pathophysiology: The short, malformed ureterovesical junction fails to act as a one-way valve during bladder contraction. During urination, urine is forced backward (retrograde) from the bladder up into the ureters and kidneys, carrying bacteria and increasing the risk of pyelonephritis and renal scarring.
diseases.symptoms_early: Often asymptomatic until the child develops a urinary tract infection. Recurrent UTIs are the primary clinical indicator.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Recurrent febrile UTIs, accompanied by bedwetting, daytime urgency, and constipation.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Bilateral severe reflux leading to progressive renal scarring (reflux nephropathy), growth failure, protein in the urine, and chronic kidney disease.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Voiding Cystourethrogram (VCUG) is the gold standard, demonstrating retrograde flow of contrast into the ureters and grading the reflux from Grade I (mild) to Grade V (severe).
diseases.treatment_plan: Prevent renal scarring by preventing UTIs. Use low-dose daily prophylactic antibiotics for mild-to-moderate cases. Manage bowel and bladder dysfunction. Severe (Grade V) cases or failure of medical management requires surgical ureteral reimplantation.
diseases.home_care: Administer daily prophylactic antibiotics consistently. Encourage frequent voiding and double-voiding (urinating twice in a few minutes) to empty the bladder. Treat constipation aggressively.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Trimethoprim-Sulfamethoxazole or Nitrofurantoin (generic active ingredients used at low, sub-therapeutic doses for daily UTI prophylaxis).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Consult a pediatric urologist if a child has recurrent febrile UTIs or if a VCUG confirms vesicoureteral reflux.
diseases.prevention: No primary prevention exists. Secondary prevention focuses on strict bowel and bladder habits and prophylaxis.
diseases.vaccination_info: No specific immunizations are associated with this anatomical urinary tract disorder.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent for mild-to-moderate cases (Grades I-III), which resolve spontaneously in over 80% of children as they grow. Severe cases carry a good prognosis with surgical correction.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Spontaneous resolution occurs over several years; surgical recovery in the hospital takes 1 to 3 days.
diseases.complications: Recurrent pyelonephritis, renal scarring, renal hypertension, and chronic kidney disease.

diseases.name: Ophthalmia Neonatorum (Neonatal Conjunctivitis)
diseases.affected_age_group: Neonates (Presents in the first 28 days of life)
diseases.causes: Chlamydia trachomatis (most common, presents at 5-14 days); Neisseria gonorrhoeae (most severe, presents at 2-5 days); Herpes Simplex Virus (presents at 5-11 days).
diseases.pathophysiology: Pathogens are transmitted from the maternal birth canal to the newborn's eyes during delivery. Neisseria gonorrhoeae rapidly penetrates intact corneal epithelium, causing severe conjunctival inflammation, corneal ulceration, and potential perforation.
diseases.symptoms_early: Mild watery or mucoid discharge from one or both eyes, accompanied by mild redness and swelling of the eyelids.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Bilateral eyelid swelling (chemosis) and hyper-purulent, thick green-yellow discharge that rapidly re-accumulates after wiping (highly characteristic of Gonorrhea).
diseases.symptoms_severe: Severe chemosis preventing opening of the eyes, corneal clouding, ulceration, perforation, and permanent blindness.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Gram stain of conjunctival exudate showing Gram-negative intracellular diplococci (Gonorrhea). PCR/NAAT swab of the conjunctiva to confirm Chlamydia or HSV.
diseases.treatment_plan: Initiate urgent systemic antibiotics (intravenous Ceftriaxone for Gonorrhea; oral Erythromycin for Chlamydia) to prevent systemic spread (such as chlamydial pneumonia). Frequent eye irrigation with sterile saline.
diseases.home_care: Home care is strictly secondary to hospitalization. Gently wipe away purulent discharge from the eyes with a warm, damp sterile compress. Wash hands thoroughly to prevent self-infection.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Ceftriaxone (intravenous active cephalosporin targeting Neisseria gonorrhoeae); Erythromycin (oral macrolide active ingredient targeting Chlamydia trachomatis); Erythromycin ophthalmic ointment.
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Any eye discharge or swelling in a newborn within the first month of life is a medical emergency requiring immediate pediatric evaluation.
diseases.prevention: Routine administration of Erythromycin 0.5% ophthalmic ointment to both eyes of all newborns within 1 hour of birth.
diseases.vaccination_info: No immunizations exist targeting these perinatal ocular pathogens.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent if treated early. Untreated gonococcal conjunctivitis can lead to permanent corneal scarring and blindness within 24–48 hours.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Resolution is typically seen within 5 to 7 days of starting appropriate systemic antibiotic therapy.
diseases.complications: Corneal ulceration, corneal perforation, permanent blindness, systemic sepsis, and chlamydial pneumonia.

diseases.name: Pediatric Dacryocystitis
diseases.classification: Infection & Inflammation of the Lacrimal Sac
diseases.affected_age_group: Infants and young children (Often associated with a persistent dacryocystobin obstruction/blocked tear duct)
diseases.causes: Streptococcus pneumoniae, Staphylococcus aureus, and Haemophilus influenzae. Often secondary to a persistent Nasolacrimal Duct Obstruction (NLDO).
diseases.pathophysiology: Congenital blockage of the nasolacrimal duct (frequently at the valve of Hasner) leads to stasis of tears in the lacrimal sac. This static fluid becomes a breeding ground for bacteria, triggering acute infection and swelling of the sac.
diseases.symptoms_early: Persistent watery eye (epiphora) and crusting of the eyelashes since birth, with occasional clear discharge.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Sudden redness, swelling, and tenderness over the lacrimal sac (the inner corner of the eyelid near the nose). Purulent material can be expressed from the punctum when pressing the sac.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Widespread cellulitis spreading to the surrounding cheek and eyelid (preseptal or orbital cellulitis), high fever, lethargy, and abscess formation over the lacrimal sac.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Clinical diagnosis based on localized erythema and swelling at the medial canthus. Expression of purulent fluid from the punctum. Culture of the discharge.
diseases.treatment_plan: Systemic oral or intravenous antibiotics targeting common respiratory flora. Perform daily lacrimal sac massage. If NLDO persists, plan for surgical nasolacrimal duct probing once the infection resolves.
diseases.home_care: Apply warm compresses over the inner corner of the eye. Perform Crigler massage (apply firm downward pressure over the lacrimal sac) to help open the duct, but avoid massage during the acute, painful stage of infection.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Amoxicillin-Clavulanate or Cephalexin (generic oral antibiotic active ingredients targeting pediatric respiratory and skin flora); Erythromycin ophthalmic ointment.
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Seek prompt care if an infant with a blocked tear duct develops redness, swelling, or warmth at the inner corner of the eye near the nose.
diseases.prevention: Perform daily lacrimal sac massage in infants with documented congenital nasolacrimal duct obstruction to prevent fluid stasis.
diseases.vaccination_info: No specific immunizations target this condition; ensure standard Hib and PCV13 vaccines are up to date.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent with antibiotic therapy and resolution of the underlying duct obstruction. Most cases of NLDO resolve spontaneously by 1 year of age.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Acute infection resolves within 5 to 7 days of starting antibiotics; nasolacrimal duct probing (if needed) provides immediate relief.
diseases.complications: Lacrimal sac abscess, fistula formation, preseptal cellulitis, orbital cellulitis, and recurrent infections.
diseases.overview: Infection & Inflammation of the Lacrimal Sac

diseases.name: Retinopathy of Prematurity (ROP)
diseases.classification: Vasoproliferative Retinal Disorder of Premature Infants
diseases.affected_age_group: Premature Neonates (Typically born <30 weeks gestation or birth weight <1500 grams)
diseases.causes: Incomplete retinal vascularization at birth, combined with exposure to high, unregulated oxygen concentrations (hyperoxia) and fluctuating oxygen levels.
diseases.pathophysiology: Retinal vessels grow from the center outward, completing development near birth. In a premature infant, growth is incomplete. Hyperoxia down-regulates Vascular Endothelial Growth Factor (VEGF), halting vessel growth. As the retina matures, it becomes hypoxic, triggering a massive, unregulated release of VEGF. This drives abnormal, fragile blood vessel growth (neovascularization) that leaks and pulls on the retina.
diseases.symptoms_early: Visually normal externally. ROP is asymptomatic in its early stages and can only be detected via dilated ophthalmoscopic screening.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Development of a white line (demarcation) separating vascularized and avascular retina, progressing to a ridge of tissue and neovascularization extending into the vitreous (Stages 1-3).
diseases.symptoms_severe: Subtotal or total retinal detachment (Stages 4-5) due to traction from scar tissue, resulting in leukocoria (white pupillary reflex), strabismus, and severe, irreversible blindness.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Serial dilated retinal examinations performed by a pediatric ophthalmologist in the NICU, beginning at 4 weeks of life or 31 weeks post-menstrual age.
diseases.treatment_plan: Carefully regulate neonatal oxygen delivery (maintain SpO2 targets between 91-95%). For severe ROP, apply laser photocoagulation to the avascular retina, or administer intravitreal injections of anti-VEGF agents.
diseases.home_care: Ensure absolute compliance with all follow-up ophthalmology appointments after discharge from the NICU. Monitor for any signs of visual preference or abnormal eye movements.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Ranibizumab or Bevacizumab (generic active monoclonal anti-VEGF antibodies used for intravitreal injection in severe cases).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Any premature infant meeting screening criteria must undergo scheduled ophthalmological evaluation; seek immediate care for a white pupillary reflex or lack of eye contact.
diseases.prevention: Avoid excessive, unregulated oxygen delivery in the NICU. Maintain stable oxygenation parameters and prevent maternal premature birth.
diseases.vaccination_info: No specific immunizations are associated with this developmental vascular disease.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent with early detection and modern therapies; over 90% of infants with early-stage ROP recover completely without treatment or visual loss.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Retinal vascularization completes around 40 to 45 weeks post-menstrual age; follow-up is maintained for years.
diseases.complications: Retinal detachment, severe myopia, strabismus, amblyopia, glaucoma, and permanent blindness.
diseases.overview: Vasoproliferative Retinal Disorder of Premature Infants

diseases.name: Congenital Cataracts
diseases.classification: Congenital Opacification of the Ocular Lens
diseases.affected_age_group: Neonates and Infants (Presents at birth or within the first year of life)
diseases.causes: Inherited genetic mutations (often autosomal dominant), intrauterine infections (TORCH syndrome: Rubella, CMV, Toxoplasmosis), or metabolic disorders (Galactosemia).
diseases.pathophysiology: Disruption of the normal arrangement of lens fibers or lens proteins (crystallins) during embryonic development leads to protein aggregation and loss of lens transparency, blocking light from reaching the retina.
diseases.symptoms_early: Leukocoria: a white or gray pupillary reflex instead of the normal red reflex, often noticed first in flash photographs.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Strabismus (crossed eyes), nystagmus (involuntary, rapid eye movements indicating failure to develop macular fixation), and poor visual tracking.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Irreversible amblyopia ("lazy eye" due to sensory deprivation), severe developmental delays, and permanent blindness if the obstruction is bilateral and not removed early.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Absence of the red reflex on ophthalmoscopic examination (Brückner test). Confirmed via slit-lamp examination of the lens.
diseases.treatment_plan: Urgently remove the opaque lens surgically (cataract extraction) within the first 6 to 8 weeks of life to allow light to stimulate the developing brain. Correct vision post-surgery with contact lenses, glasses, or an intraocular lens implant.
diseases.home_care: Strict compliance with post-surgical patching therapy (covering the stronger eye to force the brain to use the weaker eye) to treat and prevent amblyopia.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Atropine ophthalmic drops (used to dilate the pupil post-operatively); Prednisolone acetate ophthalmic drops (to manage post-surgical inflammation).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Seek immediate pediatric evaluation if you notice a white spot in your child's pupil, if their eyes seem to cross, or if they do not track objects by 3 months of age.
diseases.prevention: Ensure maternal rubella immunity prior to pregnancy and avoid teratogen exposure during gestation.
diseases.vaccination_info: Prevented in part by maternal immunization with the MMR vaccine prior to pregnancy to prevent congenital rubella syndrome.
diseases.prognosis: Good if surgical removal is performed in early infancy followed by aggressive patch therapy. Delayed surgery leads to permanent, severe amblyopia.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Surgical recovery is fast (1 to 2 weeks); visual rehabilitation and patching therapy span several years.
diseases.complications: Sensory deprivation amblyopia, glaucoma, strabismus, retinal detachment, and nystagmus.
diseases.overview: Congenital Opacification of the Ocular Lens

diseases.name: Acute Otitis Media (AOM)
diseases.affected_age_group: 6 Months to 2 Years (Peak incidence due to Eustachian tube anatomy)
diseases.causes: Streptococcus pneumoniae, non-typeable Haemophilus influenzae, and Moraxella catarrhalis. Often preceded by a viral upper respiratory infection.
diseases.pathophysiology: A viral upper respiratory infection causes swelling of the nasopharyngeal mucosa, obstructing the Eustachian tube. This creates negative pressure in the middle ear cavity, trapping secretions that become a breeding ground for bacteria. The resulting bacterial proliferation causes a suppurative fluid buildup that puts pressure on the tympanic membrane.
diseases.symptoms_early: Otalgia (ear pain) often presenting as ear pulling or rubbing in non-verbal infants, poor sleep, and a low-grade fever.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Irritability, decreased appetite, a higher fever (38.5°C), temporary conductive hearing loss, and a bulging, erythematous tympanic membrane with a loss of normal light reflex on otoscopy.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Sudden relief of ear pain followed immediately by purulent otorrhea (ear drainage), indicating a spontaneous rupture of the tympanic membrane. High fever accompanied by swelling or tenderness behind the ear.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Otoscopic examination showing a distinctly bulging tympanic membrane with impaired mobility on pneumatic otoscopy, accompanied by signs of acute inflammation.
diseases.treatment_plan: Provide effective pain management. For older children with mild unilateral symptoms, a "watchful waiting" approach for 48–72 hours may be appropriate. High-dose oral antibiotics are indicated for infants under 6 months, severe symptoms, or bilateral infections.
diseases.home_care: Apply warm compresses over the affected ear to help ease discomfort. Ensure the child stays well-hydrated and monitor for changes in pain or behavior.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Amoxicillin (first-line high-dose penicillin active ingredient for bacterial targets); Ibuprofen or Acetaminophen (systemic active ingredients used for effective pain relief).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Seek medical evaluation if a child develops a high fever, severe ear pain, fluid draining from the ear canal, or swelling and redness behind the ear.
diseases.prevention: Promote breastfeeding during the first 6 months of life, avoid exposure to second-hand tobacco smoke, and eliminate bottle-propping during feedings.
diseases.vaccination_info: Significantly reduced by routine administration of the Pneumococcal Conjugate Vaccine (PCV13) and annual Influenza vaccines.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent with appropriate management. Most cases resolve completely without residual hearing impairment or structural damage to the middle ear.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Acute symptoms typically improve within 24 to 48 hours of starting antibiotic therapy; middle ear fluid may persist for several weeks.
diseases.complications: Spontaneous tympanic membrane perforation, mastoiditis (bacterial infection of the mastoid air cells), labyrinthitis, and persistent conductive hearing loss.

diseases.name: Otitis Media with Effusion (OME)
diseases.affected_age_group: 2 to 5 Years (Peak occurrence as Eustachian tubes mature)
diseases.causes: Eustachian tube dysfunction following an episode of Acute Otitis Media, or secondary to allergic rhinitis or adenoid hypertrophy.
diseases.pathophysiology: Persistent Eustachian tube dysfunction leads to impaired ventilation of the middle ear, creating chronic negative pressure. This draws sterile, serous, or mucoid fluid out of the mucosal lining into the middle ear cleft, impairing tympanic membrane mobility and ossicular vibration.
diseases.symptoms_early: Often asymptomatic and painless. The child may exhibit mild inattentiveness, turning up the television volume, or pulling at the ears.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Persistent conductive hearing loss, popping or crackling sounds in the ear, and mild balance issues.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Significant speech and language delays in young children, behavioral issues in school, and visible retraction or thinning of the tympanic membrane (cholesteatoma risk).
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Otoscopy showing a retracted or neutral tympanic membrane that is amber or gray, with visible fluid levels or air bubbles. Tympanometry demonstrating a flat (Type B) curve.
diseases.treatment_plan: Watchful waiting for 3 months, as most cases resolve spontaneously. Treat underlying allergic rhinitis if present. Avoid antibiotics or antihistamines. If effusion persists $>3$ months with documented hearing loss, perform a myringotomy with tympanostomy tube insertion (ear tubes).
diseases.home_care: Eliminate exposure to second-hand tobacco smoke. Keep the child away from known environmental allergens. Encourage speech development through reading and talking.
diseases.medicinal_elements: None. Antibiotics, decongestants, and antihistamines are not recommended for uncomplicated OME.
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Consult a pediatrician if you notice your child turning up the TV, struggling with speech development, or if fluid persists in the ears weeks after an ear infection.
diseases.prevention: Prevent acute ear infections; treat allergies and avoid feeding infants while they are lying flat.
diseases.vaccination_info: Pneumococcal (PCV13) and Influenza vaccines help reduce the incidence of preceding AOM episodes.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent. Most cases resolve spontaneously. Surgical ear tube insertion provides immediate resolution of hearing loss and reduces fluid accumulation.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Fluid typically resolves spontaneously within 8 to 12 weeks; ear tubes remain in place for 6 to 18 months before shedding naturally.
diseases.complications: Conductive hearing loss, speech delay, tympanosclerosis, retraction pockets, and cholesteatoma.

diseases.name: Acute Tonsillopharyngitis
diseases.classification: Acute Inflammation of the Tonsils & Pharynx
diseases.affected_age_group: 3 to 15 Years (Viral causes are more common in younger children; bacterial in school-aged)
diseases.causes: Adenovirus, Rhinovirus, Epstein-Barr Virus (mononucleosis). Streptococcus pyogenes (Group A Beta-Hemolytic Streptococcus) causes 15–30% of pediatric cases.
diseases.pathophysiology: Invasion of the pharyngeal mucosa and tonsillar lymphoid tissue by viral or bacterial pathogens leads to cellular injury and recruitment of inflammatory mediators. This causes mucosal edema, vasodilation, and the formation of a purulent exudate on the tonsils.
diseases.symptoms_early: Sore throat, pain with swallowing (odynophagia), mild headache, and a low-grade fever.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Tonsillar enlargement, erythematous pharynx, painful anterior cervical lymphadenopathy, moderate fever (38.5°C), and tonsillar exudate (white/yellow patches).
diseases.symptoms_severe: Inability to swallow saliva (drooling), high fever, abdominal pain, vomiting, and a sandpapery rash (Scarlet Fever, associated with GAS). In mononucleosis: severe fatigue, splenomegaly, and generalized lymphadenopathy.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Rapid Antigen Detection Test (RADT) for Group A Strep (high specificity). Throat culture is the gold standard if RADT is negative in symptomatic children.
diseases.treatment_plan: Determine viral vs. bacterial etiology. Provide supportive care (pain relief, hydration). For confirmed GAS, administer a 10-day course of oral Penicillin or Amoxicillin to prevent acute rheumatic fever.
diseases.home_care: Encourage cold fluids, popsicles, and warm broths. Gargling with warm salt water can ease pharyngeal discomfort. Ensure the child completes the entire course of antibiotics if prescribed.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Penicillin V or Amoxicillin (generic oral antibiotic active ingredients for GAS pharyngitis); Ibuprofen or Acetaminophen (generic active ingredients for pain and fever control).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Seek medical care if a child is drooling, unable to swallow liquids, develops a stiff neck, has difficulty breathing, or shows a red sandpapery rash.
diseases.prevention: Avoid sharing cups or utensils with sick individuals. Practice rigorous hand hygiene and replace toothbrushes after starting antibiotics.
diseases.vaccination_info: No specific immunizations exist targeting Group A Streptococcus; up-to-date Haemophilus influenzae and pneumococcal vaccines are helpful.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent. Viral tonsillitis resolves spontaneously in 3-5 days. Bacterial tonsillitis resolves quickly with antibiotic therapy, and the child is no longer contagious after 24 hours of treatment.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Symptoms resolve within 3 to 7 days; antibiotic therapy for GAS must be maintained for a full 10 days.
diseases.complications: Peritonsillar abscess (Quinsy), retropharyngeal abscess, acute rheumatic fever, and post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis.
diseases.overview: Acute Inflammation of the Tonsils & Pharynx

diseases.name: Adenoid Hypertrophy
diseases.classification: Chronic Hyperplasia of the Nasopharyngeal Tonsils
diseases.affected_age_group: 2 to 6 Years (Peak size of lymphoid tissue; resolves as the airway grows)
diseases.causes: Chronic, recurrent upper respiratory tract infections, allergic rhinitis, or primary physiological hypertrophy.
diseases.pathophysiology: Recurrent immune stimulation of the pharyngeal tonsil (adenoid tissue) located in the posterior nasopharynx leads to cellular hyperplasia. Persistent enlargement obstructs the posterior nares and the Eustachian tube orifices.
diseases.symptoms_early: Persistent nasal congestion, mouth breathing (especially during sleep), and snoring.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Chronic mouth breathing, hyponasal speech ("muffled" voice), nocturnal sleep disruption, restless sleep, and chronic clear rhinorrhea.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA): marked by pauses in breathing during sleep followed by gasping, daytime somnolence, behavioral issues, and "adenoid facies" (elongated face, open mouth, high-arched palate).
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Lateral neck X-ray showing narrowing of the nasopharyngeal airway. Direct visualization via flexible nasal endoscopy.
diseases.treatment_plan: Initial trial of nasal corticosteroids to reduce mucosal inflammation and tissue volume. If obstructive sleep apnea, recurrent otitis media, or chronic sinusitis persists, perform surgical adenoidectomy.
diseases.home_care: Use saline nasal sprays to help clear mucus. Elevate the head of the child's bed slightly during sleep. Monitor and track sleep patterns (snoring, gasping).
diseases.medicinal_elements: Fluticasone propionate or Mometasone furoate (generic nasal corticosteroid active ingredients used to reduce lymphoid swelling).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Consult a pediatrician if your child snores loudly, has pauses in breathing during sleep, struggles to breathe through their nose, or exhibits persistent mouth breathing.
diseases.prevention: Prompt management of allergies and recurrent upper respiratory infections to limit chronic inflammation.
diseases.vaccination_info: No specific immunizations are associated with this lymphatic tissue hypertrophy.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent with surgical removal. Children show immediate improvement in nasal breathing, sleep quality, and reduction in ear infections.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Post-surgical recovery following adenoidectomy takes 3 to 7 days; pain is minimal compared to tonsillectomy.
diseases.complications: Obstructive sleep apnea, pulmonary hypertension (rare, severe), recurrent otitis media, facial skeletal changes, and chronic sinusitis.
diseases.overview: Chronic Hyperplasia of the Nasopharyngeal Tonsils

diseases.name: Anaphylaxis (Pediatric)
diseases.affected_age_group: All pediatric ages (Infants and young children have unique symptom presentations)
diseases.causes: Food allergens (peanuts, tree nuts, milk, eggs, shellfish) primarily; insect stings (wasps, bees); medications (penicillins); and latex.
diseases.pathophysiology: Previous exposure to an allergen generates antigen-specific IgE antibodies, which bind to mast cells and basophils. Re-exposure leads to rapid cross-linking of IgE, triggering massive, systemic degranulation. This releases histamine, tryptase, and prostaglandins, causing systemic vasodilation, increased capillary permeability, and smooth muscle spasm (bronchoconstriction).
diseases.symptoms_early: Generalized urticaria (hives), pruritus, warm flushing of the skin, and mild facial swelling (angioedema) within minutes of exposure.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Shortness of breath, wheezing, hoarseness, a dry "barking" cough, swelling of the tongue, abdominal pain, vomiting, and diarrhea.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Laryngeal edema causing inspiratory stridor, respiratory failure, hypotension, tachycardia, poor perfusion, hypotonia ("floppiness" in infants), and loss of consciousness (anaphylactic shock).
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Clinical diagnosis based on the rapid onset (minutes to hours) of skin/mucosal symptoms plus respiratory compromise or cardiovascular collapse following exposure to a known or likely allergen.
diseases.treatment_plan: Immediately administer intramuscular Epinephrine into the anterolateral thigh. Secure the airway and give high-flow oxygen. Place the child in a recumbent position with legs elevated. Provide intravenous fluids to support blood pressure.
diseases.home_care: Carry two auto-injectors of epinephrine at all times. Train parents, caregivers, and school staff on how to recognize symptoms and use the auto-injector. Create a Food Allergy & Anaphylaxis Emergency Action Plan.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Epinephrine (first-line active rescue ingredient for vasoconstriction and bronchodilation); Diphenhydramine (second-line generic H1-antihistamine active ingredient for hives); Methylprednisolone (generic active steroid to prevent biphasic reactions).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Any suspected anaphylactic reaction requires immediate administration of epinephrine and emergency transport to the nearest hospital, as symptoms can return hours later (biphasic reaction).
diseases.prevention: Strict avoidance of known allergens; read food labels carefully and prevent cross-contamination.
diseases.vaccination_info: No specific immunizations exist; children with egg allergies can safely receive the MMR and Influenza vaccines under standard precautions.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent if epinephrine is administered early; delayed epinephrine is the primary risk factor for fatal outcomes.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Acute symptoms resolve within minutes to hours of epinephrine administration; hospital observation is required for 4 to 12 hours.
diseases.complications: Hypoxic brain injury, myocardial ischemia, biphasic anaphylaxis, and respiratory arrest.

diseases.name: Allergic Rhinitis (Hay Fever)
diseases.classification: Type I IgE-Mediated Nasal Mucosal Hypersensitivity
diseases.affected_age_group: Preschoolers through Adolescents (Rarely diagnosed before 2 years of age)
diseases.causes: Inhaled environmental allergens: seasonal (pollen from trees, grass, weeds) or perennial (dust mites, mold, pet dander).
diseases.pathophysiology: Inhaled allergens bind to IgE antibodies on nasal mast cells, triggering the release of histamine and leukotrienes. This causes immediate mucosal vasodilation, vascular permeability, and mucus production (early phase). Late-phase inflammation recruits eosinophils, driving chronic nasal congestion.
diseases.symptoms_early: Paroxysmal sneezing, clear watery rhinorrhea, and itching of the nose, eyes, and palate.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Persistent nasal congestion, mouth breathing, post-nasal drip causing a nighttime cough, and dark circles under the eyes ("allergic shiners" due to venous stasis).
diseases.symptoms_severe: Chronic sinus pressure, fatigue, headache, decreased sense of smell, "allergic salute" (transverse nasal crease from pushing the nose upward), and eczema or asthma flares.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Clinical diagnosis based on history and typical exam findings (pale, boggy, bluish nasal turbinates). Confirm triggers with skin prick testing or allergen-specific IgE blood tests.
diseases.treatment_plan: Identify and avoid triggers. Use intranasal corticosteroids as first-line therapy for chronic symptoms, and non-sedating oral antihistamines for acute itching and sneezing.
diseases.home_care: Keep windows closed during high pollen seasons. Use dust mite-proof covers on pillows and mattresses. Vacuum regularly with a HEPA filter. Perform saline nasal rinses.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Fluticasone propionate (generic nasal corticosteroid active ingredient to suppress mucosal inflammation); Cetirizine or Loratadine (generic non-sedating H1-antihistamine active ingredients).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Consult a pediatrician if symptoms interfere with your child's sleep, school performance, or are accompanied by recurrent ear or sinus infections.
diseases.prevention: Limit exposure to environmental allergens; maintain low humidity in the home to prevent mold and dust mites.
diseases.vaccination_info: Allergen immunotherapy (allergy shots or sublingual tablets) is available to desensitize the immune system over time.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent for symptom control, though the condition is chronic and requires ongoing management.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Seasonal symptoms vary with pollen counts; daily medication provides relief within hours (antihistamines) to days (nasal steroids).
diseases.complications: Chronic sinusitis, otitis media, worsening asthma control, sleep disruption, and dental malocclusion (from chronic mouth breathing).
diseases.overview: Type I IgE-Mediated Nasal Mucosal Hypersensitivity

diseases.name: Severe Combined Immunodeficiency (SCID)
diseases.affected_age_group: Infants (Presents in the first few months of life with severe infections)
diseases.causes: Genetic mutations (most commonly X-linked, caused by a mutation in the IL2RG gene; or autosomal recessive, due to ADA deficiency), causing a failure of T-cell, B-cell, and NK-cell development.
diseases.pathophysiology: The genetic defect disrupts essential cytokine signaling or enzyme pathways required for lymphocyte maturation. The complete absence of functional T-cells and secondary failure of antibody production leaves the infant defenseless against viral, bacterial, and fungal pathogens.
diseases.symptoms_early: Recurrent, severe bacterial infections, persistent oral thrush (candidiasis), and diaper rashes that do not respond to standard treatments.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Failure to thrive, chronic intractable diarrhea, and recurrent opportunistic infections (such as Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia).
diseases.symptoms_severe: Generalized erythroderma (GVHD from maternal T-cells crossing the placenta), severe interstitial pneumonia, disseminated viral infections, and death before age 1 if untreated.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Newborn screening measuring T-cell Receptor Excision Circles (TRECs) from a heel stick. Confirmed by flow cytometry showing absent or extremely low T-cell counts, and genetic testing.
diseases.treatment_plan: Place the infant in strict protective isolation (reverse isolation). Initiate prophylactic antibiotics and antifungals. Administer intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) replacement. The curative treatment is a hematopoietic stem cell transplant (bone marrow transplant) or gene therapy.
diseases.home_care: Avoid all public spaces, sick contacts, and unboiled water. Administer prophylactic medications consistently. Maintain a sterile environment.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Intravenous Immunoglobulin (IVIG - active antibodies to provide passive immunity); Trimethoprim-Sulfamethoxazole (prophylactic antibiotic for Pneumocystis); Fluconazole (active antifungal to prevent systemic candidiasis).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Any positive newborn screening for TREC, or an infant with recurrent severe infections, thrush, and failure to thrive requires immediate immunological evaluation.
diseases.prevention: Avoid all exposure to potential pathogens. Genetic counseling for carrier parents.
diseases.vaccination_info: Live viral or bacterial vaccines (such as Rotavirus, MMR, Varicella, and BCG) are strictly contraindicated and can be fatal.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent if a bone marrow transplant is performed in the first 3 months of life (survival >90%). It is universally fatal by age 1 to 2 if untreated.
diseases.recovery_timeline: This is a life-threatening genetic immunodeficiency; bone marrow transplant recovery and immune reconstitution take 6 to 12 months.
diseases.complications: Opportunistic infections, GVHD, nutritional failure, organ damage, and death.

diseases.name: Selective IgA Deficiency
diseases.affected_age_group: Preschoolers through Adolescents (Often diagnosed during evaluations for recurrent infections or celiac disease)
diseases.causes: Unknown etiology; genetic factors lead to a failure of B-lymphocytes to mature into IgA-producing plasma cells.
diseases.pathophysiology: The selective absence or reduction of immunoglobulin A (IgA) in the blood and mucosal secretions (saliva, tears, gut). Because IgA is the primary defense at mucosal barriers, its absence increases susceptibility to respiratory and gastrointestinal pathogens.
diseases.symptoms_early: Often completely asymptomatic. The child may exhibit slightly more frequent viral upper respiratory tract infections.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Recurrent otitis media, sinusitis, bronchitis, or chronic gastroenteritis. Increased incidence of atopic diseases (asthma, eczema).
diseases.symptoms_severe: Development of autoimmune disorders (such as Celiac disease, Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis, or ITP). Anaphylactic reactions can occur when receiving blood products containing trace amounts of IgA (antibodies are generated against foreign IgA).
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Serum IgA levels <7 mg/dL with normal IgG and IgM levels in a child older than 4 years (to rule out transient delay).
diseases.treatment_plan: Manage active infections promptly with antibiotics. Monitor for associated autoimmune diseases. Standard immunoglobulin replacement (IVIG) is not indicated and is contraindicated due to anaphylaxis risks.
diseases.home_care: Wear a medical alert bracelet indicating IgA deficiency to prevent accidental administration of standard blood products. Practice good hand hygiene and seek care early for respiratory infections.
diseases.medicinal_elements: None. Antibiotic therapy is used only to treat active bacterial infections.
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Consult a pediatrician if your child has recurrent respiratory infections, persistent diarrhea, or develops joint pain or chronic skin rashes.
diseases.prevention: No preventative measures exist, as this is a primary immunodeficiency.
diseases.vaccination_info: Children can receive all routine immunizations, including live vaccines, as T-cell function remains intact.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent. Most children have a normal lifespan and remain asymptomatic, though ongoing monitoring for autoimmune diseases is required.
diseases.recovery_timeline: This is a lifelong genetic immune configuration; infections are managed as they occur.
diseases.complications: Autoimmune diseases (Celiac, JIA), chronic lung disease from recurrent infections, and anaphylaxis to blood transfusions.

diseases.name: Measles (Rubeola)
diseases.affected_age_group: Unvaccinated infants, young children, and adolescents.
diseases.causes: Measles virus (a single-stranded RNA paramyxovirus).
diseases.pathophysiology: The virus is transmitted via respiratory droplets and is highly contagious. It infects the respiratory epithelium, spreads to regional lymph nodes, and enters the bloodstream. It causes systemic endothelial inflammation and targets the skin and mucous membranes, triggering a T-cell mediated immune reaction.
diseases.symptoms_early: High fever (often >39.5°C), accompanied by the "3 Cs": Cough, Coryza (runny nose), and Conjunctivitis (red, watery, photophobic eyes). Koplik Spots: tiny white-blue spots on a red background inside the cheeks appear 1 to 2 days before the rash.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: A maculopapular (flat and bumpy) red rash starting on the face and behind the ears, spreading downward (cephalocaudal progression) to the neck, trunk, arms, and legs.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Sustained high fever, respiratory distress (pneumonia), neurological symptoms (seizures, altered consciousness due to encephalitis), and severe diarrhea leading to dehydration.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Detection of measles-specific IgM antibodies in serum. Confirm viral RNA via PCR throat or nasopharyngeal swab.
diseases.treatment_plan: Supportive care: hydration and antipyretics. Administer high-dose Vitamin A immediately upon diagnosis, which significantly reduces complications and mortality.
diseases.home_care: Isolate the child strictly to prevent spreading. Keep the room dimly lit if photophobia is present. Offer cool fluids and monitor breathing closely.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Vitamin A (elemental retinyl palmitate active ingredient given orally to prevent blindness and pneumonia complications); Acetaminophen (for fever comfort).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Seek immediate evaluation if your child develops a high fever, cough, and red eyes, or if a rash appears, especially if they are unvaccinated. Go to the ER for shortness of breath or extreme lethargy.
diseases.prevention: Strict isolation of infected individuals. Post-exposure prophylaxis with MMR vaccine or immunoglobulin for unvaccinated contacts within 72 hours.
diseases.vaccination_info: Prevented almost entirely by the Measles, Mumps, and Rubella (MMR) vaccine series (first dose at 12-15 months, second dose at 4-6 years).
diseases.prognosis: Good in well-nourished children. Complications are more common in infants, malnourished children, and immunocompromised patients.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Acute illness resolves in 7 to 10 days; the rash fades in the order it appeared, leaving a brown discoloration and skin peeling.
diseases.complications: Otitis media, bronchopneumonia (most common cause of death), acute encephalitis, and Subacute Sclerosing Panencephalitis (SSPE - a fatal degenerative brain disease presenting years later).

diseases.name: Varicella Zoster (Chickenpox)
diseases.affected_age_group: Unvaccinated preschool and school-aged children.
diseases.causes: Varicella-Zoster Virus (VZV - Human Herpesvirus 3).
diseases.pathophysiology: VZV enters via the respiratory tract or direct contact, replicates in local lymphoid tissue, and enters the bloodstream. It targets the capillary endothelial cells in the epidermis, causing localized fluid accumulation and vesicle formation. The virus then travels up sensory nerves to establish lifelong latency in dorsal root ganglia.
diseases.symptoms_early: Low-grade fever, mild headache, malaise, and loss of appetite, followed within 24 hours by an intensely itchy rash.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: The rash starts as small red spots (macules) that quickly turn into raised bumps (papules), then fluid-filled blisters (vesicles) on a red base (described as "dewdrops on a rose petal"). The blisters break and form crusts.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Widespread rash with hundreds of lesions covering the mouth, throat, and genitals. High fever, persistent vomiting, ataxia (loss of balance), or severe chest pain, suggesting complications.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Typically clinical diagnosis based on the unique appearance of lesions in various stages of development (macules, papules, vesicles, crusts present simultaneously). PCR of vesicle fluid can confirm VZV.
diseases.treatment_plan: Supportive care: manage fever and itching. Avoid aspirin due to Reye syndrome risk. Administer oral Acyclovir for high-risk cohorts (adolescents, children with chronic skin conditions).
diseases.home_care: Apply calamine lotion to the lesions. Give lukewarm baths with colloidal oatmeal. Keep fingernails short and clean to prevent scratching and secondary bacterial infection. Keep the child isolated until all lesions have crusted.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Acetaminophen (generic active ingredient for fever; avoid ibuprofen as it increases invasive streptococcal skin infection risk); Acyclovir (active antiviral used in older or high-risk children).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Consult a doctor if the skin lesions become very red, swollen, or warm (secondary bacterial infection), if the child develops a high fever, or has trouble walking or breathing.
diseases.prevention: Post-exposure prophylaxis with varicella vaccine or varicella zoster immunoglobulin (VZIG) for high-risk exposed contacts.
diseases.vaccination_info: Prevented almost entirely by the Varicella vaccine series (doses given at 12-15 months and 4-6 years).
diseases.prognosis: Excellent in healthy children; symptoms are typically mild. Adolescents and adults are at higher risk for severe disease.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Acute illness resolves within 7 to 10 days; all lesions crust over by day 6-7.
diseases.complications: Secondary bacterial skin infections (Staphylococcus aureus or Group A Streptococcus cellulitis), cerebellar ataxia, varicella pneumonia, and Reye syndrome.

diseases.name: Pertussis (Whooping Cough)
diseases.affected_age_group: Infants under 6 months (Highest risk for severe complications; unvaccinated children)
diseases.causes: Bordetella pertussis (a Gram-negative, fastidious coccobacillus).
diseases.pathophysiology: The bacteria colonize the ciliated respiratory epithelium, producing toxins (pertussis toxin, tracheal cytotoxin) that paralyze the cilia and trigger localized inflammation. This leads to impaired mucus clearance, necrosis of epithelial cells, and severe, spasmodic coughing fits.
diseases.symptoms_early: Catarrhal stage (lasts 1-2 weeks): mild upper respiratory symptoms including rhinorrhea, sneezing, low-grade fever, and a mild, occasional cough.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Paroxysmal stage (lasts 2-6 weeks): severe coughing fits (paroxysms) occurring 15-30 times a day. The child coughs repeatedly on a single expiration, followed by a forced inspiratory "whoop" sound as they gasp for air. Post-tussive vomiting (vomiting after coughing) is highly characteristic.
diseases.symptoms_severe: In infants: coughing fits may be absent; instead, they present with apnea, cyanosis (turning blue), bradycardia, seizures, and severe exhaustion.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Nasopharyngeal swab culture (gold standard but slow) or PCR assay for Bordetella pertussis DNA.
diseases.treatment_plan: Administer macrolide antibiotics early in the course (catarrhal stage) to shorten the disease. Prophylaxis is indicated for all household contacts. Hospitalize infants under 6 months for monitoring of apnea and airway support.
diseases.home_care: Use a cool-mist humidifier to help loosen secretions. Keep the home free of irritants (smoke, dust). Offer small, frequent meals to prevent vomiting after coughing fits.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Azithromycin or Erythromycin (generic macrolide antibiotic active ingredients used to eradicate B. pertussis and reduce transmission). Cough suppressants are not recommended.
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Any infant showing gasping, pauses in breathing (apnea), or turning blue during coughing fits requires immediate emergency medical evaluation.
diseases.prevention: Post-exposure antibiotic prophylaxis for all close contacts regardless of vaccination status.
diseases.vaccination_info: Prevented through the DTaP vaccine series in childhood (at 2, 4, 6, 15-18 months, and 4-6 years) and Tdap booster in adolescence. Maternal vaccination during pregnancy provides passive immunity to newborns.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent for older children. It is a severe, high-mortality disease in young infants under 2 months who have not received their first vaccine dose.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Convalescent stage lasts weeks to months (often called the "100-day cough") as the respiratory cilia slowly regenerate.
diseases.complications: Pneumonia (most common), hypoxic encephalopathy, seizures, subconjunctival hemorrhages, rib fractures, and death in young infants.

diseases.name: Congenital Toxoplasmosis
diseases.affected_age_group: Neonates (Infection acquired in utero)
diseases.causes: Toxoplasma gondii (an obligate intracellular protozoan parasite). Transmission occurs when a non-immune pregnant mother acquires a primary infection from raw meat or cat feces.
diseases.pathophysiology: The parasite crosses the placenta, invades the fetal bloodstream, and targets the central nervous system and retinas. Tachyzoites multiply inside cells, causing tissue necrosis and leaving calcium deposits in the brain.
diseases.symptoms_early: Term newborns are often asymptomatic at birth (up to 70–90%). Early signs include unexplained thrombocytopenia, petechiae, and mild jaundice.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: The classic triad: Chorioretinitis (inflammation and scarring of the retina), Hydrocephalus (enlarged head due to blocked CSF flow), and Intracranial Calcifications (scattered calcifications on brain imaging).
diseases.symptoms_severe: Microcephaly, severe developmental delays, seizures, hepatosplenomegaly, and severe visual impairment or blindness.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Detection of Toxoplasma-specific IgM or IgA antibodies in newborn serum. Confirm parasite DNA in cerebrospinal or amniotic fluid via PCR.
diseases.treatment_plan: Initiate a 1-year course of oral Pyrimethamine and Sulfadiazine, supplemented with leucovorin (folinic acid) to prevent bone marrow suppression. Add systemic corticosteroids if active chorioretinitis is present.
diseases.home_care: Ensure consistent daily administration of complex medications. Schedule regular pediatric neurology and ophthalmology follow-ups.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Pyrimethamine (active anti-protozoal folic acid antagonist); Sulfadiazine (active sulfonamide antibiotic); Leucovorin (generic folinic acid active ingredient to protect bone marrow).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Pregnant women should consult their doctor immediately if exposed to cat litter or raw meat. Neonates with unexplained head enlargement or failed vision screens require urgent evaluation.
diseases.prevention: Pregnant women must avoid cleaning cat litter boxes, eat only thoroughly cooked meat, and wash hands after handling soil or raw vegetables.
diseases.vaccination_info: No immunization exists for Toxoplasma gondii.
diseases.prognosis: Variable. Early, aggressive treatment for 1 year significantly improves cognitive and visual outcomes. Untreated infants face severe neurological disabilities and progressive vision loss.
diseases.recovery_timeline: This is a congenital infection requiring a full 1-year course of antiparasitic therapy; neurological monitoring continues long-term.
diseases.complications: Permanent blindness, severe developmental delay, spasticity, epilepsy, and hearing loss.

diseases.name: Down Syndrome (Trisomy 21)
diseases.affected_age_group: Apparent at birth (Screened prenatally; lifelong condition)
diseases.causes: Meiotic nondisjunction during gametogenesis leading to three copies of chromosome 21 (95% of cases); translocation or mosaicism in the remainder.
diseases.pathophysiology: The presence of an extra copy of chromosome 21 results in the overexpression of genes on this chromosome. This alters embryonic development, leading to characteristic craniofacial features, cognitive impairment, congenital heart defects, and immune system changes.
diseases.symptoms_early: Moderate to severe hypotonia ("floppiness") at birth, a flat facial profile, upslanting palpebral fissures (eye slits), small ears, and a single transverse palmar crease (Simian crease).
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Delayed motor milestones (sitting, walking), mild to moderate intellectual disability, short stature, and speech delays.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Congenital heart disease (such as an Atrioventricular Septal Defect), duodenal atresia (blockage of the intestine), hearing loss, thyroid dysfunction, and an increased risk of childhood leukemia.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Confirmed by karyotype analysis showing three copies of chromosome 21. Prenatal screening via cell-free DNA (cfDNA) or ultrasound markers (increased nuchal translucency).
diseases.treatment_plan: Multidisciplinary supportive management. Screen for associated medical conditions: echocardiogram at birth, annual thyroid function tests, regular hearing and vision exams, and early intervention therapies (physical, occupational, and speech therapies).
diseases.home_care: Support developmental milestones through structured play, physical exercises, and speech stimulation. Maintain regular pediatric checkups.
diseases.medicinal_elements: None. This is a primary chromosomal genetic condition; medications are used only to manage secondary complications (e.g., levothyroxine for hypothyroidism).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Any newborn suspected of Down syndrome based on physical features requires a karyotype. Seek urgent care for rapid breathing, poor feeding, or blue coloring, suggesting a heart defect.
diseases.prevention: No prevention exists; incidence increases with maternal age. Genetic counseling is recommended.
diseases.vaccination_info: Children with Down syndrome should receive all routine immunizations, as they have an increased susceptibility to respiratory infections.
diseases.prognosis: Good. With modern medical care and early intervention, life expectancy has increased to 60 years or older.
diseases.recovery_timeline: This is a lifelong chromosomal genetic condition requiring continuous multidisciplinary support.
diseases.complications: Congenital heart disease, thyroid dysfunction, celiac disease, sleep apnea, atlantoaxial instability, and early-onset Alzheimer's disease.

diseases.name: Turner Syndrome (45,X)
diseases.affected_age_group: Infancy through Adolescence (Often diagnosed in infancy due to physical features, or in adolescence due to short stature/amenorrhea; affects girls)
diseases.causes: Loss of one of the sex chromosomes (X chromosome) during gametogenesis or early mitotic division, resulting in a 45,X karyotype (or mosaicism).
diseases.pathophysiology: The lack of the second sex chromosome leads to the accelerated loss of oocytes in fetal ovaries, resulting in "streak ovaries" and estrogen deficiency. A deficiency in the SHOX gene on the X chromosome causes short stature and skeletal anomalies.
diseases.symptoms_early: Lymphedema (swelling) of the hands and feet at birth, a webbed neck (cystic hygroma remnant), a low posterior hairline, and a broad chest with widely spaced nipples.
