diseases.symptoms_progressive: Progressive growth failure leading to short stature (typically apparent by age 5). Normal intelligence but potential difficulty with spatial processing.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Primary amenorrhea (failure to start periods) and lack of breast development due to ovarian dysgenesis (gonadal streak). Congenital heart anomalies, particularly Coarctation of the Aorta and bicuspid aortic valve.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Confirmed by chromosomal karyotype analysis showing a 45,X configuration or mosaic variant.
diseases.treatment_plan: Initiate recombinant growth hormone therapy in early childhood to maximize adult height. Begin estrogen replacement therapy around age 11-12 to induce puberty, breast development, and support bone health, transitioning to cyclic progesterone.
diseases.home_care: Monitor growth closely using specialized Turner syndrome growth charts. Support self-esteem and address learning concerns related to spatial skills.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Somatropin (generic recombinant growth hormone active ingredient); Estradiol (generic active estrogen replacement ingredient); Progesterone (generic active progestin hormone).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Seek evaluation if a girl has unexplained short stature, lack of pubertal development, or if newborn features like webbed neck or swollen feet were present.
diseases.prevention: No preventative measures exist, as this is a random genetic nondisjunction event.
diseases.vaccination_info: No specific immunizations are associated with this chromosomal condition.
diseases.prognosis: Good. With growth hormone and estrogen therapy, girls can achieve normal height ranges and lead fulfilling lives, though fertility is generally absent (except via assisted reproduction).
diseases.recovery_timeline: This is a lifelong chromosomal genetic condition requiring active management during growth and transition to adulthood.
diseases.complications: Coarctation of the aorta, aortic dissection, hypertension, horseshoe kidney, hypothyroidism, osteoporosis, and hearing loss.

diseases.name: Cystic Fibrosis (CF)
diseases.affected_age_group: Infancy through Adulthood (Screened at birth; symptoms develop in infancy)
diseases.causes: A mutation in the CFTR gene on chromosome 7, most commonly the delta-F508 mutation, which codes for the cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator protein.
diseases.pathophysiology: The mutation disrupts the CFTR chloride channel, preventing chloride transport out of epithelial cells. This leads to increased sodium and water absorption, drying out mucosal surfaces and producing abnormally thick, sticky secretions in the lungs, pancreas, liver, and intestines, which blocks duct channels.
diseases.symptoms_early: Meconium ileus (bowel obstruction) in newborns within 48 hours of birth, or failure to thrive despite a robust appetite in early infancy.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Recurrent, wet cough, chronic wheezing, frequent bronchopneumonia (often infected with Pseudomonas aeruginosa), and pale, bulky, foul-smelling greasy stools (steatorrhea due to pancreatic insufficiency).
diseases.symptoms_severe: Progressive bronchiectasis, chronic respiratory failure, digital clubbing, nasal polyps, CF-related diabetes, biliary cirrhosis, and male infertility.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Newborn screening showing elevated immunoreactive trypsinogen (IRT). Confirmed by a Sweat Chloride Test showing chloride levels $ge 60	ext{ mEq/L}$ on two separate occasions.
diseases.treatment_plan: Aggressive pulmonary hygiene, including daily chest physiotherapy and nebulized bronchodilators/mucolytics. Administer oral pancreatic enzyme replacement capsules with every meal. Use CFTR modulator drugs if indicated by the genotype.
diseases.home_care: Perform chest percussion or use an airway clearance vest twice daily. Provide a high-calorie, high-fat diet with fat-soluble vitamin supplements (A, D, E, K). Maintain excellent hand hygiene.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Pancrelipase (generic active pancreatic enzyme replacement capsules containing lipase, protease, and amylase); Dornase alfa (nebulized recombinant DNase active ingredient to thin sputum); Ivacaftor or Lumacaftor (CFTR modulator active ingredients targeting specific mutations).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Seek prompt care if a child with CF developments a fever, has increased cough, changes in sputum color, shortness of breath, or weight loss (pulmonary exacerbation).
diseases.prevention: Genetic carrier screening for parents; prenatal testing for known mutations.
diseases.vaccination_info: Up-to-date immunizations are critical, including annual influenza and pneumococcal vaccines, to protect the lungs.
diseases.prognosis: Progressive, life-shortening disorder. With modern aggressive therapy and CFTR modulators, median survival has increased to over 40-50 years.
diseases.recovery_timeline: This is a lifelong genetic multi-system disorder requiring daily complex treatment.
diseases.complications: Chronic respiratory failure, bronchiectasis, pneumothorax, hemoptysis, distal intestinal obstruction syndrome, CF-related diabetes, and liver failure.

diseases.name: Williams Syndrome
diseases.affected_age_group: Apparent in infancy and early childhood (Lifelong condition)
diseases.causes: A microdeletion of approximately 26-28 genes on chromosome 7q11.23, including the elastin gene (ELN).
diseases.pathophysiology: The deletion of the elastin gene causes vascular abnormalities, particularly supravalvular aortic stenosis. Deletion of other genes in the region affects neurodevelopment, leading to characteristic facial features, cognitive profile, and connective tissue laxity.
diseases.symptoms_early: Infantile hypercalcemia (high calcium levels causing irritability and feeding difficulties), low birth weight, and a distinct "elfin" facial appearance (broad forehead, short nose with a broad tip, full cheeks, and wide mouth with full lips).
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Developmental delays, mild to moderate intellectual disability, a unique "cocktail party" personality (highly social, friendly, empathetic, and loquacious), and an extreme affinity/sensitivity to music.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Supravalvular Aortic Stenosis (SVAS - narrowing of the aorta just above the valve), renal artery stenosis causing severe hypertension, and progressive joint contractures.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Confirmed by Fluorescence In Situ Hybridization (FISH) or chromosomal microarray showing the deletion on chromosome 7q11.23.
diseases.treatment_plan: Supportive multidisciplinary care. Perform baseline and regular echocardiograms to monitor the aorta and heart. Monitor blood calcium levels in infancy. Provide early intervention speech, occupational, and physical therapies.
diseases.home_care: Support developmental and learning needs. Avoid calcium-fortified foods and excess vitamin D in infancy if hypercalcemia is present. Manage anxiety and sensory sensitivities.
diseases.medicinal_elements: None. Treatment is supportive; surgical correction may be needed for severe supravalvular aortic stenosis.
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Seek pediatric evaluation if an infant is unusually irritable, has feeding issues (hypercalcemia signs), or exhibits the characteristic facial features or a heart murmur.
diseases.prevention: No preventative measures exist, as the microdeletion is typically a random, de novo event.
diseases.vaccination_info: No specific immunizations are associated with this genetic deletion.
diseases.prognosis: Good. Most individuals live happy, active lives, though they require varying levels of support in adulthood. Cardiovascular complications dictate life expectancy.
diseases.recovery_timeline: This is a lifelong genetic microdeletion syndrome requiring ongoing medical and developmental support.
diseases.complications: Supravalvular aortic stenosis, severe hypertension, chronic hypercalcemia, joint contractures, and severe anxiety.

diseases.name: Nutritional Rickets
diseases.affected_age_group: Infants and Toddlers (6 to 24 months; high risk in breastfed infants without supplementation or dark skin)
diseases.causes: Inadequate vitamin D intake and lack of exposure to sunlight, leading to calcium and phosphorus malabsorption. Dietary calcium deficiency is a secondary cause.
diseases.pathophysiology: Vitamin D is required for intestinal calcium absorption. Deficiency leads to hypocalcemia, which stimulates parathyroid hormone (PTH) release. PTH mobilizes calcium from bones and increases renal phosphorus wasting. This lack of calcium and phosphorus prevents normal mineralization of the osteoid matrix at the growing epiphyseal plates.
diseases.symptoms_early: Craniotabes: thinning and softening of the skull bones (feels like a ping-pong ball) in young infants, along with irritability and delayed closure of the fontanelles.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Rachitic Rosary: visible enlargement of the costochondral junctions (bead-like bumps along the ribs). Harrison Groove: a horizontal depression along the lower chest border where the diaphragm pulls on soft ribs.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Bowing of the weight-bearing long bones (genu varum/bowlegs or genu valgum/knock-knees), widening of the wrists and ankles, delayed tooth eruption, muscle weakness, and hypocalcemic tetany or seizures.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Plain X-rays of the wrists showing cupping, fraying, and widening of the metaphyses. Serum labs showing low 25-hydroxyvitamin D, low calcium, low phosphorus, and elevated alkaline phosphatase.
diseases.treatment_plan: Administer therapeutic oral doses of Vitamin D3 (Cholecalciferol) combined with calcium supplementation. Ensure daily maintenance Vitamin D supplementation post-recovery.
diseases.home_care: Encourage safe, brief outdoor sunlight exposure. Provide a diet rich in calcium (dairy, fortified foods). Ensure daily adherence to vitamin drops.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Cholecalciferol (Vitamin D3 - generic active therapeutic and maintenance ingredient); Calcium carbonate or Calcium gluconate (generic active calcium replacement ingredients).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Consult a pediatrician if you notice bowing of your child's legs, swelling of their wrists/ankles, or if your exclusively breastfed infant is not receiving vitamin D drops.
diseases.prevention: Administer 400 IU of daily oral Vitamin D supplementation to all exclusively breastfed infants starting in the first few days of life.
diseases.vaccination_info: No specific immunizations are associated with this metabolic bone disease.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent. Bone abnormalities improve or completely remodel with Vitamin D and calcium replacement. Severe bowing may require orthopedic monitoring.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Radiographical healing of bone lesions is visible within 2 to 4 weeks of initiating therapy; full bone remodeling takes several months.
diseases.complications: Permanent bone deformities, short stature, hypocalcemic seizures, dental defects, and developmental delays.

diseases.name: Pediatric Scurvy
diseases.affected_age_group: Toddlers and young children (Rare; typically seen in children with developmental issues, autism with severe food selectivity, or poor diets)
diseases.causes: Inadequate dietary intake of ascorbic acid (Vitamin C) due to a diet lacking fresh fruits and vegetables.
diseases.pathophysiology: Vitamin C is an essential cofactor for the enzyme prolyl hydroxylase, which is required for collagen synthesis. Deficiency impairs collagen formation, weakening connective tissues, blood vessel walls, and bone matrix.
diseases.symptoms_early: Irritability, loss of appetite, mild low-grade fever, and generalized leg pain, causing the child to refuse to stand or walk.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Swollen, painful joints, and "frog-leg" posture (hips and knees flexed and abducted due to bone pain). Bleeding, swollen, spongy gums, and petechiae around hair follicles (perifollicular hemorrhages).
diseases.symptoms_severe: Subperiosteal hemorrhages (bleeding under the bone membrane) causing severe bone pain, loose teeth, poor wound healing, anemia (due to bleeding and impaired iron absorption), and severe bruising.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Plain X-rays of the knees showing characteristic signs: White Line of Frankel (dense zone of provisional calcification), Wimberger Ring (lucent center of epiphyseal center), and Trümmerfeld Zone (scurvy line/fracture zone). Serum ascorbic acid levels <0.2 mg/dL.
diseases.treatment_plan: Administer daily oral or intravenous Vitamin C (Ascorbic Acid) replacement. Dietary modification to include Vitamin C-rich foods. Address underlying food selectivity issues.
diseases.home_care: Handle the child very gently to minimize bone pain. Provide a soft diet if gums are bleeding and painful. Offer orange juice or other vitamin-rich foods.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Ascorbic acid (Vitamin C - generic active therapeutic replacement ingredient).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Seek pediatric evaluation if your child develops severe leg pain, refuses to walk, has swollen bleeding gums, or if their diet is extremely restricted (e.g., only consuming milk and bread).
diseases.prevention: Ensure a diet containing fresh fruits (citrus, strawberries) and vegetables (broccoli, bell peppers).
diseases.vaccination_info: No specific immunizations are associated with this nutritional deficiency.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent. Pain and bleeding symptoms improve rapidly within days of starting Vitamin C replacement. Bone lesions heal completely.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Pain and gum bleeding resolve within 24 to 72 hours; bone changes resolve on X-ray over several weeks.
diseases.complications: Severe anemia, pathological fractures, subperiosteal hematomas, and secondary bacterial infections.

diseases.name: Kwashiorkor
diseases.affected_age_group: Infants and toddlers (Typically occurs when a child is weaned from breast milk onto a low-protein, high-carbohydrate starch diet)
diseases.causes: Severe deficiency of dietary protein relative to energy intake, often triggered by an infection or illness in a malnourished child.
diseases.pathophysiology: Severe protein deficiency leads to a drop in hepatic synthesis of albumin. The resulting hypoalbuminemia lowers plasma oncotic pressure, causing fluid to shift into the interstitial spaces, leading to pitting edema. Reduced protein also impairs fat transport, causing fatty liver (hepatomegaly), and alters skin and hair pigmentation.
diseases.symptoms_early: Mild weight loss, lethargy, irritability, and decreased muscle mass, which are often masked by fluid retention (swelling).
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Pitting edema starting in the feet and legs, spreading to the hands and face. Skin changes include dry, scaling lesions that peel off, leaving raw patches ("flaky paint dermatosis"). Hair turns thin, dry, and reddish-yellow ("flag sign" showing bands of light and dark hair).
diseases.symptoms_severe: Anasarca (generalized swelling), massive abdominal distension (ascites and hepatomegaly due to fatty liver infiltration), extreme apathy, severe immunodeficiency leading to opportunistic infections, and metabolic collapse.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Clinical diagnosis based on the presence of bilateral pitting edema, growth failure, and typical skin/hair changes. Supportive labs show severe hypoalbuminemia (<2.5 g/dL) and hypoglycemia.
diseases.treatment_plan: This requires careful, step-by-step refeeding to prevent Refeeding Syndrome. Step 1: Stabilize life-threatening issues (treat hypoglycemia, hypothermia, dehydration, and infections). Step 2: Initiate cautious nutritional rehabilitation using specialized milk-based formulas (F-75, then F-100). Step 3: Transition to calorie-dense foods.
diseases.home_care: Home care is only appropriate under strict clinical guidance once the child is stable. Feed the child frequent, small-volume meals. Keep the child warm and monitor for any signs of infection.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Oral Rehydration Solution for Malnutrition (ReSoMal - specialized low-sodium ORS); Potassium chloride and Magnesium sulfate (active electrolytes replaced to prevent refeeding syndrome); Multivitamins including Zinc and Vitamin A.
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Any child showing bilateral swelling of the feet, skin peeling, changes in hair color, or extreme apathy requires immediate emergency medical evaluation.
diseases.prevention: Promote prolonged breastfeeding, introduce protein-rich complementary foods (beans, eggs, fish, nuts) during weaning, and ensure early treatment of childhood infections.
diseases.vaccination_info: Immunizations are critical but may have reduced efficacy in severely malnourished children; avoid live vaccines during the acute stabilization phase.
diseases.prognosis: Variable and dependent on the severity of complications. Mortality remains high (10-20%) in resource-limited settings. Early, structured refeeding carries a good prognosis.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Stabilization phase takes 1 to 7 days; nutritional rehabilitation and catch-up growth take 2 to 6 weeks.
diseases.complications: Hypoglycemia, hypothermia, severe infections, sepsis, electrolyte imbalances, refeeding syndrome, and permanent developmental delay.

diseases.name: Marasmus
diseases.affected_age_group: Infants under 1 year (High risk in infants who are bottle-fed with diluted formula or suffer from chronic diarrhea)
diseases.causes: Severe deprivation of all dietary calories (both protein and carbohydrates) due to inadequate food supply, early weaning, or chronic malabsorption.
diseases.pathophysiology: The body adapts to severe starvation by mobilizing its own tissues for energy. Subcutaneous fat stores are depleted first, followed by catabolism of skeletal muscle protein. This leads to profound wasting, atrophy of the intestinal mucosa, and down-regulation of metabolic rate and immune function.
diseases.symptoms_early: Persistent hunger, crying, slow weight gain or weight loss, and progressive loss of subcutaneous fat.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Extreme muscle wasting and loss of fat, giving the child an "old man" or "senile" facial appearance. The skin hangs in loose folds, especially around the buttocks and thighs ("baggy pants" sign).
diseases.symptoms_severe: Extreme emaciation (weight-for-height $<3$ standard deviations below the mean), hypothermia, bradycardia, severe dehydration, extreme listlessness, and complete apathy.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Clinical diagnosis based on extreme wasting of muscle and fat without edema, and a weight-for-height $<70%$ of the median. Mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) $<11.5	ext{ cm}$ in children aged 6–59 months.
diseases.treatment_plan: Similar to Kwashiorkor, follow the WHO 10-step protocol for severe malnutrition. Focus on initial stabilization: correct hypothermia, hypoglycemia, dehydration (cautiously using ReSoMal to prevent heart failure), and treat infections. Progress to gradual refeeding with F-75/F-100 formulas and Ready-to-Use Therapeutic Food (RUTF).
diseases.home_care: Keep the child warm (skin-to-skin contact, warm blankets) to prevent hypothermia. Feed F-75 formula or RUTF in small, frequent amounts as directed. Monitor temperature and bowel movements.
diseases.medicinal_elements: ReSoMal (specialized rehydration active fluid); Potassium and Magnesium supplements; Amoxicillin or Ampicillin (empiric antibiotics given to all children with severe acute malnutrition due to subclinical infection risk).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Immediate emergency evaluation is required for any infant with severe wasting, loose skin folds, cold hands/feet, or extreme lethargy.
diseases.prevention: Encourage exclusive breastfeeding for the first 6 months of life. Ensure sanitary water supply to prevent diarrhea. Provide nutritional education and support to low-income families.
diseases.vaccination_info: Maintain immunization schedule; severely malnourished children are highly vulnerable to measles, which is frequently fatal.
diseases.prognosis: Good if structured refeeding is followed. Wasted children generally tolerate refeeding better than edematous (Kwashiorkor) children, though recovery takes time.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Stabilization phase takes 1 to 5 days; full nutritional recovery and weight gain require 3 to 8 weeks.
diseases.complications: Hypothermia, hypoglycemia, heart failure (due to fluid overload during rehydration), severe infections, and high mortality.

diseases.name: Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD)
diseases.affected_age_group: Toddlerhood through Adulthood (Signs typically recognizable by 18 to 24 months; more common in boys)
diseases.causes: Complex multifactorial etiology; strong genetic basis (linked to multiple gene mutations) combined with prenatal environmental factors affecting early brain wiring.
diseases.pathophysiology: Altered synaptic pruning and atypical connectivity between brain regions (such as the prefrontal cortex and amygdala) impair the processing of social and sensory information, leading to atypical social interaction and behavioral patterns.
diseases.symptoms_early: Lack of eye contact, failure to respond to their name by 12 months, absence of pointing to show interest by 14 months, and delayed speech development.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Difficulty understanding social cues, lack of shared enjoyment (joint attention), repetitive body movements (hand flapping, rocking), and lining up toys or distress when routines are changed.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Complete absence of functional speech, severe sensory hyper- or hypo-reactivity (extreme distress from everyday sounds), self-injurious behaviors (head banging), and severe social isolation.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Standardized behavioral evaluations including the M-CHAT screening tool at 18/24 months, followed by comprehensive assessment using the ADOS-2 and ADI-R diagnostics.
diseases.treatment_plan: Early and intensive behavioral intervention. Initiate Applied Behavior Analysis (ABA) therapy, along with speech-language and occupational therapy to build communication and life skills. Create an Individualized Education Program (IEP).
diseases.home_care: Maintain highly structured daily routines with visual schedules. Design a low-sensory home environment to prevent sensory overload. Use positive reinforcement to build social skills.
diseases.medicinal_elements: None. No medications treat the core features of ASD. Atypical antipsychotics (e.g., Risperidone) may be used under strict specialist supervision only to manage severe irritability or aggression.
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Consult a pediatrician if your child exhibits any "red flags," such as loss of speech or social skills at any age, lack of eye contact, or repetitive behaviors.
diseases.prevention: No preventative measures exist, as the condition represents a primary neurodevelopmental difference.
diseases.vaccination_info: Vaccines do NOT cause autism. Maintaining the routine childhood immunization schedule is safe and essential.
diseases.prognosis: Variable. Early intervention significantly improves functional outcomes and independence. Many individuals live highly successful, independent lives.
diseases.recovery_timeline: This is a lifelong neurodevelopmental configuration; support strategies are modified as the child grows.
diseases.complications: Language impairment, learning difficulties, anxiety, depression, sensory overload, and social challenges.

diseases.name: Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD)
diseases.affected_age_group: Preschoolers through Adulthood (Typically diagnosed in school-aged children; more common in boys)
diseases.causes: Strong genetic heritability (up to 75-80%) combined with environmental factors like low birth weight, prenatal tobacco/alcohol exposure, or lead exposure.
diseases.pathophysiology: Dysregulation of dopamine and norepinephrine pathways in the prefrontal cortex, which governs executive function, impulse control, and working memory. This leads to an inability to regulate attention and behavior.
diseases.symptoms_early: Extreme restlessness, difficulty participating in quiet group activities, and high impulsivity in preschool years.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Inattention: difficulty sustaining attention, making careless mistakes, not listening, and losing things. Hyperactivity/Impulsivity: fidgeting, running or climbing inappropriately, interrupting others, and inability to wait their turn.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Severe academic failure, social rejection by peers, dangerous risk-taking behaviors, and high family conflict. Symptoms must be present in multiple settings (home and school) and impair function.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Clinical evaluation based on DSM-5 criteria, utilizing standardized rating scales (Vanderbilt or Conners) completed by parents and teachers to document symptoms across settings.
diseases.treatment_plan: A combination of behavioral therapy (parent training in behavior management) and pharmacotherapy. Stimulant medications are highly effective. Provide classroom accommodations (504 Plan or IEP).
diseases.home_care: Create a highly structured home environment with clear rules and consistent routines. Break homework tasks into small, manageable chunks with frequent breaks. Use positive reinforcement charts.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Methylphenidate hydrochloride or Amphetamine mixed salts (generic central nervous system stimulants used to increase prefrontal dopamine and norepinephrine); Atomoxetine (generic non-stimulant active ingredient).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Consult a pediatrician if your child's inattention, hyperactivity, or impulsivity is causing academic struggle, social difficulties, or behavioral issues in school and at home.
diseases.prevention: Avoid prenatal exposure to tobacco, alcohol, and lead; ensure adequate nutrition during early development.
diseases.vaccination_info: No specific immunizations are associated with this neurodevelopmental condition.
diseases.prognosis: Good. Many children manage symptoms successfully with therapy and medication. Up to 50-60% of children continue to experience symptoms into adulthood.
diseases.recovery_timeline: This is a chronic neurodevelopmental condition; management is ongoing throughout childhood and adolescence.
diseases.complications: Academic failure, oppositional defiant disorder, anxiety, depression, low self-esteem, and increased risk of accidents.

diseases.name: Cerebral Palsy (CP)
diseases.affected_age_group: Apparent in Infancy (Lifelong condition; diagnosed by age 1 to 2)
diseases.causes: Prenatal brain injury or abnormal brain development (stroke, infection, genetic factors); perinatal hypoxia or trauma; postnatal meningitis or head injury in early infancy.
diseases.pathophysiology: A non-progressive injury to the developing motor cortex or basal ganglia in the fetal or infant brain. Although the brain injury does not worsen, the clinical manifestation (muscle tone, posture, movement) changes as the child grows.
diseases.symptoms_early: Delay in reaching motor milestones (head control, sitting, crawling). The infant may feel unusually stiff (spastic) or floppy (hypotonic) when held.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Atypical motor patterns: crawling with legs dragging (commando crawl), walking on toes with a scissoring gait, or preferential use of one side of the body (hemiplegia).
diseases.symptoms_severe: Spastic Quadriplegia: severe stiffness in all four limbs, inability to sit or walk independently, scoliosis, joint contractures, dysphagia (swallowing difficulty), dysarthria, and associated seizures or cognitive impairment.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Clinical diagnosis based on serial motor exams showing persistent primitive reflexes and abnormal muscle tone. Brain MRI is performed to identify the site and extent of the brain lesion.
diseases.treatment_plan: Multidisciplinary rehabilitation. Provide physical, occupational, and speech therapy to optimize motor function and independence. Manage spasticity with muscle relaxants or orthopedic surgeries to release tight tendons.
diseases.home_care: Perform daily stretching and range-of-motion exercises to prevent joint contractures. Use adaptive equipment (braces, walkers, communication devices) consistently. Support nutrition if feeding is difficult.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Baclofen (generic active muscle relaxant used to reduce spasticity); Diazepam (sometimes used for severe muscle spasms); Botulinum Toxin Type A (injected locally into hypertonic muscles to reduce spasticity).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Seek pediatric evaluation if your infant has asymmetric movements, feels unusually stiff or floppy, or has not achieved head control by 4 months or sat independently by 9 months.
diseases.prevention: Ensure high-quality prenatal care, manage maternal infections, administer magnesium sulfate to mothers in preterm labor to protect the fetal brain, and prevent infant head trauma.
diseases.vaccination_info: Ensure all routine vaccines are up to date, as respiratory infections can be severe in children with motor impairments.
diseases.prognosis: Variable and dependent on the severity of the motor impairment. Many individuals lead active, productive lives with normal lifespans. Severe quadriplegia requires lifelong coordinate care.
diseases.recovery_timeline: This is a lifelong, non-progressive motor condition; therapeutic support is adjusted as development progresses.
diseases.complications: Joint contractures, hip subluxation, scoliosis, chronic pain, malnutrition (from dysphagia), aspiration pneumonia, and dental issues.

diseases.name: Developmental Coordination Disorder (DCD)
diseases.affected_age_group: Early Childhood through Adolescence (Typically recognized around 5 to 7 years when entering school)
diseases.causes: Unknown etiology; believed to involve atypical cerebellar function or impaired integration of visual and motor signals in the brain.
diseases.pathophysiology: Impaired planning and execution of motor tasks. The brain struggles to coordinate muscle groups and utilize sensory feedback to refine movements, making the acquisition of both gross and fine motor skills difficult.
diseases.symptoms_early: Delay in early motor milestones (sitting, crawling, walking). The child may appear unusually clumsy, frequently bumping into objects or dropping things.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Difficulty with gross motor skills like riding a bicycle, jumping, catching a ball, or running. Fine motor struggles include difficulty using scissors, buttoning a shirt, tying shoes, and poor handwriting (dysgraphia).
diseases.symptoms_severe: Significant academic struggle due to slow and illegible writing, avoidance of physical activities leading to obesity, low self-esteem, social isolation, and secondary anxiety.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Clinical diagnosis based on DSM-5 criteria: motor skills are substantially below expectation for age and impair daily activities, symptoms began in early childhood, and are not due to another neurological condition (like CP or muscular dystrophy).
diseases.treatment_plan: Focused physical and occupational therapy. Use the Cognitive Orientation to daily Occupational Performance (CO-OP) approach to help the child problem-solve motor tasks. Provide classroom accommodations (extra writing time, use of a keyboard).
diseases.home_care: Encourage non-competitive physical activities (swimming, martial arts) to build strength and coordination. Break tasks into simple steps and practice them in a positive environment. Support self-esteem.
diseases.medicinal_elements: None. This is a motor learning disorder; no pharmacological treatments are indicated.
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Consult a pediatrician if your school-aged child appears unusually clumsy, struggles significantly with handwriting, or cannot tie their shoes or ride a bike compared to peers.
diseases.prevention: No preventative measures are known, as the underlying neurological mechanism remains unclassified.
diseases.vaccination_info: No specific immunizations are associated with this motor learning disorder.
diseases.prognosis: Good. Many children learn coping strategies and improve coordination with occupational therapy, though some motor difficulties may persist into adulthood.
diseases.recovery_timeline: This is a lifelong neurodevelopmental configuration; therapy focuses on functional adaptation and skill acquisition.
diseases.complications: Low self-esteem, anxiety, depression, school failure, and obesity due to avoidance of physical activity.

diseases.name: Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL)
diseases.classification: Malignant Proliferation of Lymphoid Progenitor Cells
diseases.affected_age_group: 2 to 5 Years (Peak incidence, though occurs throughout childhood; most common pediatric cancer)
diseases.causes: Somatically acquired genetic mutations in lymphoid progenitor cells, altering cell division and maturation; associated with syndromes like Down syndrome.
diseases.pathophysiology: Malignant transformation of a lymphoid progenitor cell (typically a B-cell precursor) leads to uncontrolled clonal expansion. These immature lymphoblasts accumulate in the bone marrow, crowding out normal hematopoiesis. This results in pancytopenia (anemia, thrombocytopenia, neutropenia) and infiltration of external organs (spleen, liver, lymph nodes, CNS).
diseases.symptoms_early: Vague, flu-like symptoms including persistent fatigue, progressive pallor, loss of appetite, and a low-grade fever.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Easy bruising, petechiae, recurrent epistaxis, bone pain (often causing a limp or refusal to walk, especially at night), and generalized painless lymphadenopathy (swollen lymph nodes in neck, armpits, groin).
diseases.symptoms_severe: High fever, severe infections (due to neutropenia), hepatosplenomegaly causing abdominal distension, testicular swelling, and signs of increased intracranial pressure (headache, vomiting), indicating CNS involvement.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Complete blood count showing anemia, thrombocytopenia, and presence of lymphoblasts. Confirmed by a Bone Marrow Aspiration and biopsy showing $ge 20%$ lymphoblasts, with immunophenotyping (flow cytometry) to determine lineage.
diseases.treatment_plan: Standardized multi-agent chemotherapy protocols divided into phases: Induction (to achieve remission), Consolidation, and Maintenance therapy. CNS prophylaxis with intrathecal chemotherapy is mandatory. Supportive care with transfusions and antibiotics.
diseases.home_care: This condition requires immediate specialist oncology hospitalization. Post-discharge home care focuses on strict hygiene to prevent infection, avoiding sick contacts, managing chemotherapy side effects, and monitoring for fever.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Vincristine sulfate or Methotrexate (generic chemotherapy active ingredients); Prednisone or Dexamethasone (corticosteroid active ingredients used in leukemia protocols); Mercaptopurine (active maintenance chemotherapy).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Any child presenting with unexplained bruising, bone pain that wakes them at night, progressive pallor, or a fever that does not resolve requires immediate pediatric evaluation.
diseases.prevention: No preventative measures exist, as the genetic mutations are somatic and randomly acquired.
diseases.vaccination_info: Avoid all live viral vaccines (MMR, Varicella, Rotavirus) while the child is undergoing immunosuppressive chemotherapy; household contacts should be fully immunized.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent. The 5-year survival rate for pediatric B-cell ALL exceeds 90% with modern risk-directed chemotherapy protocols.
diseases.recovery_timeline: The total duration of chemotherapy treatment is typically 2 to 2.5 years; recovery of normal bone marrow function occurs within months of completing therapy.
diseases.complications: Tumor lysis syndrome, febrile neutropenia, severe infections, bleeding, chemotherapy-induced toxicity (cardiotoxicity, neuropathy), and secondary malignancies.
diseases.overview: Malignant Proliferation of Lymphoid Progenitor Cells

diseases.name: Hodgkin Lymphoma (Pediatric)
diseases.affected_age_group: Adolescents (Rare under 5 years; peak incidence in the teenage years)
diseases.causes: Unknown etiology; associated with Epstein-Barr Virus (EBV) infection and genetic predisposition factors.
diseases.pathophysiology: Malignant clonal B-cells (Reed-Sternberg cells) arise in a lymph node. These cells secrete cytokines that recruit a massive, non-malignant inflammatory cell infiltrate (lymphocytes, macrophages, eosinophils), leading to painless enlargement of the lymph node and systemic symptoms.
diseases.symptoms_early: Painless, firm, rubbery enlargement of a single lymph node group, most commonly in the cervical (neck) or supraclavicular area.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: The "B Symptoms": unexplained fevers ($>38^circ	ext{C}$), night sweats (soaking the sheets), and unexplained weight loss ($>10%$ of body weight over 6 months). Pruritus (itching) can also occur.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Mediastinal mass: a large tumor in the chest causing a persistent dry cough, shortness of breath, or superior vena cava syndrome (swelling of the face and neck). Splenomegaly and hepatomegaly.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Excisional lymph node biopsy showing the classic binucleated Reed-Sternberg cells ("owl-eye" appearance). Staging is performed using PET/CT scans and bone marrow biopsy.
diseases.treatment_plan: Risk-adapted chemotherapy protocols, often combined with low-dose involved-field radiation therapy. The goal is to maximize cure rates while minimizing long-term radiation toxicities.
diseases.home_care: Monitor for fever, especially during chemotherapy cycles. Ensure a high-calorie, nutritious diet to manage weight loss. Support psychological needs during adolescent cancer therapy.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Doxorubicin, Bleomycin, Vinblastine, and Dacarbazine (ABVD chemotherapy regimen active ingredients); Prednisone (steroid active ingredient).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Consult a doctor if your child has a swollen lymph node that is painless and does not shrink after 2-3 weeks, or if they develop unexplained night sweats, weight loss, or persistent fevers.
diseases.prevention: No preventative measures exist, as the primary trigger remains unclassified.
diseases.vaccination_info: Avoid live vaccines during and for 6-12 months after completing chemotherapy; maintain pneumococcal and influenza vaccines.
diseases.prognosis: Outstanding. The cure rate for pediatric and adolescent Hodgkin lymphoma exceeds 95% with modern staging and risk-directed therapy.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Chemotherapy treatment typically spans 2 to 6 months depending on the stage; full physical recovery takes several months.
diseases.complications: Secondary malignancies (breast cancer, leukemia later in life), radiation-induced thyroid dysfunction, pulmonary fibrosis (from bleomycin), and cardiotoxicity.

diseases.name: Neuroblastoma
diseases.classification: Malignant Embryonal Tumor of the Sympathetic Nervous System
diseases.affected_age_group: Infants and toddlers (Median age of diagnosis is 19 months; most common solid extracranial tumor in children)
diseases.causes: Primarily idiopathic embryonal origin; arising from primordial neural crest cells. Associated with mutations in the ALK gene in rare familial cases.
diseases.pathophysiology: Abnormal differentiation and migration of neural crest cells during fetal development. These immature sympathetic cells fail to mature and instead undergo malignant clonal expansion, forming a tumor anywhere along the sympathetic chain (most commonly in the adrenal medulla). Tumor cells secrete catecholamines.
diseases.symptoms_early: Vague symptoms depending on tumor location: mild abdominal discomfort, poor appetite, and low-grade fever.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: A firm, irregular, painless abdominal mass that crosses the midline (adrenal tumor). Hypertension (due to catecholamine release or renal artery compression) and diarrhea.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Metastatic disease signs: "raccoon eyes" (periorbital bruising/ecchymosis due to orbital bone metastasis), bone pain, bone marrow suppression (anemia, bruising), and "blueberry muffin" skin lesions in infants.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Elevated urinary catecholamine metabolites (HVA and VMA). Confirmed by tumor biopsy showing small, round, blue cells. MIBG scan to map metastatic bone lesions. Genetic evaluation showing MYCN amplification (predicts aggressive disease).
diseases.treatment_plan: Risk-stratified approach. Low-risk tumors (especially in infants $<18$ months) may require surgery only or even undergo spontaneous regression. High-risk disease requires multi-agent chemotherapy, surgical resection, autologous stem cell transplant, radiation, and immunotherapy.
diseases.home_care: Support nutrition during aggressive chemotherapy. Monitor blood counts and check for fevers. Provide gentle skin care and manage pain.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Cyclophosphamide or Cisplatin (generic active alkylating/platinum chemotherapy agents); Doxorubicin (generic active anthracycline chemotherapy); Dinutuximab (active immunotherapy monoclonal antibody).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Consult a pediatrician if you feel a hard lump in your child's abdomen, or if they develop unexplained bruising around their eyes or refuse to walk due to bone pain.
diseases.prevention: No preventative measures exist, as this is an embryonal developmental tumor.
diseases.vaccination_info: Avoid live vaccines during active immunosuppressive oncology treatments.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent for low- and intermediate-risk disease (survival $>90%$). High-risk disease (especially with MYCN amplification) has a guarded prognosis, with survival around 50% despite intensive therapy.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Low-risk surgery recovery takes weeks; high-risk multi-modality therapy spans 12 to 18 months.
diseases.complications: Metastasis, spinal cord compression (from tumor extending into the spinal canal), hearing loss (from cisplatin), and renal impairment.
diseases.overview: Malignant Embryonal Tumor of the Sympathetic Nervous System

diseases.name: Nephroblastoma (Wilms Tumor)
diseases.affected_age_group: 2 to 5 Years (Peak occurrence; rare after 8 years)
diseases.causes: Abnormal renal development involving somatic mutations in the WT1 gene on chromosome 11; associated with syndromes like WAGR, Beckwith-Wiedemann, and Denys-Drash.
diseases.pathophysiology: Malignant proliferation of embryonal renal cells (metanephric blastema) that fail to differentiate into normal glomeruli and tubules. The tumor grows rapidly within the renal parenchyma, compressing normal kidney tissue but remaining encapsulated initially.
diseases.symptoms_early: A smooth, firm, painless abdominal mass that does not cross the midline, often discovered by parents during bathing or dressing the child.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Mild abdominal pain, hematuria (blood in the urine), low-grade fever, and decreased appetite.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Severe hypertension (due to increased renin release from renal ischemia), significant weight loss, and shortness of breath (suggesting pulmonary metastasis). The abdomen should never be palpated vigorously, as it can rupture the tumor capsule and spill tumor cells.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Abdominal ultrasound showing a solid renal mass. Confirmed with CT or MRI of the abdomen and chest (to screen for lung metastasis). Definitive diagnosis made at surgery (nephrectomy).
diseases.treatment_plan: Surgical removal of the affected kidney (nephrectomy), followed by adjuvant chemotherapy. Radiation therapy is added for advanced stages or unfavorable histology. Never perform a pre-operative biopsy to avoid tumor spillage.
diseases.home_care: Protect the child from abdominal trauma (no rough contact play) prior to surgery to prevent tumor rupture. Post-surgery, monitor urine output and track kidney function indicators.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Vincristine sulfate and Dactinomycin (standard chemotherapy active ingredients for Wilms tumor); Doxorubicin (added for advanced stages).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Consult a pediatrician immediately if you feel a lump or swelling in your child's abdomen, or if you notice blood in their urine.
diseases.prevention: Children with known predisposing syndromes (e.g., Beckwith-Wiedemann) require screening abdominal ultrasounds every 3 months until age 8.
diseases.vaccination_info: Avoid live vaccines during active chemotherapy; maintain all other vaccines post-recovery.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent. The overall cure rate for pediatric Wilms tumor exceeds 90% with modern surgical and chemotherapy protocols.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Post-nephrectomy recovery takes 1 to 2 weeks; adjuvant chemotherapy spans 18 weeks to 6 months depending on the stage.
diseases.complications: Tumor rupture/spill, surgical injury to the renal vessels, chemotherapy toxicity, and chronic kidney disease in bilateral cases.

diseases.name: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome (RDS)
diseases.affected_age_group: Premature Neonates (Incidence increases with decreasing gestational age; rare in term infants)
diseases.causes: Developmental insufficiency of surfactant production by type II alveolar cells in the lungs of premature infants.
diseases.pathophysiology: Surfactant reduces surface tension within the alveoli, keeping them open during expiration. Surfactant deficiency leads to widespread alveolar collapse (atelectasis) at the end of expiration. This results in poor lung compliance, intrapulmonary shunting, hypoxia, and the formation of hyaline membranes in the alveoli.
diseases.symptoms_early: Tachypnea (rapid breathing $>60	ext{ breaths/minute}$), nasal flaring, and expiratory grunting presenting immediately at birth or within minutes of delivery.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Intercostal and subcostal retractions, paradoxical chest-abdominal motion ("seesaw breathing"), and audible grunting on every expiration.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Cyanosis (bluish tint) on room air, severe retractions, periods of apnea, hypotonia, and progressive respiratory failure.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Chest X-ray showing a characteristic diffuse "ground-glass" appearance and air bronchograms due to micro-atelectasis. Arterial blood gas demonstrating hypoxemia and respiratory acidosis.
diseases.treatment_plan: Administer exogenous surfactant directly into the trachea immediately after birth or when distress develops. Provide respiratory support using Continuous Positive Airway Pressure (CPAP) or mechanical ventilation. Maintain temperature and hydration.
diseases.home_care: Home care is strictly not applicable. This is a critical neonatal ICU emergency.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Poractant alfa or Calfactant (generic active exogenous surfactant ingredients derived from animal lungs); Caffeine citrate (active stimulant used to prevent apnea of prematurity).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Any newborn showing rapid breathing, grunting, or chest pulling in the delivery room or nursery requires immediate neonatal resuscitation and transfer to a NICU.
diseases.prevention: Administer prenatal corticosteroids (Betamethasone) to mothers in preterm labor (between 24-34 weeks gestation) to accelerate fetal lung maturation and surfactant production.
diseases.vaccination_info: No specific immunizations are associated with this developmental neonatal disease.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent with modern surfactant therapy and CPAP; mortality is low in babies born $>28$ weeks. Extremely premature infants remain at risk for chronic lung disease.
diseases.recovery_timeline: RDS typically peaks at 48 to 72 hours of life; gradual recovery occurs over 4 to 7 days as the infant's lungs begin producing native surfactant.
diseases.complications: Bronchopulmonary dysplasia (chronic lung disease), air leaks (pneumothorax, pulmonary interstitial emphysema), intraventricular hemorrhage, and patent ductus arteriosus.

diseases.name: Neonatal Jaundice (Hyperbilirubinemia)
diseases.affected_age_group: Neonates (Common in the first week of life; affects up to 60% of term and 80% of preterm infants)
diseases.causes: Physiological: immature liver enzyme (UGT1A1) and high red cell mass. Pathological: ABO/Rh blood group incompatibility, G6PD deficiency, or breast milk jaundice.
diseases.pathophysiology: Bilirubin is produced by the breakdown of senescent red blood cells. Newborns have high red cell volume and short red cell lifespans. The newborn's liver cannot conjugate this massive load of unconjugated (indirect) bilirubin for excretion. Unconjugated bilirubin is lipid-soluble and can cross the blood-brain barrier.
diseases.symptoms_early: Yellow discoloration of the sclera (white of the eyes) and face, typically appearing on day 2 or 3 of life.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: The yellow tint spreads downward (cephalocaudal progression) to the chest, abdomen, and extremities as bilirubin levels rise.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Acute Bilirubin Encephalopathy (Kernicterus): marked by extreme lethargy, poor feeding (difficulty sucking), high-pitched crying, hypotonia transitioning to hypertonia (arching of the back/opisthotonos), and seizures.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Transcutaneous bilirubin (TcB) screening. Confirmed by measuring Total Serum Bilirubin (TSB) levels. Direct Coombs test to check for maternal antibody coating.
diseases.treatment_plan: Determine risk using hour-specific bilirubin nomograms. Initiate Phototherapy (exposure to blue-green light that photo-isomerizes bilirubin into water-soluble lumirubin for excretion). Ensure frequent feedings. Exchange transfusion for critical levels.
diseases.home_care: Feed the newborn frequently (every 2-3 hours) to promote bowel movements, which clears bilirubin. Monitor the number of wet and dirty diapers. Place the baby near a window for indirect sunlight if levels are borderline (under clinical guidance).
diseases.medicinal_elements: None. No primary medications are used. Phototherapy is the standard treatment; intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) may be used in severe hemolytic disease.
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Seek pediatric evaluation if jaundice appears in the first 24 hours of life, if the yellow color reaches the baby's abdomen or legs, or if the baby is lethargic and feeding poorly.
diseases.prevention: Ensure successful initiation of breastfeeding with 8-12 feedings daily. Check maternal blood type and Rh status during pregnancy.
diseases.vaccination_info: No specific immunizations are associated with this metabolic clearance dysfunction.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent. Phototherapy is highly effective and prevents brain damage. Kernicterus is extremely rare with modern screening protocols.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Physiological jaundice peaks on day 3-5 and resolves by day 7-10; breast milk jaundice can persist for 3 to 12 weeks.
diseases.complications: Kernicterus (permanent brain damage causing athetoid cerebral palsy, hearing loss, and dental defects) if bilirubin levels rise unchecked.

diseases.name: Necrotizing Enterocolitis (NEC)
diseases.classification: Acute Ischemic & Inflammatory Neonatal Intestinal Necrosis
diseases.affected_age_group: Premature Neonates (Typically presents in the second or third week of life in the NICU)
diseases.causes: Multifactorial; intestinal immaturity, mucosal ischemia, early enteral formula feeding, and abnormal bacterial colonization of the gut.
diseases.pathophysiology: The immature gut barrier of a preterm infant is highly vulnerable to injury. Enteral feeding provides a substrate for bacterial proliferation. An ischemic or inflammatory trigger damages the mucosal lining, allowing bacteria to invade the intestinal wall. This leads to gas production within the wall (pneumatosis intestinalis), necrosis, and potential perforation.
diseases.symptoms_early: Feeding intolerance: delayed gastric emptying (large stomach residuals), mild abdominal distension, and decreased bowel sounds.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Significant abdominal distension, abdominal tenderness, erythema (redness) of the abdominal wall, and stools containing occult or gross blood.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Pneumoperitoneum (free air in the abdomen due to intestinal perforation), abdominal rigidity, septic shock (bradycardia, hypotension, apnea), disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC), and death.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Plain abdominal X-ray (KUB) showing the diagnostic "pneumatosis intestinalis" (gas bubbles in the bowel wall) or portal venous gas. Free air under the diaphragm indicates perforation.
diseases.treatment_plan: Make the infant strictly NPO (nothing by mouth). Insert a nasogastric tube for gastric decompression. Initiate broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics. Monitor abdominal X-rays frequently. Surgical intervention (peritoneal drain or laparotomy with bowel resection) is indicated for perforation or clinical deterioration.
diseases.home_care: Home care is strictly not applicable. This is a critical neonatal ICU emergency.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Ampicillin, Gentamicin, and Metronidazole (intravenous antibiotic active ingredients used to cover enteric aerobic and anaerobic bacteria); Intravenous total parenteral nutrition (TPN) components.
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Any premature infant showing abdominal distension, vomiting, or blood in their stool requires immediate emergency neonatal evaluation and NPO status.
diseases.prevention: Prioritize breast milk feeding (exclusively human milk reduces NEC risk by 50-80% compared to formula). Implement cautious, standardized feeding protocols.
diseases.vaccination_info: No specific immunizations are associated with this inflammatory bowel disease.
diseases.prognosis: Variable. NEC is a major cause of mortality in the NICU (15-30% mortality). Survivors of surgical resection are at risk for long-term gut complications.
diseases.recovery_timeline: NPO status and antibiotic therapy are maintained for 7 to 14 days; surgical recovery and re-feeding span several weeks or months.
diseases.complications: Intestinal perforation, peritonitis, sepsis, short bowel syndrome (due to extensive bowel resection), and intestinal strictures.
diseases.overview: Acute Ischemic & Inflammatory Neonatal Intestinal Necrosis

diseases.name: Neonatal Sepsis
diseases.affected_age_group: Neonates (Early-onset: first 72 hours; Late-onset: 7 to 28 days)
diseases.causes: Group B Streptococcus (GBS), Escherichia coli, and Listeria monocytogenes (Early-onset). Staphylococcus aureus and Coagulase-negative Staphylococci (Late-onset).
diseases.pathophysiology: Pathogens are acquired vertically from the maternal birth canal during delivery (early-onset) or horizontally from the environment/healthcare workers (late-onset). The newborn's immature immune system (lack of IgG and functional neutrophils) fails to localize the infection, leading to rapid bacterial dissemination and systemic inflammatory response.
diseases.symptoms_early: Vague and subtle: temperature instability (hypothermia $<36^circ	ext{C}$ or fever $>38^circ	ext{C}$), mild tachypnea, poor feeding, and irritability.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Progressive lethargy, respiratory distress (grunting, retractions), apnea, vomiting, abdominal distension, and prolonged capillary refill time.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Septic shock: hypotension, cold mottled skin, severe bradycardia or tachycardia, petechiae, disseminated intravascular coagulation, seizures, and coma.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Complete "Sepsis Workup" including blood culture, urine culture (for late-onset), and Lumbar Puncture (CSF culture to rule out meningitis). Elevated C-reactive protein (CRP) and abnormal CBC (neutropenia or high immature-to-total neutrophil ratio).
diseases.treatment_plan: Initiate immediate empirical intravenous broad-spectrum antibiotics (Ampicillin plus Gentamicin) after obtaining cultures. Provide cardiorespiratory support (intravenous fluids, oxygen, vasopressors).
diseases.home_care: Home care is strictly not applicable. Immediate emergency hospitalization and critical care monitoring are required.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Ampicillin (generic penicillin active targeting GBS and Listeria); Gentamicin (generic aminoglycoside active targeting Gram-negative enteric rods like E. coli).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Any newborn with a rectal temperature $<36^circ	ext{C}$ or $ge 38^circ	ext{C}$, or showing lethargy, poor feeding, or rapid breathing requires immediate emergency evaluation.
diseases.prevention: Screen all pregnant women for Group B Streptococcus (GBS) colonization at 36-37 weeks gestation. Administer intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis (intravenous Penicillin) during labor to GBS-positive women.
diseases.vaccination_info: No specific immunizations are associated with early neonatal sepsis; maternal immunization (e.g., Tdap, RSV) supports passive immunity.
diseases.prognosis: Good if antibiotics are started early. Untreated neonatal sepsis is highly fatal, and mortality remains significant in extremely premature infants.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Antibiotic therapy is maintained for 7 to 10 days for sepsis, and 14 to 21 days if meningitis is documented.
diseases.complications: Meningitis, septic shock, disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC), multi-organ failure, and permanent neurological disabilities.

diseases.name: Pediatric Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD)
diseases.affected_age_group: School-aged children and adolescents (onset typically after 6 years)
diseases.causes: Genetic predisposition combined with chronic stress, early trauma, and imbalances in GABA and serotonin neurotransmission.
diseases.pathophysiology: Hyperactivation of the amygdala and dysregulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis lead to sustained elevated cortisol release. This suppresses prefrontal cortex executive control, manifesting as unremitting, non-specific cognitive worry and physiological muscle tension.
diseases.symptoms_early: School avoidance, fatigue, restlessness, difficulty concentrating, and somatic complaints like chronic stomach aches.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Irrational, pervasive worries about daily schedules, sleep disturbances (difficulty falling asleep), muscle tension, and irritability.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Panic attacks, heart palpitations, complete refusal to speak or attend school (school phobia), and severe social withdrawal.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Clinical psychiatric evaluation using DSM-5 diagnostic criteria, supported by child/parent anxiety rating scales (SCARED or GAD-7).
diseases.treatment_plan: First-line therapy is Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) focusing on exposure and coping skills. For severe, non-responsive cases, select selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors under strict pediatric monitoring.
diseases.home_care: Maintain stable, predictable daily schedules. Practice relaxation breathing exercises. Work closely with school counselors to develop a supportive 504 plan.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Fluoxetine or Sertraline (generic selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor active ingredients used only under expert guidance for severe cases).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Seek pediatric mental health evaluation if school refusal exceeds a week, if physical somatic symptoms (stomach aches) prevent daily activity, or if panic attacks develop.
diseases.prevention: Cultivate supportive parenting environments, promote early resilience education, and ensure prompt treatment of early childhood stressors.
diseases.vaccination_info: No specific immunizations are associated with this psychiatric condition.
diseases.prognosis: Good. Up to 70% of children respond well to early CBT intervention. If untreated, it commonly transitions into adult anxiety and mood disorders.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Therapy typically spans 12 to 24 weeks; long-term progress requires ongoing cognitive reinforcement.
diseases.complications: Depression, social isolation, academic failure, and substance abuse in adolescence.

diseases.name: Pediatric Major Depressive Disorder (MDD)
diseases.affected_age_group: Adolescents (peak incidence), though can present in younger school-aged children
diseases.causes: Genetic susceptibility, psychosocial stressors (bullying, abuse), and functional depletion of serotonin, norepinephrine, and dopamine.
diseases.pathophysiology: Reduced synaptic concentrations of monoamine neurotransmitters in the limbic system and prefrontal cortex, accompanied by decreased expression of Brain-Derived Neurotrophic Factor (BDNF), causing reduced neuroplasticity and hippocampal atrophy.
diseases.symptoms_early: Social withdrawal from friends and family, persistent irritability (rather than classic sadness in children), and loss of interest in play.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Frequent crying spells, significant decline in school performance, altered sleep (hypersomnia or insomnia), and marked changes in appetite/weight.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Expressing feelings of worthlessness, self-harm behaviors (cutting), severe psychomotor retardation, and active suicidal ideation or suicide attempts.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Comprehensive clinical interview with child and parent based on DSM-5 criteria, utilizing screening tools like PHQ-A or Beck Depression Inventory.
diseases.treatment_plan: Multidisciplinary approach. Initiate immediate psychotherapeutic counseling (CBT or Interpersonal Therapy). Pharmacotherapy is added for moderate-to-severe depression, monitored closely for side effects.
diseases.home_care: Create a highly secure, supportive home environment. Remove access to lethal means (medications, sharp objects). Encourage light physical activity and structured sleep schedules.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Fluoxetine hydrochloride or Escitalopram (generic selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors approved for pediatric MDD).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Any talk of wanting to die, self-harm, or severe withdrawal where the child refuses to get out of bed requires immediate psychiatric emergency evaluation.
diseases.prevention: Early intervention for childhood trauma, anti-bullying school support systems, and regular pediatric screening for at-risk cohorts.
diseases.vaccination_info: No specific immunizations are associated with this psychiatric condition.
diseases.prognosis: Variable. Up to 80% recover from their first depressive episode with combined therapy and meds, but recurrence rates are high (up to 50% in 5 years).
diseases.recovery_timeline: Response to medication takes 4 to 6 weeks; therapy continues for at least 6 to 12 months post-remission.
diseases.complications: Suicide, self-injurious behaviors, academic drop-out, and social functional impairment.

diseases.name: Sensory Processing Disorder (SPD)
diseases.affected_age_group: Toddlers and preschool-aged children (usually diagnosed before age 5)
diseases.causes: Unknown etiology; likely involves atypical neural connectivity and genetic factors influencing sensory gating in the central nervous system.
diseases.pathophysiology: Inability of the central nervous system (primarily the thalamus and parietal lobes) to correctly modulate, integrate, and organize sensory inputs (tactile, auditory, vestibular). This leads to sensory hyper-reactivity (avoidance) or sensory hypo-reactivity (seeking).
diseases.symptoms_early: Dislike of tags on clothing, extreme distress during haircuts, and sensitivity to common sounds like vacuum cleaners.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Severe tantrums in crowded environments, food refusal based entirely on texture, and poor fine-motor coordination.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Complete meltdowns triggered by sensory overload, running away from sensory triggers into dangerous situations, and inability to engage in normal peer play.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Occupational therapy evaluation utilizing standardized sensory profiling tools (Sensory Integration and Praxis Tests).
diseases.treatment_plan: Occupational Therapy (OT) utilizing a sensory integration framework. Create a custom "Sensory Diet" of sensory-motor activities to help regulate the child's nervous system.
diseases.home_care: Remove tags from clothing, offer noise-canceling headphones for loud places, and establish a quiet "sensory corner" at home for decompression.
diseases.medicinal_elements: None. Pharmacotherapy is not indicated for the core features of sensory processing disorder.
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Consult an occupational therapist if sensory sensitivities cause daily meltdowns, severe food restriction, or prevent attendance at preschool.
diseases.prevention: No known preventative measures exist, as the condition represents a neurodevelopmental variation.
diseases.vaccination_info: No specific immunizations are associated with this neurodevelopmental condition.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent with early occupational therapy. Children learn coping mechanisms and adapt well as they grow.
diseases.recovery_timeline: OT therapy is tailored individually, typically showing functional progress over 3 to 6 months of weekly sessions.
diseases.complications: Severe behavioral issues, feeding disorders, motor delays, and social isolation.

diseases.name: Oppositional Defiant Disorder (ODD)
diseases.affected_age_group: Preschool through middle childhood (typically diagnosed before age 8)
diseases.causes: Combination of biological factors (low autonomic reactivity/low heart rate), temperamental factors, and inconsistent or harsh parenting practices.
diseases.pathophysiology: Deficits in prefrontal cortex executive functioning and altered amygdala-frontal connectivity impair the child's ability to regulate anger, assess consequences, and interpret social cues, resulting in oppositional behaviors.
diseases.symptoms_early: Frequent, intense temper tantrums and arguing with parents or caregivers.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Active defiance of rules, deliberately annoying others, blaming others for mistakes, and touchy/easily annoyed temperament.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Vindictiveness (spiteful acts), open verbal aggression at school, complete defiance of school authority leading to suspension.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Clinical psychiatric evaluation based on DSM-5 criteria, documenting a persistent pattern of angry/irritable mood and argumentative behavior for at least 6 months.
diseases.treatment_plan: Parent Management Training (PMT) to establish consistent discipline. Parent-Child Interaction Therapy (PCIT). Individual cognitive problem-solving therapy for the child.
diseases.home_care: Establish clear, consistent household rules and immediate positive reinforcement for compliance. Avoid power struggles; remain calm during episodes of defiance.
diseases.medicinal_elements: None. No specific medications exist for ODD, though comorbid conditions like ADHD or anxiety may be treated pharmacologically.
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Seek professional evaluation if behavioral defiance causes school suspension, family dysfunction, or if physical aggression begins to develop.
diseases.prevention: Early childhood parenting education, stable home environments, and early intervention for childhood emotional dysregulation.
diseases.vaccination_info: No specific immunizations are associated with this behavioral condition.
diseases.prognosis: Variable. Early behavioral therapy carries a good prognosis. If untreated, it can progress to Conduct Disorder and adult antisocial personality traits.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Behavioral intervention requires consistent reinforcement over 6 to 12 months to achieve stable behavioral changes.
diseases.complications: Conduct disorder, academic failure, substance abuse, and severe conflict within the family.

diseases.name: Prader-Willi Syndrome (PWS)
diseases.affected_age_group: Neonates through adulthood (recognized at birth due to hypotonia)
diseases.causes: Loss of active paternal genes in the 15q11-q13 region on chromosome 15 (due to deletion, maternal uniparental disomy, or imprinting defect).
diseases.pathophysiology: Loss of these active genes disrupts hypothalamic development, leading to hypothalamic dysfunction. This causes growth hormone deficiency, hypogonadism, and a severe dysregulation of satiety mechanisms (hyperphagia).
diseases.symptoms_early: Severe neonatal hypotonia ("floppy baby"), weak sucking reflex requiring tube feeding, and developmental delays.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Onset of hyperphagia (insatiable, obsessive hunger) between ages 2 and 8, rapid weight gain, short stature, and small hands/feet.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Extreme morbid obesity leading to cardiovascular strain, sleep apnea, type 2 diabetes, hypogonadism (undeveloped sex organs), and behavioral outbursts.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: DNA methylation testing showing maternal-only imprint in the 15q11-q13 region. Confirmed with FISH or chromosomal microarray.
diseases.treatment_plan: Initiate recombinant human growth hormone therapy in infancy to improve muscle tone and growth. Enforce strict restriction of access to food. Address behavioral outbursts.
diseases.home_care: Lock kitchen cabinets and refrigerators. Enforce a strict low-calorie, portion-controlled diet. Encourage daily physical activity and maintain highly predictable routines.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Somatropin (recombinant human growth hormone active ingredient to support growth and lean muscle mass).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Consult a geneticist immediately if a newborn exhibits severe hypotonia, or if a toddler shows sudden, uncontrollable food-seeking behaviors and rapid weight gain.
diseases.prevention: No primary prevention exists, as the chromosomal abnormality is a random de novo imprinting or deletion event.
diseases.vaccination_info: Follow standard pediatric immunization schedules.
diseases.prognosis: Good if obesity is successfully prevented. Individuals require lifelong supervision regarding food access and daily living.
diseases.recovery_timeline: This is a lifelong genetic chromosomal disorder; management is continuous and daily.
diseases.complications: Morbid obesity, type 2 diabetes, sleep apnea, cor pulmonale, scoliosis, and severe behavioral problems.

diseases.name: Fragile X Syndrome (FXS)
diseases.classification: FMR1 Gene Trinucleotide Repeat Expansion Syndrome
diseases.affected_age_group: Toddlers through adulthood (diagnosed as speech and social delays appear)
diseases.causes: Trinucleotide CGG repeat expansion (>200 repeats) in the FMR1 gene on the X chromosome, leading to transcriptional gene silencing.
diseases.pathophysiology: Hypermethylation of the expanded FMR1 gene shuts off production of Fragile X Mental Retardation Protein (FMRP). FMRP is a critical RNA-binding protein that regulates translation at synapses; its absence disrupts synaptic plasticity and brain wiring.
diseases.symptoms_early: Delays in early motor milestones (sitting, walking), hypotonia, and delayed speech/language skills.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Hyperactivity, hand-flapping, poor eye contact, tactile defensiveness (sensory sensitivity), large ears, and long face.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Moderate to severe intellectual disability, post-pubertal macroorchidism (enlarged testicles), mitral valve prolapse, and recurrent seizures.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: FMR1 gene analysis using PCR and Southern Blot to count the number of CGG repeats and assess methylation status.
diseases.treatment_plan: Supportive therapy framework. Early intervention with speech, physical, and occupational therapy. Behavioral modification plans. Medications to manage comorbid ADHD, anxiety, or seizures.
diseases.home_care: Implement visual schedules and reduce sensory stimuli to prevent meltdowns. Use positive reinforcement to build social skills. Provide speech support.
diseases.medicinal_elements: None. Medications are targeted strictly at comorbidities (e.g. Methylphenidate for ADHD, Sertraline for anxiety).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Seek evaluation if a young child exhibits significant speech delay, poor eye contact, hand-flapping, or hyperactive behaviors.
diseases.prevention: Genetic counseling for families with a history of intellectual disability or premature ovarian failure; maternal carrier screening.
diseases.vaccination_info: No specific immunizations are associated; follow standard schedules.
diseases.prognosis: Variable. Males are typically more severely affected than females. Early therapy significantly enhances daily functional and life skills.
diseases.recovery_timeline: This is a lifelong genetic X-linked condition; support strategies evolve as the child transitions to adulthood.
diseases.complications: Severe intellectual disability, autism spectrum symptoms, mitral valve prolapse, and chronic anxiety.
diseases.overview: FMR1 Gene Trinucleotide Repeat Expansion Syndrome

diseases.name: Rett Syndrome
diseases.classification: MECP2 Mutation X-Linked Neurodevelopmental Regression Disorder
diseases.affected_age_group: Infants and toddlers (typically recognized between 6 to 18 months; almost exclusively in girls)
diseases.causes: Mutations in the MECP2 gene on the X chromosome (essential for brain development and transcriptional regulation).
diseases.pathophysiology: MECP2 protein deficiency impairs chromatin remodeling and transcriptional control in mature neurons, causing progressive microcephaly, loss of synaptic connections, and severe motor and autonomic regression.
diseases.symptoms_early: Apparently normal development until 6–18 months, followed by a deceleration of head growth and developmental flattening.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Rapid regression of acquired motor skills and spoken language. Loss of purposeful hand skills, replaced by stereotypic hand movements (wringing, hand washing).
diseases.symptoms_severe: Complete loss of spoken language, severe scoliosis, breathing irregularities (hyperventilation and apnea episodes at rest), seizures, and loss of walking ability.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Genetic testing showing mutation in the MECP2 gene. Clinical diagnosis based on diagnostic criteria (hand stereotypies, motor regression).
diseases.treatment_plan: Supportive and palliative framework. Provide physical and occupational therapy to maintain joint mobility. Manage seizures and breathing abnormalities. Nutritional support (feeding tube if dysphagia is severe).
diseases.home_care: Monitor for breathing abnormalities and manage seizures. Position the child carefully to prevent scoliosis progression. Encourage non-verbal communication (eye-gaze boards).
diseases.medicinal_elements: Trofinetide (first FDA-approved active drug to treat core symptoms of Rett syndrome); Valproic acid or Levetiracetam (generic antiepileptics to manage seizures).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Seek immediate evaluation if a previously normal infant begins losing hand skills, stops babbling, or exhibits repetitive hand wringing.
diseases.prevention: No prevention is possible, as the mutation is almost always a random de novo somatic event.
diseases.vaccination_info: No specific immunizations are associated; follow standard schedules.
diseases.prognosis: Guarded. While most girls survive to adulthood, they remain severely disabled and require lifelong constant care.
diseases.recovery_timeline: This is a progressive neurodevelopmental genetic disorder; management is continuous and supportive.
diseases.complications: Seizures, severe scoliosis, prolonged QT interval (sudden cardiac death risk), dysphagia, and aspiration pneumonia.
diseases.overview: MECP2 Mutation X-Linked Neurodevelopmental Regression Disorder

diseases.name: Neonatal Abstinence Syndrome (NAS)
diseases.affected_age_group: Neonates (onset typically within 24 to 72 hours of birth)
diseases.causes: Sudden cessation of fetal exposure to maternal drugs (typically opioids, methadone, or heroin) upon delivery.
diseases.pathophysiology: Abrupt withdrawal of opioid exposure triggers a sharp drop in synaptic drug levels. This hyper-activates the locus coeruleus and norepinephrine systems, leading to autonomic instability, central nervous system hyper-irritability, and gastrointestinal dysfunction.
diseases.symptoms_early: High-pitched crying, irritability, excessive sneezing, yawning, and sleep periods lasting less than an hour.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Marked tremors at rest, muscle hypertonia, frantic fist sucking, nasal congestion, and poor feeding due to uncoordinated sucking.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Projectile vomiting, watery diarrhea leading to severe diaper rash and dehydration, high fever, and generalized withdrawal seizures.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Clinical monitoring using the Finnegan Neonatal Abstinence Scoring System or Eat, Sleep, Console (ESC) model. Toxicology screen of infant urine or meconium.
diseases.treatment_plan: First-line is supportive, non-pharmacological care (swaddling, skin-to-skin contact, low-sensory environment, small frequent feedings). If scores remain high, initiate replacement pharmacotherapy and taper gradually.
diseases.home_care: Keep the room dark and quiet. Swaddle the infant snugly. Offer frequent, small, high-calorie feedings to match high metabolic needs. Minimize stimulation.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Morphine sulfate or Methadone (generic opioid active ingredients titrated and tapered to control withdrawal symptoms); Clonidine (second-line generic active ingredient).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Any newborn suspected of prenatal drug exposure showing high-pitched crying, tremors, vomiting, or seizures requires immediate neonatal emergency care.
diseases.prevention: Maternal prenatal care, drug rehabilitation programs for pregnant women, and avoiding abrupt cessation of methadone during pregnancy without medical supervision.
diseases.vaccination_info: No specific immunizations are associated; follow standard schedule post-discharge.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent with appropriate supportive and pharmacological weaning; long-term developmental monitoring is recommended.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Withdrawal and weaning typically take 1 to 4 weeks, but can extend to 2 months depending on the drug type.
diseases.complications: Severe dehydration, skin breakdown, weight loss, seizures, and developmental delays.

diseases.name: Persistent Pulmonary Hypertension of the Newborn (PPHN)
diseases.affected_age_group: Term or near-term neonates (onset within hours of birth)
diseases.causes: Idiopathic; or secondary to meconium aspiration syndrome, respiratory distress syndrome, pneumonia, or congenital diaphragmatic hernia.
diseases.pathophysiology: Failure of the pulmonary vascular resistance to drop at birth. High pulmonary pressures force deoxygenated blood to bypass the lungs by shunting right-to-left across the patent ductus arteriosus (PDA) or patent foramen ovale (PFO), resulting in severe refractory hypoxemia.
diseases.symptoms_early: Tachypnea and mild cyanosis (bluish skin color) developing within the first 12 hours of life.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Severe respiratory distress (grunting, chest retractions, flaring) and systemic hypotension (low blood pressure).
diseases.symptoms_severe: Severe cardiogenic shock, lactic acidosis, myocardial dysfunction, systemic organ hypoxemia, and cardiorespiratory arrest.
