diseases.diagnosis_methods: Echocardiogram demonstrating high pulmonary artery pressure, right-to-left shunting, and absence of structural congenital heart disease.
diseases.treatment_plan: Immediate NICU stabilization. Provide mechanical ventilation to optimize oxygenation and avoid acidosis. Administer inhaled Nitric Oxide to selectively dilate pulmonary vessels. Maintain systemic blood pressure with ionotropes.
diseases.home_care: Home care is strictly not applicable. This is a critical neonatal ICU emergency.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Inhaled Nitric Oxide (iNO - selective active pulmonary vasodilator gas); Milrinone (active vasodilator and ionotrope); Dopamine or Epinephrine (intravenous active ionotropic infusions).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Any newborn presenting with rapid breathing, cyanosis, or low oxygen saturation in the nursery requires immediate neonatal emergency transport.
diseases.prevention: Prevention of post-term delivery (avoiding meconium aspiration), maternal avoidance of NSAIDs in the third trimester (which can cause premature closure of the ductus arteriosus).
diseases.vaccination_info: No specific immunizations are associated with this acute neonatal condition.
diseases.prognosis: Good if diagnosed early and responsive to inhaled Nitric Oxide; mortality is 10-20% and survivors require follow-up for chronic lung disease.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Acute stabilization takes 3 to 7 days in the NICU; full lung recovery can take weeks.
diseases.complications: Chronic lung disease, sensorineural hearing loss, neurological delay, and cardiac dysfunction.

diseases.name: Intraventricular Hemorrhage (IVH) of the Newborn
diseases.classification: Neonatal Germinal Matrix Capillary Rupture Syndrome
diseases.affected_age_group: Premature neonates (typically born before 32 weeks gestation or birth weight <1500g)
diseases.causes: Fragile blood vessels in the germinal matrix combined with fluctuations in cerebral blood flow in a premature infant.
diseases.pathophysiology: The subependymal germinal matrix is a highly cellular, richly vascularized region in the premature brain. Its capillaries have single-endothelial walls lacking structural support. Fluctuations in cerebral perfusion (due to blood pressure spikes, apnea, or ventilation changes) rupture these fragile capillaries, spilling blood into the lateral ventricles.
diseases.symptoms_early: Often clinically silent in mild cases (Grade I-II); identified via routine screening cranial ultrasounds in premature infants.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Sudden drop in hematocrit, metabolic acidosis, increase in apnea and bradycardia episodes, and hypotonia.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Bulging fontanelle, decerebrate posturing (stiff limbs extended), high-pitched cry, seizures, stupor, and progressive post-hemorrhagic hydrocephalus.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Cranial ultrasound performed through the anterior fontanelle, grading the hemorrhage from Grade I (limited to germinal matrix) to Grade IV (extending into brain parenchyma).
diseases.treatment_plan: Supportive neuroprotective care. Maintain stable blood pressure, avoid rapid infusions of hypertonic fluids, and optimize mechanical ventilation. Perform serial head circumference measurements. If progressive hydrocephalus develops, place a temporary ventricular reservoir or ventriculoperitoneal shunt.
diseases.home_care: Home care is strictly not applicable during the acute phase. Post-discharge, monitor developmental milestones and head circumference closely.
diseases.medicinal_elements: None. Supportive IV fluids and blood transfusions (packed red cells) to maintain perfusion and hemoglobin parameters.
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Any premature infant meeting weight or gestational criteria must undergo scheduled screening cranial ultrasounds in the NICU.
diseases.prevention: Administer antenatal corticosteroids (Betamethasone) to mothers in preterm labor (reduces IVH incidence by 50%). Delayed cord clamping at birth.
diseases.vaccination_info: No specific immunizations are associated; follow standard pediatric schedules post-discharge.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent for Grades I-II; guarded for Grades III-IV, which carry a high risk of motor deficits (cerebral palsy), intellectual disability, and developmental delays.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Acute bleeding resolves within days; long-term neurological rehabilitation spans years.
diseases.complications: Post-hemorrhagic hydrocephalus, cerebral palsy, intellectual disability, and developmental delay.
diseases.overview: Neonatal Germinal Matrix Capillary Rupture Syndrome

diseases.name: Transient Tachypnea of the Newborn (TTN)
diseases.classification: Neonatal Alveolar Fluid Clearance Delay Syndrome
diseases.affected_age_group: Term or near-term newborns (onset immediately at birth or within 2 hours)
diseases.causes: Delayed clearance of fetal alveolar fluid at birth; highly associated with Cesarean delivery (lack of labor squeeze) and maternal asthma or diabetes.
diseases.pathophysiology: During labor, epinephrine triggers type II alveolar cells to switch from fluid secretion to fluid absorption via epithelial sodium channels (ENaC). If this process is delayed or bypassed (as in C-section), residual lung fluid remains in the alveoli and lymphatic spaces. This causes lung compliance to drop, leading to tachypnea and mild hypoxemia.
diseases.symptoms_early: Tachypnea (respiratory rate exceeding 60 breaths/minute) presenting immediately after birth.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Respiratory rate increasing up to 80-100 breaths/minute, mild intercostal/subcostal retractions, and expiratory grunting.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Nasal flaring, mild cyanosis on room air requiring low-flow oxygen, resolving completely within 24 to 72 hours.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Chest X-ray showing hyperinflation, prominent vascular markings radiating from the hilum ("wet lungs"), and fluid in the fissures. Normal arterial blood gas.
diseases.treatment_plan: Supportive monitoring. Administer low-flow supplemental oxygen via nasal cannula if needed. Provide CPAP for moderate cases. Delay oral feeding if respiratory rate exceeds 80 to prevent aspiration.
diseases.home_care: Home care is not applicable. The condition is self-limiting and resolves fully in the hospital within the first few days of life.
diseases.medicinal_elements: None. No diuretics or steroids are indicated; supportive fluid management and oxygen are the mainstays of care.
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Any newborn showing rapid breathing or grunting in the delivery room or nursery requires immediate neonatal evaluation.
diseases.prevention: Avoid elective Cesarean deliveries before 39 weeks gestation to allow natural labor mechanisms to clear lung fluid.
diseases.vaccination_info: No specific immunizations are associated with this self-limiting condition.
diseases.prognosis: Outstanding. The condition is benign and resolves completely within 24 to 72 hours without long-term pulmonary complications.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Resolves fully in 1 to 3 days.
diseases.complications: Aspiration pneumonia (if fed while breathing rapidly) and pneumothorax (rare, if aggressive CPAP is applied).
diseases.overview: Neonatal Alveolar Fluid Clearance Delay Syndrome

diseases.name: Addison's Disease (Adrenal Insufficiency)
diseases.affected_age_group: School-aged children and adolescents (rare in infants, though congenital forms exist)
diseases.causes: Autoimmune destruction of the adrenal cortex (autoimmune adrenalitis), congenital adrenal hypoplasia, or adrenoleukodystrophy.
diseases.pathophysiology: Autoimmune or genetic destruction of the adrenal cortex leads to a progressive deficiency of glucocorticoids (cortisol) and mineralocorticoids (aldosterone). Deficient cortisol impairs glucose homeostasis, while deficient aldosterone impairs sodium reabsorption and potassium excretion in the kidneys.
diseases.symptoms_early: Slowly progressive fatigue, muscle weakness, salt cravings, and hyperpigmentation (darkening of skin creases, scars, and gums).
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Anorexia, weight loss, chronic abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, and recurrent orthostatic hypotension.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Adrenal Crisis: severe vascular collapse, refractory hypovolemic shock, hypoglycemia, hyperkalemia (high potassium), hyponatremia (low sodium), and cardiac arrhythmias.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Low morning cortisol and elevated ACTH levels. Confirmed by an ACTH stimulation test showing failure of cortisol to rise (<18 mcg/dL). Elevated plasma renin.
diseases.treatment_plan: Replace deficient hormones. Administer daily oral Hydrocortisone (glucocorticoid) and Fludrocortisone (mineralocorticoid). Implement a "Stress Dosing" plan (triple the steroid dose) during periods of fever, infection, surgery, or trauma.
diseases.home_care: Ensure the child carries medical alert identification. Keep an emergency Hydrocortisone injection kit at home and at school. Ensure adequate sodium intake during hot weather.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Hydrocortisone (generic active glucocorticoid replacement ingredient); Fludrocortisone acetate (generic active mineralocorticoid replacement ingredient).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Seek emergency evaluation immediately if a child with Addison's disease develops vomiting (unable to retain oral steroids), high fever, severe fatigue, or signs of shock.
diseases.prevention: No primary prevention exists for autoimmune adrenalitis or genetic adrenal disorders.
diseases.vaccination_info: Children on standard replacement doses of steroids (not immunosuppressive doses) can follow routine vaccination schedules; annual influenza vaccine is recommended.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent with strict compliance with hormone replacement and stress dosing protocols; children can live normal, active lives.
diseases.recovery_timeline: This is a lifelong primary endocrine deficiency; continuous daily hormone replacement is required.
diseases.complications: Adrenal crisis, severe hypoglycemia, electrolyte shock, and developmental growth delays.

diseases.name: Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH)
diseases.affected_age_group: Neonates and infants (salt-wasting crises present in the first 2 weeks of life)
diseases.causes: Autosomal recessive mutation in the CYP21A2 gene, leading to a deficiency of the enzyme 21-hydroxylase.
diseases.pathophysiology: Deficiency of 21-hydroxylase blocks the conversion of cholesterol precursors to cortisol and aldosterone. Deficient cortisol removes negative feedback on the pituitary, causing massive ACTH release. This overstimulates the adrenal cortex (hyperplasia), shunting accumulated precursors into androgen pathways, causing severe virilization.
diseases.symptoms_early: Atypical external genitalia in newborn girls (clitoromegaly, labial fusion). Detected via routine newborn screening.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: In salt-wasting CAH (75% of cases): vomiting, weight loss, progressive dehydration, and lethargy around 1 to 2 weeks of life.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Adrenal salt-wasting crisis: severe hypovolemic shock, hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, hypoglycemia, and death if untreated.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Elevated serum levels of 17-hydroxyprogesterone (17-OHP) on newborn screening. Confirmed with chromosomal karyotype and genetic testing.
diseases.treatment_plan: Initiate life-saving hormone replacement. Administer Hydrocortisone to suppress ACTH and excess androgens. Administer Fludrocortisone and sodium chloride supplements for salt-wasters. Implement stress dosing during illness.
diseases.home_care: Administer medications at exact scheduled times. Monitor for signs of vomiting or dehydration. Keep an emergency hydrocortisone injection kit. Coordinate pediatric endocrinology follow-up.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Hydrocortisone (generic active glucocorticoid); Fludrocortisone acetate (generic active mineralocorticoid); Sodium chloride (salt supplements for infants).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Any newborn with atypical genitalia, or any infant showing poor feeding, vomiting, or excessive weight loss in the first weeks of life, requires immediate emergency evaluation.
diseases.prevention: Genetic counseling for carrier parents; prenatal treatment with dexamethasone to prevent virilization in female fetuses is controversial and requires expert monitoring.
diseases.vaccination_info: Routine pediatric vaccines are recommended; stress dosing may be required during febrile vaccine reactions.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent with early diagnosis and compliant hormone replacement; children achieve normal growth and fertility, though close monitoring of bone age is needed.
diseases.recovery_timeline: This is a lifelong autosomal recessive metabolic disorder requiring continuous daily hormone management.
diseases.complications: Adrenal crisis, shock, severe electrolyte imbalances, short adult stature (due to advanced bone age), and virilization.

diseases.name: Maple Syrup Urine Disease (MSUD)
diseases.classification: Inherited Branched-Chain Amino Acid Decarboxylase Deficiency
diseases.affected_age_group: Neonates (onset within 48 to 72 hours of starting milk feedings)
diseases.causes: Autosomal recessive mutations in genes encoding the Branched-Chain Alpha-Keto Acid Dehydrogenase (BCKDH) enzyme complex.
diseases.pathophysiology: Deficiency of the BCKDH complex prevents catabolism of the branched-chain amino acids (BCAAs) leucine, isoleucine, and valine. BCAAs and their toxic alpha-keto acids accumulate in tissues. High leucine levels in the brain are neurotoxic, blocking other essential amino acids from crossing the blood-brain barrier and causing cerebral edema.
diseases.symptoms_early: Poor feeding, vomiting, irritability, and a characteristic sweet, maple syrup-like odor in the urine or earwax (cerumen).
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Progressive lethargy, high-pitched crying, hypertonia alternating with hypotonia, and backward arching of the neck (opisthotonos).
diseases.symptoms_severe: Seizures, signs of increased intracranial pressure (bulging fontanelle), cerebral edema, coma, and respiratory failure.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Newborn screening showing elevated blood levels of leucine, isoleucine, and valine, with the presence of alloisoleucine (diagnostic).
diseases.treatment_plan: Immediately exclude all dietary protein. Initiate emergency nutritional therapy with a specialized BCAA-free amino acid formula. If in acute metabolic crisis, administer IV glucose and insulin to promote anabolism. Hemodialysis may be needed to clear leucine.
diseases.home_care: Strictly adhere to a low-protein diet. Administer the BCAA-free medical formula. Perform regular home blood-spot leucine monitoring. Avoid illness/fasting.
diseases.medicinal_elements: BCAA-free amino acid formula; Thiamine (Vitamin B1 - given to patients with thiamine-responsive variants).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Seek immediate emergency care if a newborn shows poor feeding, vomiting, lethargy, or if their urine smells sweet. Any minor illness in a diagnosed child requires emergency protocol.
diseases.prevention: Prenatal genetic screening for carrier parents; immediately stop breast/cow milk if MSUD is suspected.
diseases.vaccination_info: Maintain standard pediatric vaccines, but monitor for metabolic decompensation during febrile post-vaccine reactions.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent if diagnosed and treated in the first week of life. Late diagnosis or poorly controlled metabolic crises lead to severe brain damage and intellectual disability.
diseases.recovery_timeline: This is a lifelong genetic metabolic disorder requiring continuous daily dietary restriction.
diseases.complications: Cerebral edema, seizures, metabolic encephalopathy, cognitive impairment, and coma.
diseases.overview: Inherited Branched-Chain Amino Acid Decarboxylase Deficiency

diseases.name: Tay-Sachs Disease
diseases.classification: Inherited GM2 Gangliosidosis Lysosomal Storage Disorder
diseases.affected_age_group: Infants (onset of symptoms typically between 3 and 6 months of age)
diseases.causes: Autosomal recessive mutations in the HEXA gene on chromosome 15, causing a deficiency of the enzyme beta-hexosaminidase A.
diseases.pathophysiology: Deficiency of hexosaminidase A prevents the degradation of GM2 gangliosides in cell lysosomes. GM2 gangliosides accumulate to toxic levels in neuronal lysosomes, causing progressive cell swelling, myelin breakdown, and widespread neurodegeneration.
diseases.symptoms_early: Mild motor delays, loss of head control, and an exaggerated startle response to loud noises (hyperacusis) between 3 and 6 months.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Loss of ability to sit, crawl, roll over, or smile. Marked muscle weakness, loss of focus, and a diagnostic macular "cherry-red spot" on retinal exam.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Recurrent generalized seizures, blindness, deafness, progressive spasticity, dysphagia (swallowing failure), decerebrate posturing, and complete vegetative state.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Measurement of beta-hexosaminidase A activity in white blood cells or serum (showing absent or near-absent activity). Confirmed by HEXA gene sequencing.
diseases.treatment_plan: Supportive, palliative, and symptomatic care. No curative or disease-modifying therapies currently exist. Focus on seizure control, chest physical therapy to prevent pneumonia, and nutritional support (G-tube placement).
diseases.home_care: Provide gentle comfort care (positioning, suctioning). Administer medications to control seizures. Maintain range-of-motion exercises to prevent joint contractures. Seek emotional support for the family.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Levetiracetam or Phenobarbital (generic antiepileptic active ingredients to manage seizures); Glycopyrrolate (to dry secretions and prevent drooling/aspiration).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Consult a pediatrician if a previously normal 3-6 month old infant starts losing motor milestones, fails to hold their head up, or shows an exaggerated startle response to noise.
diseases.prevention: Pre-conception genetic screening for carrier status (especially in high-risk populations such as Ashkenazi Jewish cohorts); prenatal diagnosis (amniocentesis/CVS).
diseases.vaccination_info: Supportive immunizations are recommended, but do not alter the course of this lysosomal storage disorder.
diseases.prognosis: Fatal. The condition is progressive and leads to death, typically by age 2 to 5, usually due to aspiration pneumonia.
diseases.recovery_timeline: This is a progressive, fatal genetic neurodegenerative disorder; management is supportive and palliative throughout the child's life.
diseases.complications: Generalized seizures, aspiration pneumonia, dysphagia, decerebrate rigidity, and premature death.
diseases.overview: Inherited GM2 Gangliosidosis Lysosomal Storage Disorder

diseases.name: Roseola Infantum (Sixth Disease)
diseases.affected_age_group: 6 Months to 2 Years
diseases.causes: Human Herpesvirus 6 (HHV-6); occasionally Human Herpesvirus 7 (HHV-7).
diseases.pathophysiology: HHV-6 replication in leukocytes triggers a high fever. Defervescence follows viral clearance, leading to immune-complex deposition in the skin and the characteristic rose-colored rash.
diseases.symptoms_early: High spikes of sudden fever (39.5°C to 40.5°C) lasting 3 to 5 days. Child is otherwise active.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Rapid defervescence (fever drops) on day 4 or 5, immediately followed by the appearance of a non-itchy, rose-pink maculopapular rash on the trunk, spreading to neck and limbs.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Febrile seizures during the initial rapid temperature spike, extreme irritability, bulging fontanelle (rare), and poor oral intake.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Clinical evaluation based on the high fever followed by sudden rash after defervescence.
diseases.treatment_plan: Supportive care. Maintain hydration and manage fever with antipyretics.
diseases.home_care: Lukewarm sponge baths. Encourage fluids. Monitor temperature spikes closely.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Acetaminophen or Ibuprofen (active ingredients for fever control); Oral rehydration solution (for hydration).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Seek emergency care if a febrile seizure occurs, the child is lethargic, or the rash becomes purpuric.
diseases.prevention: Standard contact hygiene. Avoid contact with children in the febrile phase.
diseases.vaccination_info: No vaccine available.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent. Self-limiting; rash fades in 1 to 2 days without scarring.
diseases.recovery_timeline: 5 to 7 days total.
diseases.complications: Febrile seizures, encephalitis (extremely rare).

diseases.name: Erythema Infectiosum (Fifth Disease)
diseases.affected_age_group: 4 to 10 Years
diseases.causes: Human Parvovirus B19.
diseases.pathophysiology: Parvovirus B19 infects erythrocyte progenitor cells in the bone marrow. The rash is immune-mediated, presenting once viremia clears.
diseases.symptoms_early: Mild low-grade fever, headache, rhinorrhea, and mild joint pain.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Bright red rash on the cheeks ('slapped cheek' appearance) appearing 2-5 days after initial symptoms, followed by a reticulated (lace-like) maculopapular rash on the trunk and extremities.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Aplastic crisis in children with sickle cell anemia or spherocytosis, severe arthropathy, and chronic anemia.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Clinical diagnosis based on the classic slapped-cheek rash. IgM antibody testing can confirm infection.
diseases.treatment_plan: Supportive care. Pain management for joint pain if present.
diseases.home_care: Encourage rest and fluids. Avoid hot baths or sunlight, which can cause the rash to flare up.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Ibuprofen or Acetaminophen (for fever and joint pain).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Seek medical assessment if the child has sickle cell anemia or a compromised immune system and develops a fever.
diseases.prevention: Frequent handwashing. Avoid sharing utensils.
diseases.vaccination_info: No vaccine available.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent. Self-limiting; rash resolves in 1 to 3 weeks.
diseases.recovery_timeline: 7 to 21 days.
diseases.complications: Transient aplastic crisis, arthropathy, fetal hydrops (risk to pregnant contacts).

diseases.name: Intussusception
diseases.affected_age_group: 3 Months to 3 Years (Peak: 5 to 9 Months)
diseases.causes: Idiopathic in most infants; may be associated with viral hypertrophied Peyer's patches (e.g. Adenovirus) or lead points like Meckel's diverticulum in older children.
diseases.pathophysiology: A segment of the intestine invaginates (telescopes) into an adjacent segment. This compromises venous and lymphatic drainage, causing bowel wall edema, bleeding, and potential bowel necrosis or perforation.
diseases.symptoms_early: Sudden onset of severe, episodic, cramping abdominal pain. The child draws knees to the chest and cries inconsolably, then behaves normally between episodes.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Vomiting (initially non-bilious, progressing to bilious), lethargy, and a palpable sausage-shaped mass in the right upper abdomen.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Passage of 'currant jelly' stools (stools containing blood and mucus), abdominal distention, shock, peritonitis, and grunting respiration.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Abdominal ultrasound is the gold standard, showing a 'target' or 'donut' sign.
diseases.treatment_plan: Stabilize with IV fluids. Perform non-operative reduction using an air or contrast enema. Surgical reduction is required if enema fails or there are signs of perforation.
diseases.home_care: Not applicable. This is a medical emergency requiring immediate hospitalization.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Intravenous fluids (active fluid resuscitation); Cefotaxime or Metronidazole (prophylactic antibiotics if surgery is required).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Seek immediate emergency care if a child has episodic severe pain, draws knees to chest, vomits bilious fluid, or passes bloody stools.
diseases.prevention: None available.
diseases.vaccination_info: Rotavirus vaccination is sometimes associated with a tiny risk variation but is overall protective against severe gastroenteritis. Ensure vaccines are given on time.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent if reduced early (within 24-48 hours). Delay can lead to bowel resection or mortality.
diseases.recovery_timeline: 1 to 3 days after non-surgical reduction; 5 to 7 days if surgery is required.
diseases.complications: Bowel necrosis, bowel perforation, peritonitis, sepsis, and hypovolemic shock.

diseases.name: Infantile Hypertrophic Pyloric Stenosis
diseases.affected_age_group: 2 to 8 Weeks of Age
diseases.causes: Multifactorial; genetic predisposition, male sex (first-born males), and early erythromycin exposure.
diseases.pathophysiology: Hypertrophy and hyperplasia of the circular pyloric muscle layer cause progressive narrowing and elongation of the pyloric canal, leading to complete gastric outlet obstruction.
diseases.symptoms_early: Spitting up or mild vomiting after feeds, which gradually increases in frequency and force.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Non-bilious projectile vomiting immediately after feeding. The child is hungry and demands feeding again ('hungry vomiter').
diseases.symptoms_severe: Significant dehydration, weight loss, a palpable olive-shaped mass in the right upper quadrant, visible gastric peristaltic waves, and hypokalemic hypochloriemic metabolic alkalosis.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Abdominal ultrasound showing pyloric muscle thickness >3mm and length >15mm.
diseases.treatment_plan: Correct electrolyte imbalances and dehydration with IV fluids. Once stable, perform a surgical pyloromyotomy (Fredet-Ramstedt procedure).
diseases.home_care: Not applicable. Requires inpatient stabilization and surgery.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Intravenous saline with potassium chloride (for electrolyte correction).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Consult a doctor immediately for persistent or projectile vomiting in an infant under 3 months of age.
diseases.prevention: Avoid unnecessary erythromycin exposure in newborns.
diseases.vaccination_info: No vaccine available.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent after pyloromyotomy. The child is typically cured and feeds normally within 24-48 hours.
diseases.recovery_timeline: 2 to 3 days total hospital stay.
diseases.complications: Severe dehydration, metabolic alkalosis, aspiration pneumonia, and failure to thrive.

diseases.name: Scarlet Fever (Scarlatina)
diseases.affected_age_group: 5 to 15 Years
diseases.causes: Group A Beta-Hemolytic Streptococcus (Streptococcus pyogenes) producing erythrogenic toxins.
diseases.pathophysiology: Streptococcal pharyngitis is complicated by the release of erythrogenic toxins (A, B, or C). These enter the circulation and induce a delayed-type hypersensitivity reaction in the skin, causing the rash.
diseases.symptoms_early: Sudden onset of high fever, sore throat, painful swallowing, chills, headache, and vomiting.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: A fine, red, sandpaper-like rash appearing 12-48 hours later, starting on the neck and spreading to the trunk and limbs. Pastia's lines (hyperpigmentation in skin folds) and a strawberry tongue.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Rheumatic fever signs, acute glomerulonephritis, severe pharyngeal edema causing airway compromise, and septic shock.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Rapid Strep Test or throat culture to isolate Group A Streptococcus.
diseases.treatment_plan: 10-day course of oral antibiotics (or single IM injection) to eradicate Streptococcus, prevent rheumatic fever, and limit transmission.
diseases.home_care: Soft foods and cold liquids to soothe the throat. Use warm saline gargles. Ensure completion of the full antibiotic course.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Penicillin V or Amoxicillin (active ingredients for GAS eradication); Cephalexin (alternative for penicillin-allergic patients).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Consult a doctor for sore throat accompanied by a rash, strawberry tongue, or high fever.
diseases.prevention: Avoid contact with infected individuals. Maintain good respiratory and hand hygiene.
diseases.vaccination_info: No vaccine available.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent if treated. Symptoms resolve in 3 to 5 days, followed by peeling skin.
diseases.recovery_timeline: 7 to 10 days.
diseases.complications: Acute rheumatic fever, post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis, peritonsillar abscess, and otitis media.

diseases.name: Pediatric Inguinal Hernia
diseases.affected_age_group: Infancy to Childhood (More common in males and premature infants)
diseases.causes: Failure of the processus vaginalis to obliterate before birth.
diseases.pathophysiology: A persistent patent processus vaginalis allows abdominal contents (intestine or ovary) to protrude into the inguinal canal, presenting as a groin mass.
diseases.symptoms_early: A painless, intermittent bulge in the groin or scrotum, which becomes more prominent when the child cries, coughs, or stands.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: The bulge remains present but is easily reducible (can be pushed back gently). Child is otherwise comfortable.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Incarcerated hernia: the bulge becomes painful, firm, tender, and non-reducible, accompanied by irritability, vomiting, abdominal distention, and signs of bowel obstruction.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Clinical examination. Abdominal ultrasound can confirm if diagnosis is unclear.
diseases.treatment_plan: Reducible hernias should be scheduled for elective surgical repair (inguinal herniorrhaphy) to prevent incarceration. Incarcerated hernias require urgent manual reduction or emergency surgery.
diseases.home_care: Monitor the groin bulge closely. Avoid crying fits if possible. Do not apply force to reduce it.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Acetaminophen or Ibuprofen (active ingredients for post-operative pain control).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Seek immediate emergency medical attention if the groin bulge becomes hard, painful, red, or tender, or if the child starts vomiting.
diseases.prevention: None available.
diseases.vaccination_info: No vaccine available.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent after elective surgical repair, which has a very low recurrence rate (<1%).
diseases.recovery_timeline: 1 to 2 weeks for full recovery post-surgery.
diseases.complications: Hernia incarceration, bowel strangulation (loss of blood supply to intestine), testicular atrophy, or ovarian damage.

diseases.name: Pityriasis Rosea
diseases.affected_age_group: 5 to 15 Years (Peak occurrence)
diseases.causes: Spontaneous reactivation of Human Herpesvirus 6 (HHV-6) or Human Herpesvirus 7 (HHV-7); non-contagious and not related to food/allergies.
diseases.pathophysiology: Reactivation of latent HHV-6 or HHV-7 triggers a systemic cell-mediated immune activation, starting with a localized patch (herald patch) and followed by a wider eruptive phase of secondary smaller lesions distributed along dermal cleavage lines (Langer's lines) in a "Christmas tree" pattern.
diseases.symptoms_early: A single, prominent, pinkish/salmon-colored oval plaque (2-10 cm) with fine scaling, typically on the trunk, neck, or thigh, known as the "herald patch".
diseases.symptoms_progressive: A secondary eruption of smaller, similarly scaled oval rose-colored patches (1-2 cm) spreading symmetrically across the trunk and proximal limbs along skin cleavage lines.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Pruritus (itching) that becomes intense with body heat, hot showers, or sweat; mild transient post-inflammatory color changes (hyperpigmentation) as patches resolve.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Primarily clinical based on the presence of the herald patch and Christmas tree distribution. Potassium hydroxide (KOH) skin scraping can exclude tinea infections.
diseases.treatment_plan: Supportive reassurance as it is self-limiting. Control itching with emollients and mild anti-pruritic lotions. Avoid hot water, vigorous exercise, or scrubbing.
diseases.home_care: Lukewarm showers only; apply gentle fragrance-free moisturizers; moderate outdoor sunlight exposure can help accelerate skin clearance.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Calamine lotion (generic topical anti-itch active element); Hydrocortisone cream 1% (generic low-potency topical steroid for moderate pruritus).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Seek pediatric evaluation if the rash lasts longer than 8 weeks, shows signs of secondary bacterial infection from scratching (pus, warmth), or is accompanied by joint pain.
diseases.prevention: No known prevention since reactivation is spontaneous and non-infectious to others.
diseases.vaccination_info: No immunization exists for HHV-6 or HHV-7.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent; patches resolve completely within 4 to 12 weeks with zero permanent scarring.
diseases.recovery_timeline: 4 to 12 weeks.
diseases.complications: Severe pruritus leading to excoriations and secondary bacterial superinfection.

diseases.name: Transient Synovitis of the Hip
diseases.affected_age_group: 3 to 8 Years (Most common cause of pediatric hip pain)
diseases.causes: Immune-mediated transient inflammation within the joint space, highly associated with a recent viral upper respiratory or gastrointestinal infection.
diseases.pathophysiology: Cross-reactive immune antibodies from a recent viral infection target the synovial lining of the hip joint. This causes acute synovial hypercellularity, inflammation, and fluid accumulation (effusion), increasing joint capsule pressure, causing pain, and limiting movement.
diseases.symptoms_early: Sudden onset of unilateral hip, groin, or thigh pain, causing the child to refuse weight-bearing or walk with a limp.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Referred pain down to the knee; limited range of motion of the hip, especially during abduction and internal rotation.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Low-grade fever (usually under 38°C); hip held in a flexed, abducted, and externally rotated position to minimize joint tension.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Exclusion of septic arthritis. Hip ultrasonography demonstrates joint effusion. Complete blood count and inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP) are normal or minimally elevated.
diseases.treatment_plan: Strict bed rest and restriction of all weight-bearing activity until pain resolves. Implement anti-inflammatory analgesics.
diseases.home_care: Keep child resting; apply warm compresses to the hip joint; assist with positional changes; closely monitor for fever or increasing pain.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Ibuprofen (generic nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug active ingredient to suppress synovial inflammation and manage pain).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Seek immediate emergency evaluation if the child develops a high fever, severe pain, joint swelling, or if the limp fails to resolve within 10 days, to rule out Septic Arthritis.
diseases.prevention: No specific prevention; maintain standard hygiene practices to avoid primary viral infections.
diseases.vaccination_info: No immunization exists.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent; resolves completely within 1 to 2 weeks with zero long-term joint or bone sequelae.
diseases.recovery_timeline: 1 to 2 weeks.
diseases.complications: Recurrent synovitis (occurs in ~5-10% of cases); coxae magna (temporary femoral head enlargement, benign).

diseases.name: PANDAS
diseases.affected_age_group: 3 to 12 Years (Pre-pubertal pediatric onset)
diseases.causes: Autoimmune cross-reactivity following an infection with Group A Beta-Hemolytic Streptococcus (GAS), such as strep throat or scarlet fever.
diseases.pathophysiology: Streptococcal antibodies created during infection cross-react with proteins in the basal ganglia of the brain (molecular mimicry). This triggers localized neuroinflammation, disrupting neurotransmitter pathways and causing a sudden onset of tics, compulsions, and emotional instability.
diseases.symptoms_early: Abrupt, "overnight" onset of severe obsessive-compulsive behaviors (symmetry compulsions, contamination fears) or motor/vocal tics.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Severe separation anxiety, emotional lability (sudden rage or crying), deterioration in handwriting and fine motor control, and insomnia.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Sensory sensitivities, urinary frequency or bedwetting, food restriction due to choking or contamination fears, and panic episodes.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Clinical diagnosis based on five criteria: OCD/tics onset, pediatric age, abrupt/episodic course, temporal association with GAS infection (proven by throat culture or ASLO titers), and neurological abnormalities.
diseases.treatment_plan: Eradicate streptococcal infection with antibiotics. Address OCD/tics with cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT). Consider immunomodulation (IVIG/plasmapheresis) for severe cases.
diseases.home_care: Create a supportive, low-stress environment. Reassure the child during behavioral flares. Implement school writing/time accommodations as motor control fluctuates.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Amoxicillin or Azithromycin (generic antibiotic active elements to eradicate streptococcal triggers); Ibuprofen (generic NSAID to reduce neuroinflammation).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Seek pediatric consultation immediately if a child exhibits a sudden, dramatic change in behavior, severe OCD symptoms, tics, or separation anxiety, particularly after strep throat.
diseases.prevention: Promptly diagnose and fully treat all Group A Streptococcal throat infections with a complete course of antibiotics.
diseases.vaccination_info: No immunization exists for Group A Streptococcus.
diseases.prognosis: Variable; many experience complete recovery after antibiotic course, but symptoms can flare with subsequent streptococcal exposure.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Weeks to months depending on antibiotic response and symptom management.
diseases.complications: Chronic severe OCD, nutritional deficiency from food refusal, separation anxiety interfering with schooling.

diseases.name: Biliary Atresia
diseases.affected_age_group: 2 to 8 Weeks (Newborn/Early Infancy)
diseases.causes: Unknown; believed to be an immune-mediated or infectious inflammatory process causing progressive damage to the bile ducts shortly after birth.
diseases.pathophysiology: Progressive, inflammatory sclerosis of the extrahepatic biliary tree prevents bile from draining from the liver to the duodenum. The accumulation of toxic bile acids causes rapid, progressive hepatocyte necrosis, portal tract fibrosis, biliary cirrhosis, and portal hypertension.
diseases.symptoms_early: Persistent neonatal jaundice beyond 14 days of life; dark yellow urine that stains the diaper.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Acholic (pale, clay-colored or white) stools; hepatomegaly (enlarged, firm liver) and splenomegaly.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Ascites (abdominal fluid), poor weight gain, bruising or bleeding tendencies due to malabsorption of vitamin K, and hepatic failure.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Direct/conjugated bilirubin measurement; abdominal ultrasound demonstrating small/absent gallbladder; liver biopsy showing ductular proliferation. Confirmed via intraoperative cholangiogram.
diseases.treatment_plan: Urgent surgical hepatoportoenterostomy (Kasai procedure) to bypass blocked ducts, ideally before 60 days of age. Supplemental fat-soluble vitamins and MCT formula. Liver transplantation if Kasai fails.
diseases.home_care: Monitor stool color daily using a stool color card; administer fat-soluble vitamins; feed high-calorie medium-chain triglyceride formula; watch for signs of fever/cholangitis.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Ursodeoxycholic acid (generic bile acid active ingredient to promote bile flow); Vitamins A, D, E, K (generic fat-soluble vitamins to prevent deficiency).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Seek immediate pediatric evaluation if a newborn remains jaundiced past 2 weeks of age, passes white or clay-colored stools, or has dark yellow urine.
diseases.prevention: No known prevention measures exist.
diseases.vaccination_info: Standard immunizations are recommended; live vaccines (e.g. Rotavirus) are withheld if liver failure progresses or transplant is planned.
diseases.prognosis: Highly dependent on age at Kasai surgery. Even with early surgery, ~70-80% of children eventually require a liver transplant before adulthood.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Surgical recovery is 1-2 weeks; clinical management of biliary flow is lifelong.
diseases.complications: Ascites, cholangitis, portal hypertension, esophageal varices, end-stage liver failure.

diseases.name: G6PD Deficiency
diseases.affected_age_group: All ages (Inherited; typically triggered in infancy or childhood)
diseases.causes: Genetic X-linked recessive mutation in the glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) gene, reducing red blood cell protection against oxidative stress.
diseases.pathophysiology: G6PD deficiency impairs the generation of NADPH, which is required to maintain glutathione in its reduced state. When cells are exposed to oxidative triggers, hemoglobin is oxidized and precipitates as Heinz bodies. The spleen removes these damaged cells, causing acute intravascular and extravascular hemolysis.
diseases.symptoms_early: Often asymptomatic until triggered. Early signs of a hemolytic flare include fatigue, sudden pallor, and dark tea-colored urine.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Jaundice of the skin and conjunctiva (yellow eyes), low-grade fever, and acute abdominal or lower back pain.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Severe acute anemia, tachycardia, dyspnea, splenomegaly, and potential acute kidney injury due to hemoglobinuria.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Quantitative G6PD enzyme assay (performed outside acute hemolytic windows). Peripheral blood smear demonstrating Heinz bodies, "bite cells", and "blister cells".
diseases.treatment_plan: Strict avoidance of oxidative triggers. Hydration to protect kidneys during hemolysis. Red blood cell transfusion if anemia is life-threatening.
diseases.home_care: Provide caretakers with a strict list of forbidden substances. Check ingredients of all foods, over-the-counter medications, and herbal supplements.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Intravenous hydration (supportive renal perfusion active element); Red blood cell transfusion elements (for emergency anemia support).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Seek emergency care immediately if a child with G6PD deficiency develops sudden severe pale skin, dark yellow/orange skin, tea-colored urine, or fast breathing.
diseases.prevention: Strict avoidance of fava beans (favism), naphthalene (mothballs), aspirin, sulfonamide antibiotics, antimalarial drugs, and nitrofurantoin.
diseases.vaccination_info: Ensure routine immunizations, as infections are the most common triggers for G6PD hemolytic episodes.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent with strict trigger avoidance; life expectancy and quality of life are completely normal.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Hemolytic crisis resolves within 1 to 2 weeks once the triggering agent is removed.
diseases.complications: Severe hemolytic anemia, acute renal failure (tubular necrosis), neonatal kernicterus if jaundice is untreated.

diseases.name: Laryngomalacia
diseases.affected_age_group: Newborns to 18 Months (Typically presents in first few weeks of life)
diseases.causes: Congenital developmental delay in the rigidity of laryngeal cartilages, combined with altered neuromuscular tone of the upper airway.
diseases.pathophysiology: Floppiness of the supraglottic tissues (omega-shaped epiglottis, redundant arytenoid mucosa). During inspiration, negative pressure causes these soft tissues to collapse inward, narrowing the airway inlet and producing high-pitched, noisy breathing (stridor).
diseases.symptoms_early: High-pitched, squeaking, or "wet" inspiratory stridor starting in the first 2-4 weeks, worse when supine, crying, or feeding.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Stridor worsening up to 6 months of age; associated feeding difficulties and gastroesophageal reflux (spitting up).
diseases.symptoms_severe: Sustained chest wall retractions, episodes of cyanosis, difficulty breathing causing poor weight gain (failure to thrive), and sleep apnea.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Direct laryngoscopy on an awake infant via flexible fiberoptic scope, confirming floppy arytenoids and inward collapse during inhalation.
diseases.treatment_plan: Conservative observation in 90% of cases as cartilage stiffens. Treat gastroesophageal reflux to reduce swelling. Supraglottoplasty surgery for severe obstruction or failure to thrive.
diseases.home_care: Feed infant in an upright position; keep upright for 30 minutes after feeds; monitor weight gain closely. Never ignore cyanosis.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Famotidine or Omeprazole (generic acid-suppressing active ingredients to control reflux and reduce laryngeal inflammation).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Seek immediate medical attention if the baby stops breathing for more than 10 seconds, develops blue lips, has severe chest pulling (retractions), or is unable to gain weight.
diseases.prevention: No known prevention; it is a congenital developmental delay.
diseases.vaccination_info: Routine pediatric immunizations are fully recommended.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent; resolves spontaneously without surgery in over 90% of infants by 12 to 18 weeks/months as larynx matures.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Spontaneous resolution by 12 to 18 months.
diseases.complications: Severe hypoxemia, cor pulmonale, sleep apnea, pulmonary hypertension (in extreme untreated airway blockage).

diseases.name: Osteomyelitis
diseases.affected_age_group: 3 to 12 Years (Peak pediatric age)
diseases.causes: Bacterial colonization, predominantly Staphylococcus aureus (including MRSA) or Kingella kingae (in toddlers), often via bloodstream seeding.
diseases.pathophysiology: Bacteria enter the bloodstream and lodge in the metaphysis of long bones, where slow-flowing blood in sinusoidal veins allows colonization. Localized replication leads to bone necrosis, microabscesses, and periosteal stripping, causing severe localized pain and bone resorption.
diseases.symptoms_early: Sudden onset of severe, localized bone pain, tenderness to touch, and swelling over a limb bone, causing the child to refuse weight-bearing.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Erythema and warmth over the infected site; high fever with chills; extreme irritability and refusal to move the affected limb.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Subperiosteal abscess formation; loss of range of motion of adjacent joints; signs of systemic inflammatory response (sepsis).
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Blood cultures; elevated inflammatory markers (CRP and ESR); early MRI demonstrating bone marrow edema; X-ray demonstrating periosteal reaction.
diseases.treatment_plan: Urgent hospitalization for intravenous antibiotics (targeting S. aureus). Transition to oral antibiotics upon clinical improvement for a total course of 3 to 6 weeks. Surgical drainage if an abscess is present.
diseases.home_care: Strict immobilization of the limb; assist child with repositioning; maintain high nutrition; adhere strictly to the full antibiotic course.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Cefazolin or Vancomycin (generic intravenous antibiotics to eradicate bone pathogens); Ibuprofen (generic NSAID for pain and inflammation control).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Seek immediate emergency pediatric evaluation if a child experiences sudden localized bone pain, swelling, high fever, or refuses to move a limb.
diseases.prevention: Keep all skin scrapes and wounds clean and covered; treat minor skin infections promptly.
diseases.vaccination_info: No immunization exists for osteomyelitis pathogens.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent if treated early; over 95% of children recover fully with no permanent bone deformity or growth arrest.
diseases.recovery_timeline: 4 to 6 weeks total treatment duration.
diseases.complications: Chronic osteomyelitis, pathological fracture, growth plate destruction leading to limb discrepancy, septic arthritis.

diseases.name: Pediatric Scoliosis
diseases.affected_age_group: 10 to 18 Years (Typically detected during growth spurt)
diseases.causes: Idiopathic (multifactorial genetic, structural, and neuromuscular components).
diseases.pathophysiology: During rapid pubertal growth, asymmetric spinal growth results in three-dimensional rotation of the vertebral column. This causes lateral deviation of the spine, vertebral rotation, rib cage distortion (rib hump), and pelvic tilting.
diseases.symptoms_early: Asymmetrical shoulder heights, uneven waistline, or one hip appearing higher than the other. Usually completely painless in early stages.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Visible rib hump or unilateral flank prominence when bending forward (positive Adams forward bend test); mild back fatigue.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Curvatures exceeding 45-50 degrees, causing restrictive lung symptoms (shortness of breath) due to chest distortion; chronic back pain.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Adams forward bend test with a scoliometer showing rotation >5 degrees; standing full-length spinal X-rays to calculate Cobb angle.
diseases.treatment_plan: Observation for curves <20 degrees; spinal bracing (Rigo-Cheneau or Boston brace) for curves 25-40 degrees in growing children; posterior spinal fusion surgery for curves >45-50 degrees.
diseases.home_care: Encourage core strengthening exercises (Schroth method). Ensure compliance with spinal brace wearing schedules (16-23 hours/day) and monitor skin integrity.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Acetaminophen or Ibuprofen (generic analgesics for occasional muscle soreness, though rarely needed for typical idiopathic curves).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Consult a pediatrician for spinal screening if you notice asymmetrical shoulders, uneven waist, or rib prominence when bending.
diseases.prevention: No known prevention since it is idiopathic/developmental.
diseases.vaccination_info: Standard immunizations are recommended.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent with early detection and bracing; most children lead fully active lives with minimal restrictions.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Surgical recovery is 4-6 weeks; bracing continues until skeletal maturity.
diseases.complications: Progression of curve, pulmonary restriction, cosmetic distress, chronic back pain in adulthood.

diseases.name: Congenital Diaphragmatic Hernia
diseases.affected_age_group: Newborns (Presents immediately at birth)
diseases.causes: Developmental failure of the diaphragmatic membranes in utero (multifactorial genetic and environmental factors).
diseases.pathophysiology: Incomplete closure of the pleuroperitoneal membranes during embryonic development allows abdominal viscera to herniate into the chest cavity. This compresses the developing fetal lungs, causing bilateral pulmonary hypoplasia (reduced alveoli and airways) and muscularization of pulmonary arteries, leading to persistent pulmonary hypertension (PPHN) at birth.
diseases.symptoms_early: Severe respiratory distress immediately after birth, characterized by tachypnea, chest retractions, cyanosis, and a classic sunken (scaphoid) abdomen.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Absence of breath sounds on the affected side; displacement of heart sounds to the opposite side; bowel sounds heard in the thoracic cavity.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Severe hypoxemia, hypercapnia, metabolic acidosis, and profound pulmonary hypertension leading to right-to-left shunting and cardiac failure.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Prenatal fetal ultrasound or MRI; chest X-ray immediately after birth demonstrating loop-like bowel shadows in the hemithorax and mediastinal shift.
diseases.treatment_plan: Immediate endotracheal intubation at birth (no bag-valve mask). Support with gentle ventilation, inhaled nitric oxide (iNO), or ECMO. Surgical repair of the diaphragm once hemodynamics stabilize.
diseases.home_care: Home care after hospital discharge involves feeding therapy (often G-tube), monitoring respiratory rates, oxygen saturation, and regular physical therapy.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Inhaled Nitric Oxide (iNO - generic pulmonary vasodilator active element); Fentanyl or Vecuronium (generic sedatives/paralytics to prevent lung stress during ventilation).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: This is diagnosed prenatally or at birth. Seek immediate emergency pediatric care if a post-repair infant at home shows difficulty feeding, rapid breathing, blue lips, or vomiting.
diseases.prevention: No known prevention; prenatal screening can help coordinate delivery at a tertiary neonatal center.
diseases.vaccination_info: Standard immunizations are recommended, with RSV prophylaxis and annual influenza vaccine given due to lung sensitivity.
diseases.prognosis: Survival rate is ~70-80% in specialized centers, but survivors have long-term respiratory, gastrointestinal (GERD), and developmental challenges.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Hospital stay is typically 3 to 8 weeks; long-term follow-up spans throughout childhood.
diseases.complications: Pulmonary hypoplasia, persistent pulmonary hypertension, severe gastroesophageal reflux, hearing loss (associated with ECMO), scoliosis.
related_diseases: Persistent Pulmonary Hypertension of the Newborn (PPHN)

diseases.name: Shigellosis
diseases.affected_age_group: 1 to 5 Years (Highly common in childcare settings)
diseases.causes: Infection with Shigella species (predominantly Shigella sonnei in developed nations), transmitted via the fecal-oral route.
diseases.pathophysiology: Shigella bacteria invade and replicate within mucosal epithelial cells of the colon, producing Shigella enterotoxins. This triggers an intense inflammatory response, mucosal cell death, microabscesses, and mucosal sloughing, leading to stools containing blood, mucus, and inflammatory cells.
diseases.symptoms_early: Abrupt onset of high fever (39°C to 40°C), watery diarrhea, severe colicky abdominal pain, and anorexia.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Stools containing blood, mucus, and pus; painful straining to defecate (tenesmus); multiple small-volume stools.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Dehydration, electrolyte disturbances, toxic megacolon, high fever triggering febrile seizures, and Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome (HUS).
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Stool culture and PCR assay confirming Shigella DNA; stool microscopy demonstrating numerous fecal leukocytes.
diseases.treatment_plan: Maintain strict hydration with oral rehydration solutions. Antibiotic therapy is indicated for moderate-to-severe cases or in daycare settings to shorten the shedding window. Avoid antimotility drugs (like loperamide) as they prolong infection.
diseases.home_care: Ensure the child drinks small amounts of electrolyte solution frequently; practice strict handwashing after every diaper change or restroom visit; keep the child home from daycare/school until cleared.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Oral Rehydration Solution (ORS - generic hydration fluids); Azithromycin or Ceftriaxone (generic antibiotics used for moderate/severe Shigellosis).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Seek prompt medical care if the child has bloody diarrhea, high fever, signs of dehydration (no tears, dry mouth, no wet diapers for 6+ hours), or experiences a seizure.
diseases.prevention: Strict hand hygiene, proper diaper disposal, and sanitary food handling. Keep infected children isolated from group settings.
diseases.vaccination_info: No vaccine is currently available.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent with prompt hydration; most children recover fully within 5 to 7 days without long-term issues.
diseases.recovery_timeline: 5 to 7 days.
diseases.complications: Severe dehydration, febrile seizures, toxic megacolon, Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome (HUS), reactive arthritis.

diseases.name: Pinworm Infection
diseases.affected_age_group: 5 to 10 Years (School-aged children)
diseases.causes: Ingestion or inhalation of Enterobius vermicularis eggs, commonly via contaminated fingers, clothing, bedding, or toys.
diseases.pathophysiology: Ingested eggs hatch in the small intestine, and mature worms migrate to the cecum. At night, female worms migrate to the perianal area and lay eggs, triggering a localized hypersensitivity reaction and intense perianal itching.
diseases.symptoms_early: Intense perianal or vaginal itching, typically worsening at night when the child is sleeping, leading to restlessness.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Disturbed sleep, irritability, and scratching of the bottom. Small, thread-like white worms (5-13 mm) may be seen in the perianal area or stool.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Excoriation (skin breaks) of the perianal skin from scratching, leading to secondary bacterial skin infections; mild weight loss.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: The "tape test" (applying transparent adhesive tape to the perianal skin in the morning before bathing to capture eggs for microscopic viewing).
diseases.treatment_plan: Administer oral anthelmintic medication to the child AND all household members simultaneously. A second dose MUST be given 2 weeks later to kill newly hatched worms. Wash all clothes, bedding, and toys in hot water.
diseases.home_care: Keep fingernails cut short to prevent trapping eggs under nails; encourage morning showers to wash away eggs laid overnight; clean toilets and vacuum carpets regularly.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Pyrantel pamoate (generic over-the-counter anthelmintic active element); Mebendazole or Albendazole (generic prescription anthelmintic active elements).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Consult a pediatrician if the child has persistent anal itching, sleep disruption, or if you suspect pinworms, to confirm diagnosis and obtain proper dosing.
diseases.prevention: Regular hand hygiene, keeping nails short, avoiding nail-biting, and prompt treatment of the entire family when an infection occurs.
diseases.vaccination_info: No immunization exists.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent; easily cured with anthelmintic treatment and thorough environmental hygiene.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Clearance is immediate after the second dose; environmental cleaning resolves reinfection risks.
diseases.complications: Secondary bacterial perianal skin infection (impetigo), vulvovaginitis (in young girls due to worm migration).

diseases.name: Mastoiditis
diseases.affected_age_group: 6 Months to 3 Years (Most common under 2 years)
diseases.causes: Untreated or inadequately treated acute otitis media (AOM), most commonly caused by Streptococcus pneumoniae.
diseases.pathophysiology: Infection spreads from the middle ear cavity into the mastoid air cells. Hyperemia and swelling block the mastoid antrum, trapping purulent fluid under pressure, leading to the resorption of bony septa (coalescence) and periosteal inflammation.
diseases.symptoms_early: Deep ear pain, fever, irritability, and purulent ear discharge (otorrhea) following a recent middle ear infection.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Erythema, swelling, and tenderness over the mastoid bone behind the ear; downward and outward displacement of the pinna (ear lobe).
diseases.symptoms_severe: High fever, severe lethargy, fluctuant mastoid swelling (subperiosteal abscess), facial nerve palsy, or signs of meningitis.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Clinical presentation of postauricular swelling and pinna displacement; CT scan of the temporal bone demonstrating loss of mastoid bony septa.
diseases.treatment_plan: Hospital admission for intravenous antibiotics. Performance of a myringotomy and tympanostomy tube insertion for drainage. Cortical mastoidectomy if a subperiosteal abscess develops.
diseases.home_care: Strict compliance with all post-discharge antibiotics. Keep the ear dry during baths. Monitor the ear incision or ear tube for drainage.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Ceftriaxone or Ampicillin-sulbactam (generic intravenous antibiotics to target mastoid pathogens); Acetaminophen (generic analgesic for severe pain control).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: This is a pediatric emergency. Seek immediate care if a child with an ear infection develops swelling, redness, or pain behind the ear, or if the ear lobe appears pushed forward.
diseases.prevention: Prompt and complete treatment of acute middle ear infections (otitis media) with appropriate antibiotics.
diseases.vaccination_info: Pneumococcal conjugate vaccine (PCV13/20) and annual influenza vaccines help reduce the incidence of preceding otitis media.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent with early intravenous antibiotics and drainage; most children recover fully with no permanent hearing loss or skull damage.
diseases.recovery_timeline: 2 to 3 weeks total recovery and antibiotic duration.
diseases.complications: Subperiosteal abscess, facial nerve palsy, labyrinthitis (inner ear infection), meningitis, epidural abscess, sigmoid sinus thrombosis.

diseases.name: Pediatric Dehydration
diseases.affected_age_group: Infants and Young Children (High risk)
diseases.causes: Gastroenteritis (Rotavirus, Norovirus), severe diarrhea, excessive vomiting, high environmental heat, and poor fluid intake.
diseases.pathophysiology: Increased output of water and electrolytes exceeds intake, leading to depletion of extracellular fluid. Infants are highly susceptible due to a larger surface-area-to-mass ratio, higher metabolic rate, and limited renal concentrating capacity, leading to rapid hypovolemia.
diseases.symptoms_early: Mild dry mouth, slightly decreased tear production, and decreased wet diapers (mild oliguria).
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Sunken fontanelle, dry mucous membranes, sunken eyes, loss of skin turgor (delayed skin pinch/tenting), and extreme irritability.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Cool, mottled extremities, prolonged capillary refill time (> 3 seconds), rapid thready pulse, deep tachypnea (hyperpnea), lethargy, and hypotensive shock.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Clinical evaluation of skin turgor, mucous membranes, fontanelle, and capillary refill. Electrolyte panel and urine specific gravity if severe.
diseases.treatment_plan: Oral rehydration therapy (ORT) for mild-to-moderate dehydration using standardized solutions. Intravenous bolus of isotonic fluids (e.g., Normal Saline or Ringer's Lactate) for severe hypovolemia.
diseases.home_care: Administer commercial oral rehydration salts (ORS) in small, frequent amounts. Avoid high-sugar drinks like juice or sodas which worsen diarrhea.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Oral Rehydration Salts (ORS containing sodium, potassium, chloride, glucose); Isotonic saline intravenous fluids.
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Immediate emergency evaluation is required if the child is lethargic, has cold hands/feet, cannot keep liquids down, or has no wet diaper for over 8 hours.
diseases.prevention: Ensure adequate fluid intake during illness, prompt management of vomiting/diarrhea, and vaccination against Rotavirus.
diseases.vaccination_info: Rotavirus vaccine significantly reduces severe dehydration episodes from viral gastroenteritis.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent with early recognition and appropriate fluid replacement. Untreated severe dehydration can lead to fatal hypovolemic shock.
diseases.recovery_timeline: 24 to 48 hours with correct rehydration therapy.
diseases.complications: Hypovolemic shock, electrolyte imbalance (hypernatremia/hyponatremia), acute kidney injury, and seizures.

diseases.name: Greenstick Fracture
diseases.affected_age_group: Toddlers and Children (typically under 10 years)
diseases.causes: Falls onto an outstretched hand (FOOSH), direct impact, or twisting force on soft, flexible pediatric bones.
diseases.pathophysiology: Because pediatric bones are more porous and contain more collagen than adult bones, they are highly flexible. An applied bending force causes the bone to bend and break on the convex side (outer side) while the concave side (inner side) remains bent but intact, similar to breaking a green branch of a tree.
diseases.symptoms_early: Immediate localized pain following trauma, reluctance to use the affected extremity.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Localized swelling, tenderness over the bone shaft, and visible bruising.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Deformity (angulation) of the limb, intense pain upon movement, and numbness or tingling in fingers or toes.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Plain radiographs (X-rays) in anteroposterior (AP) and lateral views showing cortical disruption on one side only.
diseases.treatment_plan: Immobilization in a cast or splint. If angulation is significant, closed reduction under sedation may be required to realign the bone before casting.
diseases.home_care: Keep the cast dry. Elevate the limb above heart level to reduce swelling. Monitor finger/toe temperature and sensation.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Acetaminophen or Ibuprofen for pain relief.
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Seek medical care immediately after any fall followed by severe pain or swelling. Go to the ER if the limb looks crooked or fingers/toes are cold or blue.
diseases.prevention: Ensure child safety protocols on playgrounds, use of protective wrist guards and pads during sports, and adequate calcium intake.
diseases.vaccination_info: Not applicable.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent. Children's bones have high remodeling capacity, and these fractures heal completely without residual disability.
diseases.recovery_timeline: 4 to 6 weeks in a cast.

diseases.name: Iron Deficiency Anemia
diseases.affected_age_group: Infants (9-24 months) and Adolescents (particularly females)
diseases.causes: Inadequate dietary iron intake, delayed introduction of iron-rich foods, excessive cow's milk intake in toddlers (>24 oz/day), and blood loss.
diseases.pathophysiology: Depletion of body iron stores leads to insufficient iron for hemoglobin synthesis. Red blood cells become microcytic (small) and hypochromic (pale), reducing the oxygen-carrying capacity of the blood and leading to tissue hypoxia.
diseases.symptoms_early: Mild pallor, particularly of the conjunctiva, palms, and nail beds; subtle irritability; and decreased activity level.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Fatigue, weakness, poor feeding, slow growth, tachypnea, and developmental delay or behavioral issues.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Pica (craving non-food items like dirt or ice), splenomegaly, cardiomegaly, systolic murmur, and heart failure in extreme cases.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Complete Blood Count (CBC) showing low hemoglobin/hematocrit and low MCV. Confirmed by low ferritin and low iron levels with high TIBC.
diseases.treatment_plan: Oral iron supplementation (ferrous sulfate). Limit cow's milk intake and increase dietary iron (red meat, iron-fortified cereals, leafy greens) along with Vitamin C to enhance absorption.
diseases.home_care: Administer iron supplements between meals with a source of Vitamin C (like orange juice). Expect dark green or black stools, which are normal.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Ferrous Sulfate (oral iron drops or syrup).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Consult if your child seems unusually tired, pale, has poor appetite, or displays strange cravings.
diseases.prevention: Exclusive breastfeeding or iron-fortified formula for the first 6 months. Introduce iron-rich complementary foods at 6 months. Avoid cow's milk before 12 months.
diseases.vaccination_info: Not applicable.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent. Prompt resolution with iron supplementation, with hematologic parameters normalizing within 1-2 months.
diseases.recovery_timeline: 3 to 6 months of iron therapy to replenish body stores.
diseases.complications: Cognitive and developmental delays, behavioral issues, motor dysfunction, and high-output heart failure.

diseases.name: Pediatric Migraine
diseases.affected_age_group: School-age Children and Adolescents
diseases.causes: Genetic predisposition combined with triggers (stress, lack of sleep, dehydration, skipped meals, bright lights, certain foods).
diseases.pathophysiology: Spreading cortical depression triggers activation of the trigeminovascular system, causing release of vasoactive neuropeptides. This leads to neurogenic inflammation of dural blood vessels and transmission of pain signals to the brain stem and cortex.
diseases.symptoms_early: Behavioral changes, irritability, yawning, fatigue, or sensitivity to light/sound (prodrome).
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Bilateral (often frontotemporal) throbbing headache, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, photo- and phonophobia.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Inability to function, severe vomiting leading to dehydration, visual aura (scintillating scotoma, flashing lights), and transient neurological deficits.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Diagnosis is clinical, based on headache history and normal neurological examination. MRI or CT only if atypical features or red flags are present.
diseases.treatment_plan: Acute relief using NSAIDs or triptans. Prophylactic therapy (topiramate, beta-blockers) if headaches are frequent or disabling. Lifestyle modifications to manage triggers.
diseases.home_care: Have the child rest in a quiet, dark room at the onset of headache. Apply a cool compress to the forehead and encourage hydration.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Ibuprofen or Acetaminophen (for acute pain); Sumatriptan (for severe acute attacks in adolescents).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Seek evaluation for any new, severe headache, headaches that wake the child from sleep, or headaches associated with neurological symptoms like weakness or vision loss.
diseases.prevention: Maintain regular schedules for sleep, meals, and hydration. Encourage stress-management techniques.
diseases.vaccination_info: Not applicable.
diseases.prognosis: Good. Most children experience reduction in frequency and severity with appropriate trigger management and treatment.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Migraine episodes typically last 2 to 72 hours.
diseases.complications: Status migraine (intractable migraine >72 hours), medication overuse headache, and school absenteeism.

diseases.name: Otitis Externa (Swimmer's Ear)
diseases.classification: Acute Bacterial Infection of the External Auditory Canal
diseases.affected_age_group: Children and Teens (particularly active swimmers)
diseases.causes: Pseudomonas aeruginosa or Staphylococcus aureus infection, favored by moisture retention in the ear canal or minor trauma (cotton swabs).
diseases.pathophysiology: Retention of water in the ear canal leads to maceration of the skin and breakdown of the protective cerumen barrier. This creates an alkaline environment that favors bacterial proliferation, leading to acute inflammation, edema, and exquisite pain.
diseases.symptoms_early: Mild itching inside the ear canal, slight redness of the outer ear, and mild discomfort.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Severe ear pain, exacerbated by pulling the pinna or pushing the tragus; swelling of the ear canal; and yellowish discharge.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Complete occlusion of the ear canal by swelling, fever, localized lymphadenopathy, and spreading cellulitis of the face or neck.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Otoscopic examination showing erythema, edema, and purulent debris in the external auditory canal. Exquisite pain on movement of the pinna.
diseases.treatment_plan: Thorough cleaning of the canal if obstructed. Topical antibiotic ear drops (often combined with a corticosteroid to reduce swelling). Keep the ear dry.
diseases.home_care: Apply warm compresses for pain relief. Ensure no water enters the ear canal during bathing (use earplugs or a cotton ball coated in petroleum jelly).
diseases.medicinal_elements: Ciprofloxacin-Dexamethasone ear drops (active bacterial and anti-inflammatory ingredients).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Consult if there is severe ear pain, spreading redness on the outer ear, high fever, or if ear drops cannot be instilled due to swelling.
diseases.prevention: Dry ears thoroughly after swimming using a towel or hair dryer on a low setting. Use drying drops (isopropyl alcohol/white vinegar) after swimming.
diseases.vaccination_info: Not applicable.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent. Swift resolution within 3 to 5 days of initiating topical antibiotic drops.
diseases.recovery_timeline: 7 to 10 days for complete resolution.
diseases.complications: Malignant otitis externa (rare in children), spreading cellulitis, and temporary hearing loss due to canal occlusion.
diseases.overview: Acute Bacterial Infection of the External Auditory Canal

diseases.name: Acute Viral Conjunctivitis (Pink Eye)
diseases.affected_age_group: Preschool and School-age Children
diseases.causes: Adenovirus (highly contagious). Often associated with common cold or upper respiratory symptoms.
diseases.pathophysiology: Direct inoculation of the conjunctiva by viral particles leads to cellular infection, causing conjunctival hyperemia (dilation of blood vessels) and chemosis (swelling), resulting in the classic pink or red appearance.
diseases.symptoms_early: Mild foreign body sensation or grittiness in one eye, mild redness, and watery discharge.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Bilateral eye involvement, burning sensation, swollen eyelids, and significant watery or serous discharge.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Photophobia, severe eyelid swelling, pseudomembrane formation over the conjunctiva, and corneal involvement (keratitis) causing blurred vision.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Clinical evaluation. Conjunctival swab or PCR only in severe or atypical cases.
diseases.treatment_plan: Cold compresses and artificial tears for comfort. Viral conjunctivitis is self-limiting and does not respond to antibiotic drops. Practice strict hygiene to prevent transmission.
diseases.home_care: Use a clean, damp cloth to gently wipe away crusting. Wash hands frequently. Keep the child home from school until eye discharge is gone.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Lubricating eye drops (artificial tears); Cold compresses.
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Consult if the child has severe eye pain, changes in vision, extreme sensitivity to light, or if the discharge becomes thick and green/yellow.
diseases.prevention: Strict hand hygiene, avoid touching or rubbing the eyes, and wash towels and pillowcases frequently.
diseases.vaccination_info: Not applicable.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent. Most cases resolve spontaneously without any long-term visual impairment.
diseases.recovery_timeline: 7 to 14 days.
diseases.complications: Epidemic keratoconjunctivitis (corneal infiltrates), secondary bacterial infection, and localized transmission.

diseases.name: Prematurity & Low Birth Weight
diseases.affected_age_group: Newborns born before 37 weeks of gestation
diseases.causes: Maternal preeclampsia, multiple gestations, intrauterine infection, cervical insufficiency, and preterm labor.
diseases.pathophysiology: Birth before full gestational maturation results in structural and functional immaturity of all organ systems. Crucially, surfactant deficiency causes alveoli collapse, and an immature brain stem leads to periodic apnea.
diseases.symptoms_early: Gestational age <37 weeks, birth weight <2500g, thin transparent skin, and absence of breast buds or ear cartilage.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Tachypnea, retractions, grunting, temperature instability, and poor sucking reflex requiring gavage feeding.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Severe respiratory failure, bradycardia, recurrent apnea episodes, abdominal distended loops (NEC warning), and neonatal shock.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Gestational age assessment (Ballard score), continuous cardiorespiratory monitoring, blood gases, and head ultrasound to screen for IVH.
diseases.treatment_plan: Thermal regulation in an incubator. Respiratory support (CPAP or mechanical ventilation with exogenous surfactant). Total parenteral nutrition (TPN) leading to enteral feeding.
diseases.home_care: Kangaroo care (skin-to-skin contact). Maintain strict sanitization. Monitor feeding volume and breathing patterns continuously after discharge.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Exogenous Surfactant (for lung expansion); Caffeine Citrate (for apnea of prematurity).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: NICU care is required. Post-discharge, seek immediate care for fever, feeding refusal, vomiting, lethargy, or rapid breathing.
diseases.prevention: Regular prenatal care, management of maternal chronic conditions, and avoiding smoking and alcohol during pregnancy.
diseases.vaccination_info: Preterm infants should receive standard childhood vaccinations according to chronological age, not corrected gestational age.
diseases.prognosis: Variable. Very high survival rates for infants born after 28 weeks. Extremely premature infants face higher risk of long-term developmental challenges.
diseases.recovery_timeline: NICU stays often last until the infant reaches their original due date.
diseases.complications: Respiratory Distress Syndrome (RDS), Intraventricular Hemorrhage (IVH), Necrotizing Enterocolitis (NEC), and Retinopathy of Prematurity (ROP).

diseases.name: Cow's Milk Protein Allergy (CMPA)
diseases.classification: IgE or Non-IgE Mediated Pediatric Food Hypersensitivity
diseases.affected_age_group: Infants (usually presenting in the first 6 months of life)
diseases.causes: Immune system hypersensitivity to casein or whey proteins found in cow's milk. Can occur in breastfed or formula-fed infants.
diseases.pathophysiology: The infant's immune system identifies cow's milk proteins as foreign antigens. In IgE-mediated CMPA, this causes mast cell degranulation and histamine release. In non-IgE mediated CMPA, T-cells trigger local inflammation in the intestinal mucosa, leading to malabsorption and blood loss.
diseases.symptoms_early: Spitting up, mild colic, fussiness after feeding, and occasional loose stools.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Eczema, chronic diarrhea, stools containing mucus or visible blood, vomiting, and poor weight gain (failure to thrive).
diseases.symptoms_severe: Anaphylaxis (swelling, hives, wheezing, hypotension) following milk exposure, severe enteropathy, and severe anemia due to intestinal blood loss.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Elimination diet (removing cow's milk from maternal diet or switching to hydrolyzed formula) followed by challenge. Skin prick tests or IgE levels if IgE-mediated.
diseases.treatment_plan: Strict elimination of cow's milk proteins. For formula-fed infants, switch to extensively hydrolyzed or amino acid-based formula. Breastfeeding mothers must eliminate dairy from their diet.
diseases.home_care: Read all food labels carefully to avoid hidden dairy. Reintroduce dairy only under medical supervision after a specified period.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Extensively Hydrolyzed Formula (specialized dairy-free formula); Epinephrine auto-injector (if history of anaphylaxis).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Consult if the infant has blood in their stool, persistent vomiting, eczema, or poor growth. Call emergency services for breathing difficulty or facial swelling.
diseases.prevention: Exclusive breastfeeding for the first 4-6 months. No other preventive methods exist.
diseases.vaccination_info: Not applicable.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent. Most children outgrow CMPA by age 3 to 5 years.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Symptoms improve within 2 to 4 weeks of dairy elimination.
diseases.complications: Failure to thrive, severe anemia, anaphylaxis, and increased risk of other food allergies.
diseases.overview: IgE or Non-IgE Mediated Pediatric Food Hypersensitivity

diseases.name: Acute Bronchitis
diseases.affected_age_group: Preschool and School-age Children
