diseases.causes: Common cold viruses (Rhinovirus, Adenovirus, Influenza) or occasionally atypical bacteria (Mycoplasma pneumoniae).
diseases.pathophysiology: Infection causes acute inflammation of the mucous membranes of the large bronchial tree. Hyperemia and edema of the bronchial lining, combined with increased mucus production, leads to airway narrowing, bronchospasm, and a persistent cough.
diseases.symptoms_early: Dry, hacking cough following a cold, mild low-grade fever, sore throat, and nasal congestion.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Cough becomes productive with clear or purulent sputum, chest soreness with coughing, and mild wheezing.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Dyspnea, sustained high fever, chest wall retractions, and cyanosis (suggesting progression to pneumonia).
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Clinical diagnosis based on cough history and auscultation (revealing rhonchi or coarse wheezing that often clears with coughing).
diseases.treatment_plan: Supportive care with hydration, humidification, and cough relief. Antibiotics are not indicated for viral cases. Bronchodilators if wheezing is present.
diseases.home_care: Ensure the child drinks plenty of fluids. Use a cool-mist humidifier. Avoid exposure to secondhand smoke or other airway irritants.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Albuterol (bronchodilator active ingredient if bronchospasm is present); Acetaminophen or Ibuprofen (for fever/comfort).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Seek evaluation if the cough lasts more than 2-3 weeks, is accompanied by high fever, rapid breathing, or if the child has trouble catching their breath.
diseases.prevention: Frequent handwashing, avoid contact with sick individuals, and ensure annual influenza vaccination.
diseases.vaccination_info: Annual Influenza vaccination reduces the risk of viral bronchitis episodes.
diseases.prognosis: Excellent. Self-limiting condition with complete recovery and no permanent lung damage.
diseases.recovery_timeline: 7 to 14 days, though cough may linger for up to 3 weeks.
diseases.complications: Pneumonia, secondary bacterial infection, and bronchospasm exacerbation in children with asthma.

diseases.name: Fever of Unknown Origin (FUO)
diseases.affected_age_group: Infants and Children of all ages
diseases.causes: Atypical infections (Bartonella, tuberculosis, osteomyelitis), autoimmune diseases (Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis, Lupus), or childhood malignancies.
diseases.pathophysiology: Endogenous pyrogens (IL-1, TNF) released by immune cells act on the hypothalamus, raising the temperature set-point. In FUO, the persistent activation of this pathway remains unexplained after standard initial investigations.
diseases.symptoms_early: Persistent fever (>38.3°C) lasting for 8 or more days without a clear localizing cause on initial physical exam.
diseases.symptoms_progressive: Intermittent, remittent, or continuous fever spikes; fatigue; decreased appetite; and weight loss.
diseases.symptoms_severe: Significant weight loss, night sweats, bone pain, lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, and cytopenias.
diseases.diagnosis_methods: Detailed history and physical exam. Laboratory tests include CBC, inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP), blood cultures, TB test, and imaging (chest X-ray, abdominal ultrasound).
diseases.treatment_plan: Address underlying cause once identified. Antipyretics for comfort. Empiric antibiotics or anti-inflammatory therapies are avoided until a diagnosis is established, unless septic.
diseases.home_care: Ensure adequate hydration. Dress the child in light clothing. Use antipyretics strictly according to weight-based dosing guidelines. Keep a detailed fever diary.
diseases.medicinal_elements: Acetaminophen or Ibuprofen (antipyretics for fever control and comfort).
diseases.when_to_see_doctor: Seek evaluation for any fever lasting more than 5-7 days. Seek immediate emergency care if the child is lethargic, has a stiff neck, a new rash, or difficulty breathing.
diseases.prevention: Ensure timely pediatric vaccinations. Practice standard hygiene and vector precautions (e.g., tick/insect bite prevention).
diseases.vaccination_info: Up-to-date vaccinations prevent many common causes of prolonged febrile illness.
diseases.prognosis: Generally favorable. Most cases in children are due to unusual presentations of common infections and resolve fully once diagnosed and treated.
diseases.recovery_timeline: Highly variable depending on the underlying etiology.
diseases.complications: Dehydration, progressive organ dysfunction, and delays in treatment of serious underlying conditions.
